technique: To test divisibility of a power-sum a^b+b^a by a+b, reduce modulo a+b by replacing one base with the negative of the other.
how_used: Mod a+b, a ≡ -b, so a^b+b^a ≡ (-b)^b + b^a; with b odd this is -b^b + b^a = b^b(b^{a-b}-1), and since gcd(b,a+b)=1 the condition collapses to the single power congruence b^{a-b} ≡ 1 (mod a+b), i.e. b^d ≡ 1 (mod d+2b) with d=a-b.

technique: Eliminate an unknown base from a power congruence by scaling so the base merges with a modulus relation into a constant.
how_used: From b^d ≡ 1 (mod d+2b), multiply by 2^d and use 2b ≡ -d (mod d+2b): (2b)^d = 2^d b^d ≡ 2^d while (2b)^d ≡ (-d)^d = -d^d (d odd), so the congruence becomes d+2b | d^d + 2^d, with the unknown b now only inside the modulus.

technique: Force a divisibility n | N by choosing the free parameter so that n equals a known nontrivial factor of N.
how_used: d+2 divides d^d+2^d (since d ≡ -2 mod d+2 gives d^d ≡ -2^d), so set the modulus d+2b = (d^d+2^d)/(d+2), i.e. b = ((d^d+2^d)/(d+2) - d)/2; taking d ≡ 1 (mod 4), d>1 makes this an integer with correct parity (b odd, a even) and gcd(a,b)=gcd(d,b)=1, yielding infinitely many coprime pairs.

technique: Peel one unit fraction 1+1/m off a target of form 1+a/n by inserting a single intermediate numerator, choosing that intermediate by the parity of n so the remaining factor is a smaller copy of the same problem and m is a positive integer.
how_used: Inducting on k, to realize 1+(2^j-1)/n one writes it as a product of two telescoping factors. For n=2t-1 (odd): 1+(2^j-1)/(2t-1) = (1+(2^{j-1}-1)/t)(1+1/(2t-1)), setting m_j=2t-1. For n=2t (even): 1+(2^j-1)/(2t) = (1+1/(2t+2^j-2))(1+(2^{j-1}-1)/t), setting m_j=2t+2^j-2. Either way the numerator parameter drops from 2^j-1 to 2^{j-1}-1, so the induction hypothesis on k=j-1 supplies the other j-1 factors.

technique: Split 2^k-1 into exactly k binary pieces by reading off the bit-positions of two complementary residues mod 2^k whose digit-sets partition {0,...,k-1}, then telescope a quotient through the partial sums of those bits.
how_used: Write n-1 ≡ sum 2^{a_p} and -n ≡ sum 2^{b_q} (mod 2^k); since -1 ≡ 2^0+...+2^{k-1}, the sets {a_p} and {b_q} partition {0,...,k-1} with r+s=k. Using partial sums S_p, T_q one telescopes 1+(2^k-1)/n into a product of k factors 1+2^{a_p}/(n+S_{p+1}+T_s) and 1+2^{b_q}/(n+T_{q-1}); each denominator is divisible by the corresponding 2^{a_p} or 2^{b_q} because n+T_s ≡ 0 (mod 2^k), so every m_i is an integer.

technique: Recognize a quartic as a difference of squares that factors into the SAME quadratic evaluated at two consecutive arguments, turning a largest-prime question into one about a single quadratic's value-sequence.
how_used: Write n^4+n^2+1=(n^2+1)^2-n^2=(n^2-n+1)(n^2+n+1). With f(x)=x^2+x+1 (the cyclotomic Phi_3) and noting n^2-n+1=f(n-1), this is the identity f(n^2)=f(n)f(n-1). Letting q_n be the largest prime divisor of f(n), the largest prime of n^4+n^2+1 is p_n=q_{n^2}=max{q_n,q_{n-1}}, reducing the whole problem to the behavior of the single sequence (q_n).

technique: Use a self-referential index identity that equates a far-ahead term of a sequence to a nearby term to forbid an infinite strictly-increasing run.
how_used: The identity q_{(k+1)^2}=max{q_k,q_{k+1}}=q_{k+1} forces the very-far-ahead term at index (k+1)^2 to equal the term at index k+1; if (q_n) were strictly increasing from some k onward this would contradict (k+1)^2>k+1. Since it also cannot strictly decrease forever among positive integers, the sequence has infinitely many local maxima n, and at each such n the reduction gives p_n=q_n=p_{n+1}, the desired infinitely many n.

technique: Strip an even factor from a gcd of two near-equal quadratics via the Euclidean step to prove successive value-prime-divisors are distinct.
how_used: gcd(n^2+n+1, n^2-n+1)=gcd(2n, n^2-n+1)=gcd(n, n^2-n+1)=1 (using that n^2-n+1 is odd). Hence f(n) and f(n-1) are coprime, so q_n != q_{n-1}; this strictness is what makes the increasing/decreasing dichotomy yield genuine strict local maxima.

technique: Read an impartial game's losing positions off its recursion as a non-adjacency class: a position from which the mover loses can never move to another such position, so any two of them fail the move's legality condition.
how_used: Good (Banana wins) means the player to move from n loses, i.e. n is a P-position; a legal move replaces m by a coprime m' with k<=m'<m. Hence no legal move connects two good numbers, so any two good numbers are NOT coprime and must share a prime factor; since k itself is good, every good number shares a prime factor with k.

technique: To produce a number with the same allowed-prime signature but no forbidden (large) prime factors, take the product of all allowed primes times the least power of one allowed prime that reaches the threshold, and bound it below the original via a comparison of products of distinct prime factors.
how_used: Given good b with a big prime factor q>k, let a = product of b's small prime factors and p a small prime factor of b; choose least n>=0 with x = p^n*a >= k. Then x has exactly b's small primes and no big primes (similar to b) and x<=b: if n>0, minimality gives x<p*k, and with p<=a and k<q this yields x<a*q<=b, since a*q is a product of distinct prime factors of b.

technique: Strengthen a 'two special objects share some forbidden-class prime' statement to a 'they share an allowed-class prime' statement by minimal-counterexample descent: take the smallest violating pair and use a legal move out of an auxiliary blocked object to manufacture a strictly smaller violating pair.
how_used: To show two good numbers share a SMALL prime: else take good (b,b') with b'>=b minimal sharing only big primes; b and k good force a common small prime p, so p does not divide b' and b'>b. Strip b to a similar small-prime-only x with k<=x<=b; x is coprime to good b' so x is bad, giving a move x->b* with b* good; b's small primes all divide x so miss b*, making (b*,b) a smaller violating pair. With this, for similar bad a and good b, the move a->b' to a good b' shares a small prime with b that similarity forces into a, contradicting gcd(a,b')=1.

technique: Split a functional equation by whether the unknown function fixes every element of the embedded substructure (acts as the identity on the integers), separating the degenerate family from the rigid family.
how_used: Cases are 'there exists integer m with f(m) != m' (which forces f to be a single constant) versus 'f(m)=m for all integers m' (which forces f to be floor or ceiling). The whole classification into {constants, floor, ceiling} hangs on this dichotomy on the restriction f|_Z.

technique: Choose the integer shift a so that the inner numerator f(x)+a becomes a multiple of b, collapsing the equation into an equality between f at a known fixed point and f at the target value.
how_used: Repeatedly: plugging (1/2, -omega, 1-2omega) lands the inner argument on 0 to force omega=f(0)=0; (m, rb-C, b) gives f(r)=f(r-eta) linking adjacent integers; (C-p, p-C, q) collapses any p/q to f(0)=C. Each time a is picked to make (f(input)+a)/b an integer whose f-value is already pinned by f(m)=m or by an earlier step.

technique: Run an infinite descent on the denominator: assume a counterexample fraction in lowest terms with the smallest possible denominator, then construct a substitution producing a counterexample with a strictly smaller denominator, contradicting minimality.
how_used: Take a/b in (0,1) with minimal b such that f(a/b) != omega. If b is even, (1/2,(a-1)/2,b/2) gives a smaller-denominator (b/2) counterexample; if b=2k+1 is odd, coprimality yields r,m with ra-mb=k+omega and 0<=m<=r-1, r<b, and ((k+omega)/b, m, r) produces a denominator-r<b counterexample. Either way contradicts minimality, forcing f(x)=omega on all of (0,1).

technique: Bound a partition's ratio parameter from below by forcing the part-sums to divide a fixed total, so the ratio-plus-one is at least the total's smallest prime factor.
how_used: Since 1+3+...+(2k-1)=k^2 and a k-pabel splitting with B-sum s gives total (l+1)s=k^2, we get (l+1)|k^2 with l+1>=2, hence l+1>=p (smallest prime divisor of k), i.e. l>=p-1; this lower bound is then matched, and gcd(p-1,k)=1 is automatic since k has no prime factor below p.

technique: Hit a prescribed subset sum by scaling the identity 1+3+...+(2m-1)=m^2 over an arithmetic subprogression of the available odd numbers.
how_used: For odd k=pm, take B={p,3p,...,(2m-1)p}, a subset of the odds {1,3,...,2k-1}; its sum is p(1+3+...+(2m-1))=pm^2, so the complement A sums to k^2-pm^2=(p-1)pm^2, exactly p-1 times the sum of B, realizing l=p-1.

technique: Extend an equal-sum partition inductively by appending a block of consecutive odd numbers split into two equal-sum pairs (extreme pair vs middle pair).
how_used: For even k (p=2) needing two equal halves, induct k->k+4 by placing the new odds {2k+1,2k+7} in one set and {2k+3,2k+5} in the other (both pairs summing to 4k+8), with base cases k=4 ({1,7}|{3,5}) and k=6 ({1,3,5,9}|{7,11}).

technique: Divide out the leading factor of a binomial coefficient using C(n,m) = (n/m)·C(n-1,m-1) to turn 'n divides C(n,m)' into the condition that m! divides the sliding falling product (n-1)(n-2)···(n-m+1).
how_used: Both directions of the problem are recast this way: n | C(n,k) becomes k! | (n-1)···(n-k+1), and the non-divisibility n ∤ C(n,m) for 2≤m<k becomes m! ∤ (n-1)···(n-m+1). All subsequent work is checking these factorial-divides-product statements prime by prime via p-adic valuations.

technique: Use CRT to independently prescribe the p-adic valuation of each factor (n-i) of a falling product, fixing ν_p(n-i)=ν_p(i) for almost all i while deliberately boosting the valuation at exactly one chosen index.
how_used: For each prime p≤k pick t_p = ν_p(lcm(1,…,k-1)); require n ≡ 0 mod p^{t_p+1} when p∤k, and ν_p(n-k_p)=ν_p(k_p)+ν_p(k) when p|k (achieved by n ≡ k_p + p^{t_p+ν_p(k)} mod p^{t_p+ν_p(k)+1}). This forces ν_p(n-i)=ν_p(i) for all i<k except i=k_p where it gains an extra ν_p(k), so the whole product (n-1)···(n-k+1) has exactly ν_p(k!) — matching k! precisely but exceeding what smaller m need only at k.

technique: Place the required valuation boost on the LARGEST argmax of ν_p over a range, then use a doubling argument (if the maximizer were below half the range, doubling it stays in range with valuation ≥ the max, contradicting maximality) to prove that index is ≥ half the range.
how_used: k_p is chosen as the largest k_p∈{1,…,k-1} with ν_p(k_p)=t_p; then k_p ≥ k/2, since otherwise 2k_p<k would have ν_p(2k_p)≥t_p and be a larger maximizer. This guarantees that for every m≤k/2 the boosted index k_p lies at or beyond m, so the boost never enters the product (n-1)···(n-m+1), keeping its ν_p equal to ν_p((m-1)!) < ν_p(m!) and ensuring m! fails to divide it. This settles precisely the m|k sub-case (proper divisors of k, all ≤k/2); non-divisor m are closed separately by crux 1's exact-valuation property.

technique: When a value lying just above k^2 is a perfect square, write it as (k+r)^2 with r a positive integer to turn the square condition into a linear relation.
how_used: Set m^2+an+b=(m+r)^2 and n^2+am+b=(n+s)^2 with r,s>0, giving the linear relations an+b=2rm+r^2 and am+b=2sn+s^2; each infinite solution now carries shift data (r,s).

technique: To bound a shift parameter, substitute the crude linear upper bound on one variable into the other square relation and compare polynomial coefficients in the unbounded variable.
how_used: From an+b>=2m+1 get m<=(an+b-1)/2; plug into (n+s)^2=n^2+am+b<=n^2+a(an+b-1)/2+b, and infinitely many n forces 2s<=a^2/2, i.e. s<a^2/4 (symmetrically for r), so only finitely many shift pairs (r,s) are exceptional and one of them recurs for infinitely many (m,n).

technique: Two linear equations in two unknowns admitting infinitely many integer solutions must be proportional; equate their coefficient ratios.
how_used: With (r,s) fixed, an=2rm+r^2-b and 2sn=am+b-s^2 are linear in (m,n); infinitely many solutions force a/(2s)=2r/a=(r^2-b)/(b-s^2), so a^2=4rs; writing r=kx^2, s=ky^2 (gcd(x,y)=1) gives a=2kxy and b=k^2xy(x^2-xy+y^2), whence a|2b.

technique: To get the elementary symmetric functions of the shifted values 1-omega (or their reciprocals 1/(1-omega)) for omega ranging over the roots of a known polynomial, apply the root-coordinate change x -> x+1 (and pass to the reciprocal polynomial) to that polynomial and read its coefficients.
how_used: Starting from Phi_q(x)=1+x^{p^{r-1}}+...+x^{(p-1)p^{r-1}} whose roots are the primitive q-th roots omega, form Q(x)=Phi_q(1+x)=sum_{j=0}^{p-1}(1+x)^{j p^{r-1}} with roots omega-1; reciprocating, Q(x)=p(-x)^d P(-1/x) where P has roots 1/(1-omega) and d=phi(q). Comparing coefficients shows c_n (the n-th elementary symmetric polynomial of the 1/(1-omega)) equals (1/p) times the x^n coefficient of Q, turning integrality of the target sums into a coefficient computation.

technique: Determine the p-divisibility of a polynomial's coefficients by reducing it modulo p with the Frobenius/freshman's-dream identity (x+y)^p == x^p+y^p, collapsing the nested binomial expansions so every coefficient except the leading one vanishes mod p.
how_used: Modulo p, Q(x) == 1+(1+x^{p^{r-1}})+...+(1+x^{p^{r-1}})^{p-1} == [(1+x^{p^{r-1}})^p - 1]/x^{p^{r-1}}, and since binom(p,j)==0 mod p for 0= r-2 with first equality at n = d - p^{r-1} + 1.

technique: Locate the first index where a power sum fails integrality by propagating p-adic valuations through the Newton-identity recurrence, at each step identifying the single summand of minimum valuation that drives the running valuation down by one.
how_used: Newton's identities give S_n = sum_j (-1)^{j+1} c_j S_{n-j} (+ (-1)^{n+1} n c_n for n<=d). Using nu_p(n c_n)>=r-2 with equality first at m+1=d-p^{r-1}+1, two propagation rules hold for n>d: if all nu_p(S_{n-j})>=l and nu_p(S_{n-d})>=l+1 then nu_p(S_n)>=l, while if nu_p(S_{n-d})=l then nu_p(S_n)=l-1. Induction yields nu_p(S_{kd+m+1})=r-2-k, so the least n with nu_p(S_n)<0 (i.e. S_n not an integer) is n=(r-1)d+m+1=((p-1)r-1)p^{r-1}+1, which is the answer.

technique: Exploit the 2-adic valuation of the multiplier as a fixed bit-shift, so the low bits of ax are forced zero and known low bits of x bootstrap the next block of x from the given combination.
how_used: With k=ν₂(a)>0, the bottom k bits of ax vanish, so the bottom k bits of c=x⊕ax equal those of x; those recovered k bits of x then determine the next k bits of ax (since a's nonzero part starts at bit k), recovering the next k bits of x, and iterating block-by-block recovers x entirely — proving x↦x⊕ax is injective when a is even.

technique: Disprove injectivity by building an explicit collision from numbers of the form 2^n±1, whose products with the multiplier are non-overlapping concatenations of its bit-block.
how_used: For odd a, pick n with 2^n>a and set x=2^n−1 (n ones) and y=2^n+1; then ay=a·2^n+a is two non-overlapping copies PP of a's n-bit block P, and ax=a·2^n−a=(a−1)·2^n+(2^n−a) splits as QR with Q=a−1, R=2^n−a. XORing y clears the bottom bit of each P-copy (both 1 since a odd) and XORing x complements R to Q, so x⊕ax=y⊕ay=QQ with x≠y.

technique: Multiply an irrational linear form by all sign-conjugates of its radicals to collapse the product into a rational integer (the field norm), which is nonzero and therefore at least 1 in absolute value.
how_used: The four-fold product (a√2+b√3+c√5)(a√2−b√3−c√5)(a√2+b√3−c√5)(a√2−b√3+c√5) collapses (cross terms in √15 cancel) to Z=(2a²−3b²−5c²)²−60b²c², a rational integer. Z≠0 because Z=0 would force 60 to be a perfect square (false by unique factorization), so |Z|≥1, supplying the lower bound that drives the second part.

technique: Bound the remaining conjugate factors of a norm by Cauchy-Schwarz to convert a lower bound on the norm into a lower bound on the largest coefficient.
how_used: One factor of Z is the given small form (<ε); each of the other three is bounded via |a|√2+|b|√3+|c|√5 ≤ √(2+3+5)·√(a²+b²+c²) ≤ √30·max(|a|,|b|,|c|). Combining with |Z|≥1 gives 1<ε·(√30·max)³, hence max(|a|,|b|,|c|)>ε^{−1/3}/√30.

technique: Produce an arbitrarily small nonzero element of a quadratic ring by taking a high power of a ring element whose absolute value lies in (0,1).
how_used: Since 0<√6−2<1, the power (√6−2)^k lands in (0,ε/√3) for large k and equals a₀√6+b₀ with nonzero integers a₀,b₀; setting a=3a₀, b=b₀, c=0 gives |a√2+b√3+c√5|=√3·(√6−2)^k<ε, proving the existence half.

technique: Recast a single base-B digit as the suffix residue N mod B^m, turning a per-digit magnitude claim into a lower bound on that residue.
how_used: Instead of reasoning about digits of n^k in base 2n, look at the rightmost l digits = n^k mod (2n)^l; the l-th digit from the right is at least d+1 exactly when that residue is at least (d+1)(2n)^{l-1}, so the whole 'every digit > d' statement reduces to proving such a size bound for each 1 <= l <= k.

technique: Compute a remainder N mod (a*b) when a common factor divides both N and the modulus by pulling that factor out and reducing only the cofactor.
how_used: Since n^l divides both n^k = n^l * n^{k-l} and the modulus (2n)^l = 2^l * n^l, factor out n^l to get n^k mod (2n)^l = n^l * (n^{k-l} mod 2^l), reducing an awkward composite modulus to a reduction of n^{k-l} modulo just 2^l.

technique: Use that the base is a unit modulo the prime-power factor (odd number is invertible mod 2^l) to guarantee the reduced cofactor is nonzero, hence at least 1.
how_used: Because n is odd, n^{k-l} is odd and so n^{k-l} mod 2^l is an odd integer c(k,l) with 1 <= c <= 2^l - 1; the bound c >= 1 is the only fact needed and is exactly why the 'odd n' hypothesis is required so the suffix residue never collapses to a smaller digit.

technique: To force a necessary condition on an exponent in a 'for all n' divisibility, evaluate at the structured index n = p-1 where each binomial coefficient collapses to a sign mod p, turning the sum into a parity count.
how_used: Taking n=2 needs 3 | 2+2^k forcing k even; more generally n=p-1 gives binom(p-1,i) ≡ (-1)^i mod p, so S(p-1) ≡ 1+(-1)^k+1+(-1)^k+... mod p, which is divisible only when k is even — pinning the answer to even k.

technique: Expand a binomial coefficient as the running product ∏ (n-(j-1))/j and, using n ≡ -1 mod p^e, discard every factor whose index j is coprime to p because it is a unit ≡ ±1, so only the factors with p | j survive and reassemble into a smaller binomial coefficient.
how_used: With p^e fully dividing n+1, this yields binom(n,i) ≡ ± binom((n+1)/p - 1, floor(i/p)) mod p^e: the p∤j fractions are each ±1, while the p|j fractions (j=p,2p,...) contract to binom((n+1)/p-1, floor(i/p)).

technique: Collapse a sum whose index splits into consecutive blocks of length p, on each of which the summand is congruent mod p^e to a single term of a smaller self-similar copy of the sum, into p times that smaller sum.
how_used: Grouping the n+1 terms of S(n) by floor(i/p) into ranges of length p makes each block ≡ one term of S((n+1)/p - 1), so S(n) ≡ p·S((n+1)/p - 1) mod p^e; the routine descent on e (base e=0, plus CRT over the prime powers of n+1) then yields n+1 | S(n) for even k.

technique: Encode a shift-and-finite-difference operator on functions indexed by Z/aZ as multiplication by a fixed polynomial in the group ring of the cyclic group over the coefficient ring.
how_used: Each f:Z/aZ->F_p is identified with alpha_f = sum_{k} f(-k) x^k in F_p[x]/(x^a-1); the shift becomes multiplication by x, so the difference operator Delta corresponds exactly to multiplication by (x-1), turning 'Delta^k f = f for some k>=1' into '(x-1)^m alpha = alpha for some m>=1' in that ring.

technique: Split a finite ring by CRT at a chosen element into its nilpotent-local and unit-local factors to control which elements are recurrent under repeated multiplication by that element.
how_used: Substituting y=x-1 and writing P(y)=(y+1)^a-1 = y^d Q(y) with Q(0)!=0 gives F_p[y]/(P) ≅ F_p[y]/(y^d) × F_p[y]/(Q); y is nilpotent in the first factor and a unit in the second, so y^m alpha = alpha is solvable iff alpha vanishes in the nilpotent factor, leaving the unit factor of dimension a-d and hence p^{a-d} solutions, where d = p^{nu_p(a)} is the multiplicity of the root x=1 (via Lucas, or Frobenius x^a-1=(x^b-1)^{p^d} with p∤b).

technique: Lift a count from mod p to mod p^e by an induction that shows each solution mod p^{e-1} has a constant-size fiber of solutions above it, using the additive splitting of a lift as base plus p^{e-1} times a mod-p function.
how_used: A mod-p^{e+1} function is (e+1)-good iff its reduction mod p^e is e-good; existence of at least one good lift is shown by iterating Delta^m on any lift until Delta^{am}g=Delta^{bm}g. The good lifts of a fixed e-good f form the coset g + p^e·{1-good h}, so each e-good function has exactly p^{a-p^{nu_p(a)}} good lifts, multiplying the count by that factor per level to give p^{e(a-p^{nu_p(a)})}.

technique: Among infinitely many length-L windows of a {0,1}-valued (parity) function on the integers, pigeonhole over the finitely many possible window-patterns to find two starting positions whose windows agree termwise.
how_used: Balancedness of P(x)=(x+a)(x+b) means x+a and x+b share the same parity of Omega (count of prime factors). The parity of Omega over 50 consecutive integers is one of only 2^50 patterns, so among all starting integers two starts m<n have identical length-50 patterns; setting a=m-1, b=n-1 makes x+a and x+b (x=1..50) the two equal windows, so each P(x) (x=1..50) is balanced — answering part a) Yes.

technique: Reindex a sliding two-factor product so its factors become n and n+d, turning 'product always balanced' into a fixed-shift equality of the per-term prime-count parity for all large arguments.
how_used: With b>a, substitute x=m-a in P(x)=(x+a)(x+b) to get P(m-a)=m(m+(b-a)); balanced for all m>a forces Omega(m) and Omega(m+(b-a)) to have equal parity for every m>a, i.e. the Omega-parity is periodic with period d=b-a for arguments exceeding a, so all sufficiently large multiples k(b-a) share one fixed parity type.

technique: Defeat a claimed period of a total-prime-count parity by comparing an argument with its double, which differ by exactly one prime factor and so must carry opposite parity.
how_used: Both k(b-a) and 2k(b-a) are multiples of d=b-a and exceed a, so the induced periodicity forces them to the same Omega-parity; but Omega(2k(b-a))=Omega(k(b-a))+1 has opposite parity, a contradiction — hence b>a is impossible and a=b (part b).

technique: Build the required summands as the entrywise products of a 2x2 grid whose row-sums and column-sums come from a factorization of the target value.
how_used: To realize any composite s, write s=(w+x)(y+z) with w,x,y,z positive integers and set a=wy, b=wz, c=xy, d=xz; then a+b+c+d=(w+x)(y+z)=s automatically, and abc+abd+acd+bcd factors as wxyz(w+x)(y+z), which is divisible by s.

technique: Complete an elementary symmetric expression into a product of partial sums by adding a multiple of the total sum, so divisibility by the sum becomes divisibility of a factored product.
how_used: Using the identity (a+b)(a+c)(a+d) = (abc+abd+acd+bcd) + a^2(a+b+c+d), reduce mod s to get abc+abd+acd+bcd ≡ (a+b)(a+c)(a+d); since each of a+b, a+c, a+d is a positive integer strictly less than s=a+b+c+d, s dividing this product forces s to be composite.

technique: Set two of the three free summands equal to a fixed monochromatic element so the sum-of-three closure rule becomes closure of that color under adding twice that element.
how_used: With k red, k+k+(2n-1)k=(2n+1)k inducts to show every odd multiple (2n+1)k is red (and symmetrically for green); taking 1 red then forces all odd numbers red.

technique: Iterate the same additive closure on two same-colored elements of opposite parity to force every sufficiently large integer into that color.
how_used: Assuming some even 2m is red alongside the red 1, the step 1+1+x makes 2m, 2m+2, 2m+4, ... all red, so the only integers that could still be green are the finitely many evens below 2m.

technique: Contradict a finite candidate set for a color by showing that single element of a color is closed under odd multiples and therefore generates infinitely many of that color.
how_used: Each leftover even below 2m, if green, would force its infinitely many odd multiples green, contradicting that only finitely many values remain; if none is green the both-colors-exist condition fails, so no even can be red and all evens are green.

technique: Factor a^{2k}-b^{2k}=(a^k-b^k)(a^k+b^k) and evaluate the cofactor a^k+b^k modulo 4 to pin its 2-adic valuation as exactly one when k is even.
how_used: With a=5, b=3 and n=2k where k is even (k carries the remaining factors of 2), 5^k+3^k ≡ 1^k+(-1)^k ≡ 2 (mod 4), so v_2(5^k+3^k)=1; hence v_2(5^n-3^n)=v_2(5^k-3^k)+1, i.e. a(2k)=a(k)+1, the engine that increments a under exponent-doubling.

technique: Show the difference of two integer-valued functions is invariant under a halving step by proving each side increases by exactly one, reducing the inequality to base cases via induction on v_2(n).
how_used: Under n=2k with k even, a(n)=a(k)+1 (from the cofactor valuation) and b(n)=b(k)+1 (since 2^{b(k)} ≤ k < 2^{b(k)+1} gives 2^{b(k)+1} ≤ 2k < 2^{b(k)+2}); thus a-b is preserved, so a ≤ b+3 follows by induction on the number m of factors of 2 in n from the base cases.

technique: Compute the cofactor a^k+b^k modulo 16 using the period-4 cycles of each base mod 16 to extract its exact 2-adic valuation at the base of the induction.
how_used: For n ≡ 2 mod 4 (k odd), 5^k mod 16 ∈ {5,13} and 3^k mod 16 ∈ {3,11} (period 4), so 5^k+3^k ≡ 8 (mod 16) in both subcases — exactly 3 factors of 2; combined with the single factor from 5^k-3^k this anchors a=4 for n ≡ 2 mod 4.

technique: When the defining relation only promises SOME prime divisor satisfies it, evaluate at prime powers (where the prime divisor is unique) to turn the existential into a forced equation.
how_used: Taking n=q prime, the unique prime divisor is q itself, so f(q)=f(1)-f(q), forcing f(q)=½f(1); taking n=q^k the only prime is q, giving the recurrence a_k=a_{k-1}-a_1 with a_1=f(q), so a_2=0 and f(q^k)=(2-k)/2·f(1). Feeding the data f(2^2014)+f(3^2015)+f(5^2016)=2013 pins ½f(1)=-1/3, i.e. f(p)=-1/3 for all primes.

technique: Induct on the number of prime factors counted with multiplicity to extend a closed form from prime powers to all integers, the chosen prime being harmless because every prime carries the same value.
how_used: With every f(p)=-1/3, induction on Ω(n) gives f(n)=(Ω(n)-2)/3: whichever prime p the existential selects, f(n/p) depends only on Ω(n)-1 so the choice is immaterial. Applied to 2014^2 (Ω=6), 2015^3 (Ω=9), 2016^5 (Ω=40, since 2016=2^5·3^2·7) yields 4/3+7/3+38/3=49/3.

technique: Contradict a forced integer ratio of two nearly-equal elements by showing the resulting divisor would have to divide a strictly smaller positive quantity than itself.
how_used: If A had three elements a<b<c then B holds a+b<a+c<b+c, so the largest quotient (b+c)/(a+c) must be a positive integer, forcing a+c | b+c; subtracting gives a+c | b-a, but b-a>0 and a+c>b-a (since c>b), impossible. Hence |A|<=2.

technique: Bound one closure-linked set's size by mapping it injectively into the other: fix the largest element and divide it by each smaller element to produce that many distinct images.
how_used: If B had four elements a<b<c<d, then d/a, d/b, d/c are three distinct elements of A (each a legal quotient), contradicting |A|<=2; so |B|<=3, giving |A cup B|<=5.

technique: Make the only two permitted quotients coincide by forcing the three generating values into geometric progression, then parametrize so the required closure element lands back in the set.
how_used: To attain 5, take B={a,ta,t^2 a}; since A has only two elements the ratios c/b and b/a must be equal (GP), and the A-sum t+t^2 must lie in B, achieved by a=t+1, t=2, giving A={2,4}, B={3,6,12}.

technique: Prove an impossibility/non-existence claim by assuming the forbidden quantity is positive and explicitly constructing the very object it forbids, contradicting the hypothesis.
how_used: The thieves' leader asserts no equal 11-way split of the 5940 Kulotnik exists. Rather than argue directly, the solution assumes b>0 (at least one 12-coin was stolen) and builds a concrete fair division of the loot among the 11 thieves, contradicting the leader's proven impossibility, which forces b=0.

technique: When a coefficient times a variable is shown divisible by a modulus coprime to that coefficient, transfer the divisibility onto the variable itself using gcd = 1.
how_used: From 11a+12b=5940 rewrite 11a=12(495-b), so 12 | 11a; since gcd(11,12)=1 this gives 12 | a. Symmetrically 12b=11(540-a) gives 11 | 12b, hence 11 | b. These two divisibilities (a a multiple of 12, b a multiple of 11) are what make the fair-split construction possible.

technique: Realize each recipient's exact target share as a fixed base unit plus an integer number of equal packets each worth lcm of the denominations, handing out the base units first so the remainder regroups cleanly.
how_used: Each thief must get 540 = 12 + 4*132. The a multiple-of-12 eleven-coins form a/12 bags of 132 = lcm(11,12); give each of the 11 thieves one 12-coin (possible since 11 | b and b>0), leaving b-11 twelve-coins still divisible by 11 that form (b/11 - 1) more 132-bags; the leftover 5940-132 = 5808 = 132*44 splits as 44 = 11*4 bags, 4 per thief.

technique: Replace a sequence by the integer-valued running average of its partial sums, and use integrality to upgrade a strict inequality on the average into a weak monotonicity that forces eventual constancy.
how_used: Set b_k = (a_1+...+a_k)/k, an integer because k divides the partial sum by definition of a_k. From (k+1)b_{k+1} = k b_k + a_{k+1} and the range bound a_{k+1} <= k, get b_{k+1} <= (k b_k + k)/(k+1) < b_k + 1; since b_{k+1} is an integer this gives b_{k+1} <= b_k, so (b_k) is a non-increasing nonnegative integer sequence, hence eventually constant.

technique: Recover an original term from consecutive values of a partial-sum running average via a difference identity, to transfer eventual-constancy of the average back to the original sequence.
how_used: Using a_k = (k+1)b_{k+1} - k b_k (the difference of consecutive partial sums), once b_N = b_{N+1} = ... = b substitute to get a_k = (k+1)b - k b = b for all k >= N, so the original sequence is eventually constant equal to b. (The solver noted a harmless off-by-one in the label but recovered the correct identity and conclusion.)

transfer constancy from auxiliary to original

technique: Rewrite a cyclotomic-type quotient (x^p+1)/(x+1) as an Aurifeuillian identity (x+1)^{p-1} - p*x*(g(x))^2, turning it into a difference of two squares whenever p*x is a perfect square.
how_used: For p=7 the quotient X^6-X^5+X^4-X^3+X^2-X+1 = (X+1)^6 - 7X*(X^2+X+1)^2. With X=7^{7^n}, 7X = 7^{7^n+1} has even exponent (7^n is odd), so 7X = C^2 with C = 7^{(7^n+1)/2}; then Q = [(X+1)^3]^2 - [C(X^2+X+1)]^2 = (A-B)(A+B) with A=(X+1)^3, B=C(X^2+X+1). Both factors exceed 1 (a one-line size estimate: A ~ X^3, B ~ sqrt(7) X^{2.5}, so A>B and A-B grows; e.g. n=0 gives A=512, B=399, A-B=113>1), so Q is composite (Omega(Q)>=2).

technique: Lower-bound the number of prime factors with multiplicity by induction, peeling off a known proper factor at each step whose cofactor is itself composite, so the count rises by at least two per step.
how_used: Since X+1 | X^7+1 with X=7^{7^n}, write 7^{7^{n+1}}+1 = (7^{7^n}+1)*Q where Q=(X^7+1)/(X+1) is composite (Omega(Q)>=2 by the previous crux), so Omega increases by at least 2 as n->n+1. Base case n=0: 7^{7^0}+1 = 7+1 = 8 = 2^3 has Omega=3 = 2*0+3. By induction Omega(7^{7^n}+1) >= 2n+3.

technique: To bound a count over an interval [1,M] whose length M is a sum of structured summands, partition the interval into consecutive blocks whose lengths are exactly those summands, then bound the count block by block.
how_used: Since sigma(n) = sum of the divisors d_1,...,d_k of n, tile [1, sigma(n)] into consecutive intervals I_j of length d_j; the n-th coprime-to-n integer is at least sigma(n) iff [1,sigma(n)] holds at most n coprimes, which is then established blockwise.

technique: Relax a coprimality condition to a divisor of the modulus and use that any window of d consecutive integers contains exactly phi(d) integers coprime to d, giving a per-window cap.
how_used: On a block I_j of length d_j a divisor of n, integers coprime to n are coprime to d_j, so at most phi(d_j) of them; summing over all divisors and applying Gauss's identity sum_{d|n} phi(d) = n caps the total coprimes in [1,sigma(n)] by n.

technique: Force a tiling bound to be strict by reordering the blocks so that a chosen block of length d secretly contains two forbidden residues, dropping its coprime count below phi(d).
how_used: When distinct primes p<q both divide n, reorder divisors so d_1=q; then both p and q lie in I_1=[1,q], so I_1 holds strictly fewer than phi(q)=q-1 coprimes-to-n, making the overall inequality strict and leaving only n=p^e as the equality case.

technique: Express a target quantity as a difference of two squares, then factor it into shifted linear factors of the half-sum and half-difference variables to read off a product-form parametrization of all solutions.
how_used: Integer root of x^2+mx+n forces m^2-4n=p^2; consecutive m gives (m+1)^2-4n=q^2, and subtracting gives 2m+1=q^2-p^2 (so p,q opposite parity). Setting q-p=2x+1, q+p=2y+1 and writing m=(q^2-p^2-1)/2, the equation 4n=m^2-p^2 factors as (q-p-1)(q-p+1)(q+p-1)(q+p+1)/4 = (2x)(2x+2)(2y)(2y+2)/4, yielding the exact characterization n=x(x+1)y(y+1).

technique: Bound a reciprocal sum over a set parametrized as a Cartesian product by factoring it into a product of independent one-variable sums and telescoping each via 1/(k(k+1)) = 1/k - 1/(k+1).
how_used: Since S = {x(x+1)y(y+1) : x,y>0}, the sum over S is bounded by the full double sum which factors as (sum_{x>=1} 1/(x(x+1)))(sum_{y>=1} 1/(y(y+1))); each telescopes to 1, giving sum_{n in S} 1/n <= 1.

technique: Force a number to have many representations as a sum of two squares by making it divisible by several distinct primes congruent to 1 mod 4, combining their two-square forms via the Brahmagupta-Fibonacci (Lagrange) identity.
how_used: Three consecutive m reduce to 2q^2+2 = p^2 + r^2; the trivial (p,r)=(q-1,q+1) gives n=0, so a third sum-of-two-squares representation is needed. Choosing q with q^2+1 divisible by at least three distinct primes 1 mod 4 yields at least three ways to write 2q^2+2 as a sum of two squares, producing infinitely many valid n (with no parity obstruction mod 8).

technique: To kill solutions of n^2 | b^n+1, pass to the smallest prime divisor p of n and bound ord_p(b) via gcd of the apparent exponent with p-1.
how_used: When b+1 is a power of 2: n is odd (else b^n+1 = 2 mod 8 kills 4|n^2), so p is odd; from b^{2n}=1 (mod p) the order divides gcd(2n,p-1)=2 (since p is the least prime factor of n, p-1 shares no factor with n), so p | b^2-1=(b-1)(b+1); b+1 being a pure power of 2 forces p | b-1, whence b^n+1 = 2 (mod p), contradiction, leaving only n=1.

technique: Guarantee a brand-new prime divisor at each step of an inductive prime chain by invoking Zsigmondy on the growing exponent.
how_used: Building distinct primes p_0,p_1,... with p_0^2...p_{k-1}^2 p_k | b^{p_0...p_{k-1}}+1: since p_0...p_k | b^{p_0...p_{k-1}}+1, Zsigmondy yields an odd prime q outside {p_0,...,p_k} dividing b^{p_0...p_k}+1, taken as p_{k+1}; the b>2 hypothesis is exactly what defeats the only exceptional case (2^3+1=3^2 has no primitive prime), and elementarily one shows (c^p+1)/(c+1) is coprime to c+1 apart from p once c^p+1 > p(c+1).

technique: Lift a single prime factor to its square by multiplying the exponent by that same prime and reading off the +1 jump in its p-adic valuation via LTE.
how_used: Given p_k | b^{P}+1 with P=p_0...p_{k-1} and p_k odd, nu_{p_k}(b^{P p_k}+1) = nu_{p_k}(b^P+1)+1, so p_k^2 | b^{P p_k}+1; old primes p|P keep their valuation since p_k is coprime to p, so every prime of n=p_0...p_k appears to at least twice its exponent, giving n^2 | b^n+1, producing infinitely many valid n.

technique: Exploit a nontrivial factorization of the modulus to build a constant sequence whose every fixed-length cyclic window sums to exactly the modulus
how_used: To show composite n fails, write n=a*b with 1<a,b<n, set m_i=a for 1<=i<n and m_n=0. The total (n-1)a is not divisible by n (neither factor is), yet from any start index i a window of length b (or b+1, depending on whether it must skip the m_n=0 slot) sums to a*b=n, which is 0 mod n, so no index i avoids a divisible partial sum.

technique: Iteratively jump from each position to the nearest later index that closes a zero-residue window, producing a walk whose step length is bounded by the period
how_used: Under the contradiction hypothesis (every start index i admits a window summing to 0 mod n, and the window length is < n since the full sum is nonzero mod n), define i_0 arbitrarily and let i_{k+1} be the smallest index after i_k (going cyclically mod n) with m_{i_k+1}+...+m_{i_{k+1}} = 0 mod n. Each step satisfies i_{k+1}-i_k <= n-1, giving a chain i_0,...,i_n of n+1 indices.

technique: When a walk returns to its starting residue mod n, equate its telescoped block-sum to the winding count times the full-period sum, then apply primality
how_used: Pigeonhole on the n+1 chain positions mod n yields i_r = i_s (mod n) with r<s; the telescoped sum of the intervening zero-blocks is 0 mod n. But i_s-i_r=d*n with 1<=d<=n-1, so that same sum equals d*(m_1+...+m_n). Since n is prime and divides neither d nor the total sum, d*(total) cannot be 0 mod n, a contradiction proving every prime works.

technique: To produce a smaller term lying in the same arithmetic progression An+B as a given square, lower the square's root by A so the shifted square stays in the same residue class mod A.
how_used: From the minimal square M^2 = Ak+B, form (M-A)^2 = M^2 - A(2M-A) = A(k-(2M-A))+B, so it is again a term of the sequence (index shifted down by 2M-A), and it is strictly smaller because 0 < M-A < M when M > A.

technique: Use the extremal (minimal) hypothesis to force the constructed smaller object out of the valid index range, then read the desired bound off the boundary inequality.
how_used: Since M^2 is the smallest square at index >= 0, the smaller square (M-A)^2 cannot have a nonnegative index, so k-(2M-A) < 0; then A(k-(2M-A))+B < B gives (M-A)^2 < B, hence M-A < sqrt(B), i.e. M < A+sqrt(B) (the case M <= A being immediate).

technique: Build a structure-preserving self-map on a residue-class-defined set by using an algebraic identity that rewrites the complementary quantity as a perfect square.
how_used: On A∪B (elements n with n and 4−n both QR, or both non-QR, equivalently n(4−n) a nonzero QR), the map n↦n(4−n) lands in B because 4−n(4−n)=(n−2)² is automatically a QR; so the image value b=n(4−n) has both b and 4−b QR, i.e. b∈B.

technique: Count the preimages of a fiber by reducing the defining relation to a quadratic and reading off whether its discriminant is a nonzero quadratic residue.
how_used: For fixed b∈B, n(4−n)=b becomes n²−4n+b=0 with discriminant 4(4−b); since 4−b is a nonzero QR, the discriminant is a nonzero QR, so there are exactly two roots, proving n↦n(4−n) is two-to-one onto B off the degenerate fiber b=4.

technique: Pair each element with its partner under the defining involution so the product over the set collapses to the product over the image set, isolating the involution's unique fixed point as the leftover factor.
how_used: Pairing n with 4−n (the involution leaving φ(n)=n(4−n) invariant), each pair {n,4−n} multiplies to its image b=n(4−n)∈B, so ∏_{n∈A∪B∖{2}} n = ∏_{b∈B} b. The involution's unique fixed point is n=2 (4−2=2, where the discriminant vanishes and φ(2)=4∉B): it is the single element removed from A∪B before the collapse (its A-vs-B class depends on whether 2 is a QR, but its image escapes B). Since A∪B=A⊔B, the left side equals (∏_{a∈A}a·∏_{b∈B}b)/2; cancelling ∏_{b∈B}b gives ∏_{a∈A} a ≡ 2.

technique: To evaluate a floor-sum of k-th roots exactly, partition {1,...,c} by each integer's unique decomposition into its k-th-power-free part times a perfect k-th power, and count each part-class by a floor.
how_used: Writing every positive integer uniquely as b*m^k with b k-th-power-free, the class C_b={x<=c : x/b is a perfect k-th power} has size floor((c/b)^{1/k}); since these classes over k-th-power-free b<=c partition [1,c], one gets sum_{b in B, b<=c} floor((c/b)^{1/k}) = c, the closed-form engine of the whole proof.

technique: Match a greedily-minimal recursion x = 1 + S(x) to a candidate set by plugging the established count identity into S at a candidate: a target leaves exactly +1 slack (equation solvable) while a non-target makes S(x)=x (forcing the absurd x=x+1).
how_used: Enumerating the k-th-power-free integers b_1<b_2<... and inducting, the identity gives b_n = 1 + sum_{i<n} floor((b_n/b_i)^{1/k}), so a_n<=b_n; but any a_n strictly between b_{n-1} and b_n is non-power-free, where the identity yields sum_{i<n} floor((a_n/b_i)^{1/k}) = a_n, contradicting a_n = 1 + that sum, so a_n=b_n.

technique: To show a generating condition is necessary, exhibit a proper subset closed under the defining operation that still contains the given seeds.
how_used: When gcd(m,n)=d>1, the set dZ of all multiples of d is admissible (if d|x and d|y then d|x^2+kxy+y^2 for every k), and it contains m,n yet is not all of Z, so coprimality is forced.

technique: Specialize the equal-argument case of a symmetric closure relation to extract a cleaner closure operation.
how_used: Setting y=x in x^2+kxy+y^2 gives (k+2)x^2; as k ranges over all integers k+2 ranges over all integers, so every integer multiple of any square in A again lies in A — in particular a*m^2 and b*n^2 are admissible.

technique: Manufacture the unit 1 not as a linear Bezout combination but as the SQUARE of one, so it survives a sum-of-squares closure.
how_used: Since gcd(m,n)=1 implies gcd(m^2,n^2)=1, pick a,b with a*m^2+b*n^2=1; with a*m^2,b*n^2 in A and the k=2 closure (x+y)^2 in A, get 1=(a*m^2+b*n^2)^2 in A, after which every integer k=k*1^2 lies in A.

technique: Bound a variable forced to divide a fixed constant by comparing the p-adic valuation each prime power gets on the two sides of the equation.
how_used: x | 2012·2 = 2^3·503. A factor 503 | x would cube on the LHS forcing 503^3, hence 503^2 | xyz+2, impossible since 503|x makes xyz+2 ≡ 2; and v_2 forces x not 2^m for m≥2 because 2012 contributes only 2^2 and xyz+2 = 2^m yz + 2 only 2^1. So x ∈ {1,2}.

technique: Deduce a linear congruence y ≡ -z mod p from the cubic congruence y^3 ≡ -z^3 by combining it with Fermat's little theorem to cancel exponents down to 1.
how_used: With 503 | y^3+z^3 and p=503 (=3·167+2): Fermat gives y^502 ≡ z^502, while y^3 ≡ -z^3 raised to the 167th power gives y^501 ≡ -z^501; dividing the two power congruences forces y ≡ -z (mod 503), so 503 | y+z, i.e. y+z = 503k.

technique: Factor the symmetric sum a^3+b^3=(a+b)((a-b)^2+ab) to expose the constrained linear factor (a+b), then substitute its known value to collapse the equation into a small bounded form.
how_used: Writing y^3+z^3=(y+z)((y-z)^2+yz) with y+z=503k turns the two equations into k(y-z)^2+(k-4)yz=8 and k(y-z)^2+(k-1)yz=1, whose tiny right sides bound k (k≤4, then k∈{2,4} ruled out for (1); k=1 for (2)), yielding (y,z)=(251,252).

technique: To force a factor n into a binomial coefficient, rewrite it with the absorption identity that swaps the multiplier off the lower index onto the top, turning a divisibility claim into a coprimality claim.
how_used: To show n divides C(n, p-2n) for a prime p, use (p-2n)C(n,p-2n) = n C(n-1, p-2n-1); since all factors are nonzero (p>=2n, p odd), the factor n on the right must land inside C(n,p-2n) once n and p-2n are shown coprime.

technique: Pin a gcd to 1 by showing it divides a prime yet is strictly smaller than that prime.
how_used: d = gcd(p-2n, n) divides (p-2n)+2n = p, but d <= n < p, so d=1, giving the coprimality of n and p-2n needed to conclude n | C(n,p-2n).

technique: Disprove a forced divisibility by exhibiting an index where the normalized binomial coefficient fails to be an integer, detected through p-adic valuation of a consecutive-integer numerator versus a factorial denominator.
how_used: For odd composite m = p(2k+1), choosing n = pk lands in [m/3, m/2] and makes (1/n)C(n, m-2n) = (1/(pk))C(pk,p) = (pk-1)(pk-2)...(pk-(p-1))/p!, where p divides p! but none of the consecutive numerator terms, so n does not divide the binomial (the even case m=2k uses n=k, where m-2n=0 makes the coefficient 1).

technique: To show a whole arithmetic progression of target values is attainable, parametrize the unknowns linearly in a single parameter so the cubic difference factor becomes that parameter and the equation forces the target into a linear function of it.
how_used: Set m=2k-1, n=k-1 so m-n=k; then (m^2+n)(n^2+m)=(4k-3)k^3=a(m-n)^3 forces a=4k-3, covering 5,9,...,2009 (an arithmetic progression of step 4) and giving over 500 friendly values in {1,...,2012}.

technique: Rewrite a product on one side as one quarter of a difference of two squares, so that after clearing a common factor the equation states that a specific expression must itself be a perfect square.
how_used: Write (m^2+n)(n^2+m)=1/4((m^2+n+n^2+m)^2-(m^2+n-n^2-m)^2); using m^2+n-n^2-m=(m-n)(m+n-1) the a=2 equation becomes (m^2+n+n^2+m)^2=(m-n)^2(8(m-n)+(m+n-1)^2), forcing 8(m-n)+(m+n-1)^2 to be a perfect square.

technique: When a quantity just above a known square B^2 must be a perfect square, write it as (B+2s)^2 with s>=1, subtract to get an equation in s, and bound s by comparing the sizes of the two factors to pin s to a single value.
how_used: Set (m+n-1+2s)^2=8(m-n)+(m+n-1)^2 with B=m+n-1; subtracting gives s(m+n-1+s)=2(m-n), and since m+n-1+s>m-n one forces s<2, so s=1 and m=3n, after which the size check LHS>27n^3>16n^3=RHS kills it.

technique: When the hypothesis lets you replace the argument n by n^e and the exponent e is itself invariant under that replacement, re-apply the hypothesis indefinitely to reach n^{e^k} for all k.
how_used: Reading the rad-divisibility condition as 'p | f(n) implies p | f(n^{rad(n)})', and noting rad(n^{rad(n)^k}) = rad(n) for every k, lets one iterate the implication to conclude p | f(n^{rad(n)^k}) for all k from a single p | f(n).

technique: Engineer one argument that simultaneously satisfies a divisibility (p | f at that argument) and makes the iterated exponent a multiple of p-1, so Fermat collapses the huge power to 1 and forces f(1) to be divisible by p.
how_used: Writing f(x)=g(x)x^m with g(0)≠0, taking t>0, p a prime factor of g(-t), and n=(p-1)t gives p-1 | n and f(n) ≡ f(-t) ≡ 0 (mod p); then for large k, p-1 | rad(n)^k so n^{rad(n)^k} ≡ 1, yielding f(1) ≡ f(n^{rad(n)^k}) ≡ 0 (mod p).

technique: Show every prime dividing a polynomial's values lies in a fixed finite set, then invoke that a polynomial with finitely many prime divisors over the integers must be constant.
how_used: Each prime factor of g(-t) is shown to divide the fixed nonzero number g(0)·f(1) (the p | g(0) case arising when gcd(p,(p-1)t)>1, since then p|t and g(-t)≡g(0)); since the prime factors of g(-t) over all t are thereby finite, g must be constant, giving f(x)=a x^m.

technique: Count the quadruples on which two copies of an expression agree as the additive energy of its representation-count function, i.e. set the collision count equal to the sum of squares of the per-value multiplicities.
how_used: With s_r = #{(a,b): a^2+b^5 ≡ r mod p}, define N = #{(a,b,c,d): a^2+b^5 ≡ c^2+d^5}. Reading N value-by-value gives N = Σ_r s_r^2, converting 'every r is represented (s_r>0)' into a statement about an energy sum that can be squeezed between an upper and lower bound.

technique: To count solutions of a difference of two squares equal to a fixed field element, factor it as (a-c)(a+c) so that fixing the value of one factor determines the other, hence the whole pair.
how_used: Bounding N: fix (b,d); then a,c must satisfy (a-c)(a+c) = d^5 - b^5. When d^5 ≠ b^5 each choice of a-c forces a+c (and thus a,c), giving exactly p-1 pairs; when d^5 = b^5 there are 2p-1 pairs (a≡±c). Combined with the bound ≤5p-4 on pairs with b^5≡d^5, this yields N ≤ p(p^2+4p-4).

technique: Exploit a multiplicative scaling automorphism of the quadratic-plus-power form: rescaling each variable by the appropriate power of a tenth-power unit maps a non-representable residue class to its entire coset of tenth powers, manufacturing many empty classes from one.
how_used: If S_r = ∅, then for t = z^{10} the substitution (a,b) → (z^{-5}a, z^{-2}b) shows S_{tr} = ∅, since (z^{-5}a)^2 + (z^{-2}b)^5 = t^{-1}(a^2+b^5). The set of nonzero tenth powers has size ≥ (p-1)/10 ≥ 4 (as p>100), so at least 4 of the s_r vanish; with ≤ p-4 nonzero terms summing to p^2, Cauchy-Schwarz forces N ≥ p^4/(p-4) > p(p^2+4p-4), contradicting the upper bound.

technique: Identify a mod-2 invariant by checking that every allowed move changes the coordinate sum by an even amount
how_used: Each type-A or type-B step sends (x,y) to (x±a,y±a) or (x±b,y±b), changing x+y by ±2a, ±2b, or 0 — all even. Since the pawn starts at (0,0) with x+y=0, x+y stays even forever, so every target with x+y odd is unreachable, pinning the answer to {x+y even}.

technique: Honor a forced alternation between two operation types by padding the currently-inactive type with sign-canceling move pairs that net to zero displacement
how_used: From gcd(a,b)=1 take Bezout m·a+n·b=1, so 2ma+2nb=2. To advance x by 2 while the alternation A,B,A,B,... must be respected, run 2m A-steps that net (+2ma,0) interleaved with 2m B-steps split as m of (+b,+b) and m of (-b,-b) which cancel to zero; then run a block where the A-steps cancel and B-steps net (-2|n|b,0). The off-type still 'fires' on schedule but contributes nothing, yielding clean (±2,0) and (0,±2) generators that compose to reach all even-even points.

technique: Reach the remaining residue class by appending one move of odd step-size, available because coprimality forces at least one step length odd
how_used: Even-even points are reachable; an odd-odd point (still even sum) is reached by going to a nearby even-even point and adding a parity-flipping step. Since gcd(a,b)=1, a or b is odd: if a is odd, reach (x-a,y-a) (even-even) ending on a B-step, then append one A-step; if a is even (so b odd), reach (x-b,y-b) ending on B and append the trio A,B,A through (x-b-a,y-b-a)→(x-a,y-a)→(x,y), preserving start-with-A alternation.

technique: Take the discrete difference of the same expression evaluated at consecutive values of the running parameter, so any common divisor of all instances must divide the simpler difference.
how_used: Subtracting S(a)=a^n+(a+1)^n+(a+2)^n from S(a+1) leaves (a+3)^n - a^n; hence any prime p dividing D_n divides (a+3)^n - a^n for every positive integer a, converting the 3-term gcd into a clean 2-term constraint.

technique: Specialize the free variable to the prime p itself so that the p-multiple term vanishes mod p, collapsing the divisibility condition to a constraint on the constant shift.
how_used: Setting a=p in p | (a+3)^n - a^n gives (p+3)^n - p^n ≡ 3^n mod p, forcing p | 3, so the only possible prime factor of D_n is 3 and D_n = 3^k with k≥0.

technique: Compute an exact p-adic valuation of an exponentially-indexed family by hand-rolled LTE: replace the exponent by p times itself, binomial-expand the p-th power, and show the valuation rises by exactly one at each step.
how_used: With n=3^{k-1}, write 1+2^n = 3^m c (3∤c); then 1+2^{3n}=(3^m c -1)^3+1 ≡ 3^{m+1} c mod 3^{m+2}, so v_3 rises by exactly one and v_3(1+2^n)=k, giving 3^k || 1^n+2^n+3^n. The identical cubing step (a+3)^n=a^n+3^m c ⇒ (a+3)^{3n}-a^{3n} divisible by 3^{m+1} lifts 3^k | (a+3)^n - a^n for all a; feeding both into the crux-0 telescoping pins D_n = 3^k exactly.

technique: Reduce both sides modulo increasing powers of 2 to force the exponents' parities and strip factors of 2.
how_used: Since 7^k-3^n is even, k^4+n^2 is even so k,n share parity; if both odd then k^4+n^2 is 2 mod 4 while 7^k-3^n is 0 mod 4, a contradiction, so both are even. Writing k=2a, n=2b gives 49^a-9^b which is 0 mod 8, forcing 16a^4+4b^2 to be 0 mod 8, hence b even (b=2c).

technique: Factor a difference of equal-exponent powers as (sum)(difference) and discard the small factor (bounded below by a constant) to keep only the dominant factor as the divisor.
how_used: 7^{2a}-9^{2c}=(7^a+9^c)(7^a-9^c); since 7^a is never equal to 9^c and both are odd, |7^a-9^c| is at least 2, so from 7^{2a}-9^{2c} | 16(a^4+c^2) one extracts 7^a+9^c | 8(a^4+c^2).

technique: Cap the variables by proving the exponential divisor eventually exceeds the polynomial dividend (by induction), so |D| <= |N| can hold only in a finite range, then check it by hand.
how_used: Inductively prove 7^a>8a^4 for a>=4 and 9^c>8c^2, giving 7^a+9^c>8(a^4+c^2) once a>=4, which kills divisibility there; remaining cases a in {1,2,3} are checked finitely (using 9^c>305+8c^2 for c>=3), yielding only (k,n)=(2,4).

technique: Encode a prescribed pairwise-coprimality pattern by assigning a distinct prime to each element of a ground set and defining each term as the product of primes over a chosen finite subset, so that gcd-coprimality of two terms becomes disjointness of their subsets.
how_used: Take distinct primes p_1,p_2,... indexed by positive integers and set a_n = prod_{i in I_n} p_i. Then gcd(a_m,a_n)=1 iff I_m and I_n are disjoint, reducing the 'coprime iff |m-n|=1' requirement to finding finite nonempty sets I_n that are disjoint iff |m-n|=1.

technique: Realize a 'subsets disjoint iff their indices are adjacent' set system by placing each ground element into an arithmetic progression of indices with common difference 2, parity-shifted, so every non-adjacent index pair is forced to share an element while adjacent indices never share one and each set stays finite.
how_used: Element 2n is put into I_k for k = n, n+3, n+5, n+7, ... and element 2n-1 into I_k for k = n, n+2, n+4, ...; thus p_{2n} links I_n to all I_{n+2i} and p_{2n-1} links I_n to all I_{n+2i+1}, no index lands in two consecutive sets, and I_k contains no number exceeding 2k so it is finite.

technique: Represent any value built from +, -, ×, ÷ applied to distinct atoms, each used exactly once, as a ratio of two multilinear (squarefree) polynomials in the atoms whose monomial coefficients all lie in {0, ±1}, proved by induction on the operation tree.
how_used: Each of the finitely many ways to combine x_1,...,x_n (with x_k = k+k offset) via the elementary operations yields P(x)/Q(x) where P = sum a_α x_1^{α_1}...x_n^{α_n} and Q similarly, with a_α, b_α in {0,±1}, a_{0...0}=b_{1...1}=0, and a_α·b_α=0 termwise. Induction step merges u=P1/Q1 (atoms S1) and v=P2/Q2 (atoms S2), S1∩S2=∅: +→(P1Q2+P2Q1)/(Q1Q2), ×→P1P2/(Q1Q2), ÷→P1Q2/(Q1P2); disjoint variables keep products multilinear with ±1 coeffs, and monomial-disjointness of P_i,Q_i prevents any coefficient from exceeding ±1 in the '+' numerator. This normal form is what makes the later coefficient and degree bounds available.

technique: Bound a signed sum of squarefree monomials in the atoms by the product over atoms of (1 + |atom|), since every coefficient has absolute value at most 1 and the constant monomial is absent.
how_used: Setting x_k = c+k with c an integer in {-n,...,-1}, the numerator satisfies |P(c)| ≤ ∏_{k=1}^n (1+|c+k|) - 1. Writing c=−m (1≤m≤n), the factors 1+|k−m| are {m,...,1} times {2,...,n−m+1}, giving product m!·(n−m+1)! = (-c)!·(n+c+1)! ≤ n!, so |P(c)| ≤ n! - 1 < n!, an integer.

technique: Force a contradiction by exhibiting a quantity that must simultaneously be a nonzero integer strictly smaller in absolute value than a modulus N and an integer multiple of N.
how_used: Infinitely many valid k would make one fixed construction's value equal n! identically (pigeonhole over finitely many ways from crux 0), i.e. P = n!·Q as polynomials; since b_{1...1}=0 gives deg Q < n, also deg P < n, so the nonzero P cannot vanish at all of c=-1,...,-n, giving some c with P(c)≠0; then |P(c)| is a positive integer < n! by the product bound, yet equals n!·|Q(c)|, a multiple of n! — impossible.

technique: Decompose the first-hitting-time of an iterated map modulo N as the lcm, over the prime-power factors of N, of the first-hitting-times modulo each prime power.
how_used: Defining zord(P mod N) = least e>0 with P^e(0)=0 mod N, the identity zord(P mod N)=lcm over prime powers q|N of zord(P mod q) (via CRT) reduces both the existence question and the necessity bound to the single-prime-power case, and lets the construction assemble a target m as an lcm of per-prime-power return times.

technique: To force an iterated polynomial map to have a prescribed first-return-to-0 length at a prime-power modulus, construct the map by Lagrange interpolation carried out over Z/p^e rather than over the field Z/p.
how_used: For 1<=k

technique: Bound how much an iteration's first-return-to-0 time can grow when the modulus is lifted from p^{e-1} to p^e by counting the residues mod p^e that reduce to 0 mod p^{e-1}.
how_used: Setting k=zord(P mod p^{e-1}) and Q=P^k gives zord(P mod p^e)=k*zord(Q mod p^e) (orbit must be periodic mod p^{e-1} first); since Q(0)=0 mod p^{e-1}, all Q-iterates are multiples of p^{e-1}, of which only p exist mod p^e, so zord(Q mod p^e)<=p, forcing every prime factor of zord(P mod p^e) to be <=p and yielding the rad(n) necessity condition.

technique: Normalize the unknown function at factorial arguments by dividing its value by the argument, producing a real-valued sequence whose limit is the hidden parameter.
how_used: Set a_n = f(n!)/n!; applying the equation at (m,n)=(n!, n+1) forces a_{n+1} to lie in [a_n, a_n + 1/n!), so a_n is non-decreasing and bounded above by a_1 + e, hence converges to a real number alpha that parametrizes f.

technique: Evaluate the unknown at an arbitrary argument m by choosing the multiplier so the product mn lands on a controlled value (a factorial divisible by m), then read f(m) straight off the functional equation.
how_used: For each m pick ell with m | ell!, set mn = ell!, so f(m) = floor(f(ell!) / (ell!/m)) = floor(alpha m), recovering f from the limit alpha at every m.

technique: Resolve the rounding ambiguity by splitting on whether the limit is attained, and in the strict-approach case choose the approximation error finer than the target's fractional part to fix the floor-versus-ceiling direction.
how_used: When a_k < alpha for all k, write a_ell = alpha - x and get f(m) = floor(alpha m - m x); choosing ell so that m x < {alpha m} (or m x < 1 when alpha m is an integer) pins f(m) = ceil(alpha m) - 1, yielding the second solution family alongside floor(alpha m).

technique: Rewrite a divisibility edge condition as a multiplicative fixed-point congruence: read d | x(y-1) as x ≡ xy (mod d), so the relation says 'right-multiplying the source by the target leaves the source unchanged mod n'.
how_used: The edge rule n | a(b-1) is rewritten as a ≡ ab (mod n). Applied to a cycle edge i_k -> i_{k+1} it gives i_k ≡ i_k i_{k+1} (mod n), turning the graph condition into an absorption identity that drives the contradiction in solutions 3 and 5.

technique: Telescope a cyclic chain of absorption congruences x ≡ xy around the whole cycle so every vertex label is congruent to the single full product of all labels; cyclic symmetry then forces all labels equal.
how_used: From i_1 ≡ i_1 i_2 ≡ i_1 i_2 i_3 ≡ ... ≡ i_1 i_2 ... i_r (mod n), and the identical chain started at each i_k, every i_k ≡ i_1 i_2 ... i_r (mod n); since the labels lie in {1,...,n} this means i_1 = ... = i_r, contradicting a genuine cycle.

technique: Show the adjacency relation is transitive via an explicit algebraic combination of the two edge relations, so any directed cycle would force a length-2 cycle, which is then ruled out directly.
how_used: From ab-a ≡ 0 and bc-b ≡ 0 (mod n), the identity ac-a = a(bc-b) + (ab-a) - (ab-a)c gives ac-a ≡ 0, so a->b->c implies a->c; hence a minimal cycle has length 2, but a 2-cycle a<->b forces a-b ≡ 0 (mod n), i.e. a=b, impossible.

technique: Encode a multiplicative property as a completely additive Z/2-valued parity function so that products become sums mod 2.
how_used: 'Balanced' = even number of prime factors, so define f(n)=Omega(n) mod 2 (parity of prime-factor count); then f(nm)=f(n)+f(m), and f(P(k))=f(a+k)+f(b+k) captures balancedness of (x+a)(x+b), reducing 'P(k) balanced' to 'f(a+k)=f(b+k)'.

technique: Pigeonhole on length-L windows of a {0,1}-sequence taken over all starting positions to force two equal windows.
how_used: The window (f(n+1),...,f(n+50)) has only 2^50 possible values, so two distinct shifts a,b give identical windows; matching f(a+k)=f(b+k) for k=1..50 makes every P(k)=(a+k)(b+k) have even parity hence balanced, and a not equal to b is automatic.

technique: Substitute the variable so the two linear factors become consecutive multiples of their difference, turning the product into (square)*(consecutive integers).
how_used: Take n=k(b-a)-a so x+a=k(b-a) and x+b=(k+1)(b-a), giving P(n)=k(k+1)(b-a)^2; balancedness then forces f(k)=f(k+1) for all large k, contradicting f(prime)=1 vs f(square)=0, so a=b.

technique: When every value's prime factors lie in a fixed finite set, choose the divisor argument divisible by each such prime to a power exceeding the valuation cap of both values, so the difference cannot attain that valuation unless the two values coincide.
how_used: Assuming only p_1,...,p_m divide all values of f, pick a divisible by p_i to a power above v_{p_i}(f(1)); since a | f(a+1)-f(1), each v_{p_i}(f(a+1)-f(1)) = min(v_{p_i}(f(a+1)),v_{p_i}(f(1))) would be <= v_{p_i}(f(1)) < v_{p_i}(a), a contradiction unless f(a+1)=f(1). (Sol 2 does the same with M chosen so p_i^{alpha_i+1} | M-1, pinning f(M)=f(1).)

technique: A fixed integer that is divisible by arbitrarily large integers must be zero, used to upgrade a relation that holds on an infinite set to a forced equality.
how_used: Having shown f(a+1)=f(1) for infinitely many a, the divisibility (a+1-b) | (f(1)-f(b)) holds with divisor a+1-b unbounded, so the fixed integer f(1)-f(b) must vanish, giving f(b)=f(1) for every b and contradicting non-constancy. (Sol 2 instead bounds M-r > |f(M)-f(r)| with M-r | f(M)-f(r) to force f(r)=f(1).)

technique: Use that an odd square plus one is congruent to 2 mod 4, hence cannot be divisible by 4 nor be a product of two even factors, to force the parity of every term in a divisibility chain
how_used: From a_2^2+1=(a_1+1)(a_3+1) etc., if any a_i were odd then a_i^2+1 ≡ 2 mod 4 could not equal a product of two even numbers a_{i-1}+1, a_{i+1}+1; chasing this forces a_1,...,a_5 all even, reducing the configuration to the symmetric pair conditions (x+1)|(y^2+1) and (y+1)|(x^2+1) with x=a_2, y=a_3 even

technique: Turn a pair of reciprocal divisibility conditions into one homogeneous equation by proving the two divisor-sides are coprime (their gcd divides a small constant), so a single integer multiplier k clears the divisibility into an equality
how_used: Adding gives (x+1)|(x^2+y^2) and (y+1)|(x^2+y^2); any common divisor d of x+1,y+1 divides (x^2+1)+(y^2+1)-(x^2+y^2)=2, and both being odd forces d=1, so x+1,y+1 coprime and there is k with k(x+1)(y+1)=x^2+y^2

technique: Apply Vieta jumping: fix the larger variable as a root of the quadratic the symmetric equation defines, take the conjugate root via the sum/product relations, and show it gives a smaller positive solution to descend against a minimal-sum choice
how_used: Reading k(x+1)(y+1)=x^2+y^2 as x^2-k(y_1+1)x+(y_1^2-k(y_1+1))=0, the second root x_2 satisfies x_1+x_2=k(y_1+1), x_1x_2=y_1^2-k(y_1+1); (x_1+1)(x_2+1)=y_1^2+1 odd forces x_2 even positive with x_2+1=(y_1^2+1)/(x_1+1) ≤ y_1, so (y_1,x_2) is a smaller even solution, contradicting minimality and proving no n≥5 works

technique: To show a point fixed by an iterate T^n has minimal period dividing n, run a Bezout combination of the two exponents: with d=gcd(k,n) write rk-sn=d and compose the fixed-point relations to get T^d(x)=x, then invoke minimality.
how_used: x lies in B(k) (minimal period k) and in A(n) (T^n(x)=x). With d=gcd(k,n), pick positive r,s with rk-sn=d, so x=T^{rk}(x)=T^{sn+d}(x)=T^d(T^{sn}(x))=T^d(x); minimality of k forces d=k, i.e. k|n. This yields the disjoint decomposition A(n)=⊔_{d|n}B(d) and |A(n)|=Σ_{d|n}|B(d)|.

technique: When a map acts on a finite set so that every element has the same exact period n, observe it splits the set into disjoint n-cycles, so the set size is divisible by n.
how_used: For x in B(n) (exact period n), the n iterates x,T(x),...,T^{n-1}(x) are shown distinct and all in B(n) (any shorter return would contradict minimality of n). Hence T partitions B(n) into length-n cycles, giving n | |B(n)| — the key arithmetic constraint fed into the contradiction.

technique: Evaluate a multiplicative divisor-sum identity at a squarefree argument and reduce modulo one of its prime factors q, so the divisibility q | (term) annihilates every summand whose index is a multiple of q, collapsing the sum to its few q-free terms.
how_used: From P(n)=Σ_{d|n}|B(d)| with q | |B(d)| whenever q|d, taking n=pq gives P(pq) ≡ |B(1)|+|B(p)| (mod q); since P(0)≡P(pq) (mod q) too, P(0)-|B(1)|-|B(p)| is divisible by arbitrarily large primes q, forcing P(0)=|B(1)|+|B(p)|=P(p) for every prime p, so P agrees with the constant P(0) at infinitely many points — contradiction since P is non-constant.

technique: Find a polynomial particular solution to a divided recurrence by fixing its coefficients top-down through coefficient comparison.
how_used: Rewrite the recursion as n a_n = a_{n-1} + n^k and seek P in Z[x] of degree k-1 with x P(x) = x^k + P(x-1) + q_k; set the leading coefficient b_{k-1}=1, then for m = k-1,...,1 compare coefficients of x^m to express each b_{m-1} as an integer linear combination of the already-known higher coefficients, leaving a single integer constant q_k = -P(-1).

technique: Force an unknown integer constant to vanish by showing an integer-valued sequence tends to zero and is therefore eventually zero.
how_used: Setting c_n = a_n - P(n) reduces the recurrence to q + n c_n = c_{n-1}, which iterates to the closed form c_n = c_0/n! - q*(0!+1!+...+(n-1)!)/n!; this expression tends to 0 as n grows, and since each c_n is an integer it must be 0 for large n, and back-substitution forces q = q_k = 0, upgrading the identity to the exact x P(x) = x^k + P(x-1).

technique: Evaluate a polynomial identity over the finite field F_4 and exploit its multiplicative group of order 3.
how_used: Reduce x P(x) = x^k + P(x-1) mod 2 and evaluate at the two non-subfield elements of F_4 = {0,1,alpha,alpha+1}: alpha P(alpha) = alpha^k + P(alpha+1) and (alpha+1)P(alpha+1) = (alpha+1)^k + P(alpha). Using alpha(alpha+1)=1 (so alpha+1 = alpha^2, alpha^3=1) to chain the two equations collapses P(alpha) against itself, leaving (alpha+1)^{k-1} = alpha^k; substituting alpha+1=alpha^2 gives alpha^{2(k-1)} = alpha^k, i.e. 2(k-1) ≡ k (mod 3), hence k ≡ 2 (mod 3).

technique: In arithmetic modulo 2^n - 1, collapse a tower exponent by reducing it modulo n, using that 2^n is congruent to 1.
how_used: Since 2^n \equiv 1 \pmod{2^n-1}, the order of 2 divides n, so 2^{2^n} \equiv 2^{(2^n \bmod n)} \pmod{2^n-1}; at n=25, 2^{25} \bmod 25 = 7, and 0 < 2^7 < 2^{25}-1 makes 2^7 the actual remainder, not just a residue.

technique: Recast a 'is a power of 4' predicate on a power of 2 as a parity condition on its exponent.
how_used: The remainder 2^{(2^n \bmod n)} is a power of 4 exactly when 2^n \bmod n is even, so the assertion fails wherever 2^n \bmod n is odd; n=25 gives 2^{25} \bmod 25 = 7 (odd), so 2^7 = 128 is not a power of 4, a counterexample.

technique: After ordering the variables, sandwich the symmetric sum between its largest term and (number of terms) times that term to pin the opposite side between two multiples of the dominant variable.
how_used: Assuming a>=b>=c gives a^3 < a^3+b^3+c^3 <= 3a^3; setting this equal to (abc)^2 = a^2 b^2 c^2 and cancelling a^2 yields the two-sided bound a < b^2 c^2 <= 3a, so the product b^2 c^2 is squeezed into a narrow band around a (and squaring gives the upper handle 9a^2 >= b^4 c^4 used next).

technique: Isolate the equation so one variable's square is an explicit factor of the remaining terms; since the resulting cofactor is a positive integer it is at least 1, handing a lower bound on those terms.
how_used: Rewrite a^3+b^3+c^3=(abc)^2 as b^3+c^3 = a^2(b^2 c^2 - a), so a^2 | b^3+c^3 with positive cofactor, giving b^3+c^3 >= a^2. Combined with b^3+c^3 <= 2b^3 and the squared band 9a^2 >= b^4 c^4, this chains to 18b^3 >= b^4 c^4 >= b^3 c^5, i.e. c^5 <= 18, forcing c=1; afterward a^2 | b^3+1 leaves only finitely many b to check, yielding (3,2,1) up to permutation.

technique: To force a target modulus to divide a difference of two equal-base powers, pick the exponents via Euler's theorem so the difference is a guaranteed multiple of the modulus.
how_used: To show the seed family {2^a-2^b} already contains a multiple of every positive n, write n=2^alpha*t with t odd; Euler gives t | 2^{phi(t)}-1, hence n=2^alpha*t divides 2^{alpha+phi(t)+1}-2^{alpha+1}, a number of the required form 2^a-2^b that thus lies in S.

technique: Make a chosen integer an explicit polynomial root by expanding a known multiple of it in that integer's own base, so the base digits become coefficients already known to lie in the set.
how_used: With 0..n-1 already in S (induction hypothesis) and N a multiple of n in S, the base-n expansion N=sum a_i n^i has every digit 0<=a_i<n in S and a_0=0; rearranging gives a_k x^k+...+a_1 x - N evaluated at x=n equal to 0, so n is a root of a polynomial with coefficients in S, forcing n into S.

technique: Produce a vanishing integer combination of fixed terms by a counting/pigeonhole bound: show the number of admissible exponent choices outgrows the range of attainable values, forcing two configurations to coincide.
how_used: For fixed k>2 and degree n, count tuples (a_i) with each 2^{a_i}k^i at most 2k^n: there are at least n!(log_2 k)^n of the sums 2^{a_n}k^n+...+2^{a_0}, but each value is at most 2k^n(n+1); for large n the count exceeds the range, so two sums are equal and their term-by-term difference yields coefficients 2^{a_i}-2^{b_i} making k a root.

technique: Pick the free variable so the divisor of a constraint becomes an exact multiple of a chosen modulus, then read the constraint modulo that modulus.
how_used: In a+f(b) | a^2+b f(a), set a = nb - f(b) with n large enough that a >= C, so the divisor a+f(b) = nb is a multiple of b. Then nb | a^2 + b f(a); since b | nb and b | b f(a), reducing gives b | a^2 = (nb-f(b))^2 ≡ f(b)^2 (mod b), i.e. b | f(b)^2 for every b.

technique: When a quotient is integer-valued and bounded above by a constant, pigeonhole a single value that is attained by infinitely many inputs.
how_used: From b | f(b)^2 one gets p | f(p) for every prime p, so f(p) = k(p)·p. The easy upper bound f(p) <= f(1)·p forces k(p) <= f(1), a bounded integer, so some fixed k is attained by infinitely many primes p — the infinite supply of such primes is what powers the finish.

technique: Substitute a prime for the free parameter so a common prime factor can be cancelled out of one side of a divisibility, leaving a fixed expression divisible by an unbounded modulus.
how_used: Put b = p (a large prime with k(p)=k) in a+f(b) | a^2 + b f(a): a+kp | a^2 + p f(a); subtracting a(a+kp) gives a+kp | p(f(a)-ka). Since gcd(a+kp, p)=1 for large p, a+kp | f(a)-ka; as p grows the divisor exceeds the fixed |f(a)-ka|, forcing f(a) = ka for all a.

technique: To plant a prime p into a binomial written as a falling factorial over b!, pick the argument's residue modulo a prime power so that one falling-factorial term absorbs more powers of p than the denominator carries.
how_used: For a prime p<=b with p not dividing a, set t=v_p(b!) and use p not dividing a to solve a c == 1 (mod p^{t+1}); taking n=(p-1)c gives an == p-1 (mod p^{t+1}), so the numerator term an-p+1 is divisible by p^{t+1} while v_p(b!)=t, hence v_p of the numerator exceeds the denominator and p divides C(an,b); since p | an+1 this kills a-goodness, forcing p | a for every prime p<=b.

technique: Evaluate a length-b falling product modulo m=an+1 by reducing each factor an-k to -(k+1), collapsing the product to (-1)^b b! and exposing a parity obstruction.
how_used: When every prime <=b divides a, b! is invertible mod an+1, so a-goodness reduces to an(an-1)...(an-b+1) == b! (mod an+1); since an == -1, the left side equals (-1)^b b!, which forces b even (for b odd, picking an+1 > 2 b! makes (-1)^b b! not congruent to b!), giving the 'b even' half of the characterization.
technique_tags: reduce factors to negatives mod m

technique: Force nth-power residues into the two-element set {0,1} modulo a prime power by making the exponent a multiple of the group order phi(p^e).
how_used: Impose condition (dagger): n divisible by phi(p^e) for every prime power p^e exactly dividing N. Then by Euler's theorem each nth power is congruent to 0 or 1 modulo p^e, so the only freedom in a sum mod p^e is how many summands are units.

technique: Cap the number of attainable residue classes by multiplying per-modulus options via CRT, using pairwise coprimality to allow at most one summand divisible by a given prime.
how_used: A sum of m pairwise coprime nth powers is congruent to m or m-1 modulo each p^e (at most one summand is divisible by p), so CRT gives at most 2^k*m residues mod N, where k is the number of prime factors of N; choosing N > 2^k*m leaves infinitely many residues, hence infinitely many integers, non-representable.

technique: Produce primes congruent to 1 modulo a high power of 2, of controlled size, by taking prime factors of Fermat numbers 2^{2^k}+1.
how_used: Pick p | 2^{2^{t-1}}+1 and q | 2^{2^t}+1, so 2^t | p-1 and 2^{t+1} | q-1; set N=pq and n=(p-1)(q-1)/2^t, satisfying (dagger). Since these prime factors are roughly 2^{2^t}, log_2 n < 2^{t+1} < 2N/n, yielding N > (1/8) n log_2 n and giving the required c n log n bound.

technique: Eliminate a ceiling/floor in a "this value is a perfect square" hypothesis by sandwiching the square between consecutive integer estimates, converting it into a single remainder constrained to a fixed bounded range.
how_used: From assuming a²+⌈4a²/b⌉=c² one gets c²−1 < a²+4a²/b ≤ c², hence the integer d := c²b − a²(b+4) satisfies 0 ≤ d < b — a fixed constant that absorbs the ceiling and turns the statement into a clean quadratic Diophantine condition.

technique: Pass to sum-and-difference variables x=c+a, y=c−a to recast a quadratic relation between two unknowns into a symmetric two-variable Diophantine form set up for Vieta jumping.
how_used: Substituting c=(x+y)/2, a=(x−y)/2 into c²b−a²(b+4)=d turns it into the symmetric equation x²−(b+2)xy+y²+d=0 with 0≤d<b, whose symmetry in x,y enables the Vieta descent.

technique: From a minimal positive solution of a symmetric quadratic, replace one variable by its Vieta conjugate second root and prove the conjugate is strictly smaller by factoring (x−1)(z−1)=xz−(x+z)+1 and bounding it below (x−1)², contradicting minimality (infinite descent).
how_used: Treating x²−(b+2)xy+y²+d=0 as a quadratic in x with second root z gives z=(b+2)y−x=(y²+d)/x (integer, positive); then (x−1)(z−1)=(y²+d)−(b+2)y+1 < (y−1)²−b(y−1) ≤ (x−1)² forces z<x, so z+y<x+y contradicting the minimal x+y.

technique: Factor the maximal power of 2 out of the side of a two-power-vs-factorial equation and equate 2-adic valuations of both sides.
how_used: Rewrite 2^p = 2^{q-2} + q! as q! = 2^{q-2}(2^{p-q+2}-1); since p-q+2>0 the cofactor 2^{p-q+2}-1 is an odd integer >1, so the RHS has exactly q-2 factors of 2, forcing nu_2(q!) = q-2.

technique: Apply Legendre's formula nu_2(q!) = q - s_2(q) so a valuation equation becomes a constraint on the number of binary 1-digits of q.
how_used: nu_2(q!)=q-2 gives s_2(q)=2; since q is an odd prime its lowest bit is 1 and the only other 1-bit is 2^n, so q = 2^n + 1. (Equivalently, bounding the floor sum by (q-1)(1-1/2^n) <= q-2 with equality iff q=2^n+1.)

technique: Reduce the equation modulo a small prime dividing the factorial term and use the multiplicative order of 2 there to force a divisibility on the unknown exponent.
how_used: For q>=7, 7 | q! gives 2^{p-q+2} ≡ 1 (mod 7); ord_7(2)=3 forces 3 | (p-q+2). With q=2^n+1 ≡ -1 (mod 3) for prime q>3, this yields p ≡ q-2 ≡ 0 (mod 3), so p=3, impossible for q>=7; the small cases q=2,3,5 are checked directly to give (3,3) and (7,5).

technique: Among p consecutive integers in a product, isolate the unique multiple of the prime p and factor it out to cancel the single power of p in the factorial denominator.
how_used: In the numerator n(n-1)...(n-6) of C(n,7), writing n=7k+ell singles out the factor (n-ell)=7k as the one divisible by 7; pulling out 7k cancels the 7 in 7! and leaves k times the reduced fraction, exposing the target residue k=floor(n/7).

technique: Recognize that the leftover factors form a complete set of nonzero residues mod p matching the reduced factorial denominator, so they cancel to 1 as units mod p.
how_used: After removing (n-ell), the six remaining numerator factors are congruent to 1,2,3,4,5,6 mod 7 in some order, exactly the residues in the denominator 6!, which is invertible mod the prime 7; clearing the denominator (6!*C(n,7)=k*P) and cancelling gives C(n,7)≡k mod 7.

technique: Assign each board position the squarefree part of its number and use it as a one-sided monovariant for a game.
how_used: S(n) is defined as the product of primes with odd exponent (S(0)=0). Amy's n -> n^k cannot increase S since S(n^k)=S(n) for odd k and =1 for even k, so the squarefree part is bounded below by 0 and controlled across all of Amy's moves, making it the quantity to drive to 0.

technique: Subtract the largest perfect square dividing the current number to strictly lower its squarefree part by one.
how_used: Writing n = S(n)*b^2 with b^2 the maximal square factor, Bob plays n - b^2 = (S(n)-1)*b^2, which has squarefree part at most S(n)-1; iterating forces the squarefree part down to 0, i.e. the number to 0, in at most S(N) moves.

technique: Read a monic integer quadratic through Vieta: the two roots multiply to the constant term, so a root must be a divisor of it; replace the root by its complementary divisor to recast the sum-of-roots relation as an equation in the divisor exponents.
how_used: Viewing the equation as a quadratic in m, the product of its two roots is the constant term 2*3^n, forcing m to be 3^p or 2*3^q. Pairing m with its cofactor 2*3^n/m and using sum-of-roots = 2^{n+1}-1 turns the problem into solving 3^p + 2*3^q = 2^{n+1}-1 with p+q=n.

technique: Bound each exponent in a sum of prime-power terms equal to a power of 2 by sandwiching that power of 2 between consecutive powers of 3 (using 8 < 9), then combine with the fixed exponent sum to force both exponents near a common value.
how_used: From 3^p < 2^{n+1} = 8^{(n+1)/3} < 9^{(n+1)/3} = 3^{2(n+1)/3} (and similarly for 2*3^q), get p,q < 2(n+1)/3; with p+q=n this yields (n-2)/3 < p,q, so h=min(p,q) > (n-2)/3, in particular h > 1.
