technique: Turn an integer lower bound into an exact equality by writing each contributing term as ±(a quantity strictly less than 1/2), forcing a unique sign pattern and the minimal value.
how_used: Using floor(x+1/2)=x±||x||, the positive integer d_k expands as ±||(a+b)/p^k||±||a/p^k||±||b/p^k|| (the linear parts cancel). Because p^k is odd, each ||·|| has odd denominator hence is <1/2, so this signed three-term combination lies in (-3/2,3/2); an integer d_k≥1 then forces all three signs positive and d_k=1, giving ||a/p^k||+||b/p^k||+||(a+b)/p^k||=1.

technique: Index by the inverse map: replace 'does a pair of indices m<n with residues in a value range exist' by studying the function sending each residue value to the index whose multiple realizes it.
how_used: Define f(x) on x in {1,...,s-1} as the unique k in {1,...,p-1} with s*k = x mod p (so the residues below s are exactly {1,...,s-1}). The wanted m<n with {sm/p}<{sn/p}<s/p exist iff some values x<y have f(x)<f(y); hence they FAIL exactly when the preimage sequence is strictly decreasing: f(s-1)<f(s-2)<...<f(1).

technique: Express modular preimages in closed form as an arithmetic progression by writing the inverse index linearly through the negative inverse of the multiplier.
how_used: Pick t = -s^{-1} mod p with 0<t<p; then f(x)=1+(s-x)t mod p, verified since s*f(x)=s+(s-x)(st) = s-(s-x) = x mod p. The decreasing chain f(s-1)<...<f(1) thus becomes (1+t)<(1+2t)<...<(1+(s-1)t) mod p, i.e. the progression with step t never wraps around modulo p.

technique: When an arithmetic progression never wraps around mod p, deduce every term lies below p and use the index whose term is congruent to 0 to force an exact integer equation.
how_used: Since 1+kt increases by t and never wraps for k=1,...,s-1, all 1+kt<p; because 1+st = 0 mod p with 0<1+st<2p, this forces 1+st=p, i.e. st=p-1, so s | p-1. Thus the pair fails to exist precisely when s divides p-1, giving the iff (converse: s|p-1 gives t=(p-1)/s making the AP strictly increasing below p).

technique: Recast a 'greatest prime divisor stays bounded above by a linear function of the index' condition into the existence, for each constant, of a prime divisor whose modular root is small relative to (p-1)/2.
how_used: p(f(n^2)) - 2n bounded above is negated by finding, for every c, a prime p and n with p | f(n^2) and n <= (p-1)/2 - c (so p >= 2n + 2c+1, making p(f(n^2)) - 2n >= 2c+1 unbounded). The whole irreducible-nonconstant case reduces to producing such (p,n) pairs.

technique: Invoke Schur's theorem that a nonconstant integer polynomial has infinitely many prime divisors, then fold each root into [0,(p-1)/2] using x^2 = (-x)^2 mod p.
how_used: Schur supplies infinitely many odd primes p > c dividing some f(n^2); since n^2 = (-n)^2 mod p, each such p has a root n lying in 0..(p-1)/2, which is the candidate witness for the reduction's inequality.

technique: Reflect a modular root sitting near (p-1)/2 to a fixed half-integer point via n = (p-1)/2 - k giving n = -(k+1/2) mod p, so the failing primes all divide one fixed product of polynomial values.
how_used: A prime fails only if its root n is in ((p-1)/2 - c, (p-1)/2); writing n = (p-1)/2 - k forces f((k+1/2)^2) = 0 mod p for some 0<=k<=c, so failing p divide the fixed product prod_{k=1}^{c} f((k+1/2)^2). That product is a fixed nonzero integer (finitely many prime divisors) unless some f((k+1/2)^2)=0 identically, i.e. 4n-(2k+1)^2 divides f(n), pinning the answer to products of factors 4n-a^2.

technique: To rule out a two-term case where the symmetric functions are prescribed, treat the two unknowns as roots of the monic integer quadratic with those sum/product coefficients and require its discriminant to be a perfect square.
how_used: For k=2, a1+a2=a1a2=n makes a1,a2 roots of x^2-nx+n, so (a1-a2)^2=n^2-4n=(n-2)^2-4 must be a (rational hence integer) perfect square; two squares differing by 4 force n=4, so any solution needs k>2.

technique: When one quantity is simultaneously a sum and a product of k positive terms, feed both into AM-GM to obtain a self-referential inequality in that single quantity and solve it for a lower bound.
how_used: From n/k=(a_1+...+a_k)/k >= (a_1...a_k)^{1/k}=n^{1/k} get n >= k^{k/(k-1)}; since 3^{3/2},4^{4/3}>5 and k^{k/(k-1)}>k>=5 for k>=5, every admissible n exceeds 5, eliminating n=1,2,3,5.

technique: To realize a target sum and product simultaneously, build a short base tuple whose product already equals the target, then append copies of 1 (which leave the product fixed) to top the sum up to the target.
how_used: For even n>=6 use base (n/2,2) (product n) padded with n/2-2 ones; for odd n>=9 use base (n/2,1/2,4) (product n) padded with (n-9)/2 ones; the exceptional n=7 uses the fraction triple (4/3,7/6,9/2) with product and sum both 7.

technique: Detect a squared irreducible factor of a polynomial by checking that a root of that factor is also a root of the derivative.
how_used: To show (x^2+x+1)^2 divides F(x)=(x+1)^p - x^p - 1, take a primitive cube root of unity zeta. Then F(zeta)=(1+zeta)^p - zeta^p - 1 = (-zeta^2)^p - zeta^p - 1; for p == 1 (mod 3) this is -zeta^2 - zeta - 1 = 0, and F'(zeta)=p(zeta+1)^{p-1} - p*zeta^{p-1} = p - p = 0. So zeta (and its conjugate) is a DOUBLE root, hence the minimal polynomial squared (x^2+x+1)^2 divides F. This is the conceptual pivot, foreshadowed by the factorization (x+1)^7-x^7-1 = 7x(x+1)(x^2+x+1)^2.

technique: Prove a number-theoretic divisibility by first establishing it as a polynomial identity in two variables and only then specializing the variables to the chosen integers.
how_used: Rather than attack p^5 | (a+b)^p - a^p - b^p directly, prove the stronger statement p*(x^2+xy+y^2)^2 divides (x+y)^p - x^p - y^p in Z[x,y] (the global factor p coming from every binomial coefficient C(p,k), 0<k<p, being divisible by p), then specialize x=a, y=b. The exponent bookkeeping p^5 = p * (p^2)^2 splits cleanly: the leading p from the binomial content, and (p^2)^2 from the squared quadratic form evaluated at a point where p^2 | a^2+ab+b^2.

technique: Produce a bounded pair satisfying a quadratic-form congruence by solving the form's defining congruence to the required prime power and using Thue's lemma to shrink the solution into range.
how_used: Need 0

technique: Partition the set by residue mod a forbidden modulus and flag the classes that contain only finitely many members, so that any member of an unflagged class can be swapped for another set-member in the same class.
how_used: Assuming a prime p is not in M, call a prime q in M 'sparse' if only finitely many elements of M are congruent to q mod p; there are only finitely many sparse primes. This dichotomy means a non-sparse prime factor can always be replaced by another prime of M in its residue class mod p, keeping a product squarefree and inside M while leaving its value mod p unchanged.

technique: Premultiply the sliding term by the product of every 'excluded' prime so that the +1 expression is automatically coprime to all of them, forcing its prime factors into the freely-substitutable class.
how_used: Let C be the product of all sparse primes (p does not divide C). Since every sparse prime divides C, no sparse prime divides C*a_k+1, so all its prime factors are in M (by the hypothesis applied to the squarefree product C*a_k) and are non-sparse. Replacing each such factor by a class-representative yields a_{k+1}, a squarefree product of M-primes with a_{k+1} = C*a_k + 1 (mod p), setting up the affine recurrence.

technique: Read off the affine recurrence's closed form as a geometric sum mod p and invoke Fermat's little theorem to pick an index that forces the value to 0.
how_used: From a_0=1 and a_{k+1} = C*a_k+1 (mod p) one gets a_k = C^k+C^{k-1}+...+1 (mod p). Since C is not 0 mod p, choosing k=p-1 when C=1 (sum of p ones = 0) or k=p-2 otherwise drives this geometric sum to 0 mod p, contradicting that a_k is a product of primes in M, none of which equals p, so a_k cannot be divisible by p.

technique: Reduce an 'infinitely many' claim to producing one seed by exhibiting a fixed self-map that sends any valid instance to a strictly larger valid instance.
how_used: Djukic's argument: the doubling map n -> N = 2^n+1 (with N>n and N odd) preserves BOTH required properties, so one explicit seed n=57 (=3*19, for which 2^57+1 is not divisible by 57 while 57 | 2^(2^57+1)+1) generates an infinite strictly increasing chain. The two preservation checks themselves are carried out with the divisibility criterion (separate crux).

technique: Turn a divisibility between numbers of shape 2^k+1 into a divisibility between their exponents via the identity gcd(2^M+1, 2^N+1) = 2^gcd(M,N)+1 (valid when M,N have equal 2-adic valuation, e.g. both odd).
how_used: Engine for both preservation checks in the Djukic self-map. Non-divisibility (property B for N): from n not dividing N=2^n+1, gcd(2^n+1,2^N+1)=2^gcd(n,N)+1 is a proper divisor of 2^n+1, so N does not divide 2^N+1. Divisibility (property A for N): property A for n gives n | 2^N+1, and the quotient is odd, so 2^n+1 = N divides 2^(2^N+1)+1. Along the chain every exponent stays odd, so the identity applies unconditionally.

technique: Reduce a symmetric two-variable Diophantine by setting s=x+y and using the power-sum identity x^3+y^3=s(s^2-3xy) to solve for the product xy as a single rational function of the sum s and the parameter.
how_used: From x^3+y^3=p(xy+p), rewrite the left side as s(s^2-3xy) so s(s^2-3xy)=p(p+xy); collecting xy gives s^3-p^2=(3s+p)xy, hence xy=(s^3-p^2)/(3s+p). The pair (x,y) becomes the two roots of t^2-st+xy=0, so both unknowns are encoded by (s,p) with xy determined.

technique: Bound the sum of two positive integers with fixed sum and determined product by imposing the real-roots / AM-GM constraint s^2>=4xy on the associated monic quadratic.
how_used: Substituting the determined product gives s^2>=4xy=4(s^3-p^2)/(3s+p); clearing the positive denominator and factoring yields (s-2p)(s^2+sp+2p^2) <= 4p^2-4p^3 = 4p^2(1-p) < 0 for p>=2. Since the quadratic factor s^2+sp+2p^2 is always positive, this forces s-2p<0, i.e. s<2p. (Note: the constant is 4p^2(1-p), not p^2-p^3; only its negative sign is used.)

technique: Turn an integrality requirement on a rational function of the running parameters into a clean divisor relation by long-dividing out the polynomial part, so the single fixed remainder term's denominator must divide its numerator.
how_used: Multiply xy by 27 and divide: 27xy = (9s^2-3sp+p^2) - p^2(p+27)/(3s+p), so integrality of xy forces 3s+p | p^2(p+27). With p not dividing s (and p!=3), gcd(3s+p,p)=1, stripping p^2 to give 3s+p | p+27. Reducing p ≡ -3s (mod 3s+p) turns the dividend into 27-3s=3(9-s), so 3s+p | 3(9-s); since 3s+p>0, a nonzero 3(9-s) would need |3(9-s)|>=3s+p, forcing s<=4 (ruled out by hand), leaving s=9. Then xy=27-p and t^2-9t+(27-p)=0 gives (1,8,19),(2,7,13),(4,5,7). The routine side-cases p|s (forces s=p, impossible) and p=3 (checked directly, no solutions) complete it.

technique: Encode the coprimality condition geometrically: a coordinate-coprime point is exactly a lattice point lying in none of the sublattices p·Z^2, so existence of one near a target reduces to the bad sublattices failing to cover a disk around the target.
how_used: A pair (m,n) is admissible iff gcd(m,n)=1, i.e. (m,n) is in none of the sublattices p*Z^2 over primes p. To produce such a pair within c*log(x^2+y^2+2) of (x,y), it suffices to show the disk of radius rho = c*log r (with r^2 = x^2+y^2) about (x,y), which contains about pi*rho^2 lattice points, is not entirely covered by the union of these bad sublattices.

technique: Bound the number of points of a sublattice p*Z^2 inside a disk by about (radius/p)^2, then sum the small-prime contributions using convergence of the prime-square series sum_p 1/p^2 to get a constant-fraction cover.
how_used: For primes p <= rho, the points divisible by p in both coordinates number at most (rho/p + 1)^2; summing over these primes gives about pi*rho^2 * sum_p 1/p^2 ~ 0.45*pi*rho^2, a fixed fraction strictly below the total ~ pi*rho^2 lattice points, so small primes alone cannot cover the disk. The boundary O(rho/p) terms sum to O(rho*loglog rho) = o(rho^2).

technique: Bound the number of points of a region divisible by some prime larger than the region's scale by summing, over the integers in one coordinate window, the count of such large prime factors per integer, then calibrate the region's radius so this total stays below the region's area.
how_used: For a prime p > rho a multiple lands in a coordinate window only O(rho/p) times, so disk points divisible by some prime > rho number at most about (window length ~ rho)*(max number of prime factors > rho of an integer ~ log r / log rho) ~ rho * log r / log rho. Requiring this to be below rho^2 forces rho to be of order log r, which is precisely why the radius c*log(x^2+y^2+2) succeeds; large primes then contribute only o(rho^2).

technique: Scale a half-integer-valued recurrence by a constant to make every term an integer, then read off the never-integer hypothesis as a parity condition
how_used: Set b_k=2a_k; the recurrence becomes b_{k+1}=b_k⌊b_k/2⌋, an integer for all k. The original a_k is never an integer exactly when every b_k is odd, which collapses ⌊b_k/2⌋ to (b_k-1)/2 and gives the clean integer recurrence b_{k+1}=b_k(b_k-1)/2.

technique: Subtract a nontrivial fixed point of the recurrence from both sides so the shifted iterate factors
how_used: 3 is the nontrivial fixed point of b->b(b-1)/2; subtracting it gives b_{k+1}-3 = (b_k-3)(b_k+2)/2, exposing the factor (b_k-3) carried forward each step.

technique: Track the 2-adic valuation across a factored recurrence whose extra factor is odd to force a strictly decreasing valuation sequence
how_used: Since b_k+2 is odd, the factorization b_{k+1}-3=(b_k-3)(b_k+2)/2 gives v_2(b_{k+1}-3)=v_2(b_k-3)-1; these positive integers strictly decrease, impossible unless b_0-3<=0, forcing b_0=3 and M=1.

technique: Reduce a divisibility of the form (M+1) | K·M to (M+1) | K by invoking gcd(M, M+1) = 1 to cancel the M factor.
how_used: After reducing to the case a<b with c=b-a, the hypothesis a!+b! | a!b! is divided through by a! to give 1+M | a!·M where M = b!/a! = (a+1)(a+2)···(a+c). Since consecutive integers M and M+1 are coprime, the M is stripped to yield the clean relation 1+M | a!, which is the engine for everything that follows.

technique: Strip k! out of a divisibility by noting that a product of k consecutive integers is divisible by k!, hence M is a multiple of k! and M+1 is coprime to k!.
how_used: M = (a+1)···(a+c) is a product of c consecutive integers, so c! | M and therefore gcd(1+M, c!) = 1. This lets 1+M | a! be sharpened to 1+M | a!/c! = (c+1)(c+2)···a, removing the small factors below c+1 and setting up the size comparison.

technique: Contradict a divisibility N | P by counting and sizing factors: N is a product of more (or equally many) terms each strictly larger than every term of P, forcing N > P.
how_used: 1+M is just above the product M of c integers all exceeding a, while a!/c! = (c+1)···a is a product of a-c ≤ c integers all at most a; with the assumption a ≤ 2c (so a-c ≤ c) this gives 1+M > a!/c!, contradicting 1+M | a!/c!. The boundary case a=2c+1 (where a-c = c+1) is closed by first extracting the extra common factor c+1 (since a+1 = 2(c+1) gives c+1 | M) to reduce to the c-factor product (c+2)···a, again forcing 1+M to exceed it.

technique: Recast 'the number has an odd prime divisor' as 'the number is not a power of 2', reducing the whole problem to controlling the single prime 2 via 2-adic valuation.
how_used: Set P = x_1 x_2 ... x_{n+1} - 1 and a_k = x_k - 1 = (m-n)/(n+k) > 0 (all integers). Proving P has an odd prime factor is equivalent to showing P is not a power of 2, so the argument only ever studies the power of 2 dividing each a_k.

technique: In a block of consecutive integers of length L, pick out the unique multiple of the largest power of 2 not exceeding the block's top; that term carries strictly the most factors of 2, isolating a single index of minimal valuation.
how_used: The denominators n+1,...,2n+1 form a length-(n+1) block; with 2^c the largest power of 2 with 2^c <= 2n+1, one shows n+1 <= 2^c <= 2n+1 so 2^c is the only multiple of 2^c in the block. Writing n+ell = 2^c, and with d = v_2(m-n) >= c since a_ell is an integer, v_2(a_ell) = d-c is strictly minimal: 2^{d-c+1} does not divide a_ell, while 2^{d-c+1} divides every other a_k.

technique: Choose the modulus equal to the threshold power so that every factor but one is congruent to 1, collapsing the whole product modulo that power to the single exceptional factor.
how_used: Working mod 2^{d-c+1}: for k != ell, a_k ≡ 0 so x_k ≡ 1, hence P = prod(a_k+1) - 1 ≡ (a_ell+1)*1^n - 1 ≡ a_ell, which is nonzero mod 2^{d-c+1}; so 2^{d-c+1} does not divide P. Since some other a_k >= 2^{d-c+1} forces P >= 2^{d-c+1}, P cannot be a power of 2, giving the odd prime factor.

technique: Form the sum of the two coupled sequences as a candidate invariant, and isolate the regime where the recurrence simplifies so the sum is exactly conserved there.
how_used: Set s_n = a_n + b_n. Because a_{n+1}=gcd(a_n,b_n)+1 and b_{n+1}=lcm(a_n,b_n)-1, on the regime a_n | b_n one has gcd=a_n, lcm=b_n, so a_{n+1}=a_n+1, b_{n+1}=b_n-1 and s_{n+1}=s_n. Thus during the divisibility phase a_n climbs by 1 while the sum s_n stays frozen, giving a fixed target to compare a_n against.

technique: Construct a min-of-a-set integer monovariant — the least integer at least the current value that fails to divide the frozen invariant — and prove it never increases, forcing boundedness.
how_used: Define W_n = {m >= a_n : m does not divide s_n} and w_n = min W_n. While a_n | b_n, W_n is unchanged so w_n is constant; when a_n does not divide b_n then a_n in W_n gives w_n=a_n, and since gcd(a_n,b_n)<a_n and s_{n+1}=gcd+lcm has only the lcm term divisible by a_n, one gets a_n in W_{n+1}, so w_{n+1} <= a_n = w_n. Hence (w_n) is non-increasing, eventually equals a constant w, and a_n <= w_n bounds the whole a-sequence.

technique: Once one coordinate of a coupled integer recurrence is bounded, reduce the other coordinate modulo the lcm of the bounded coordinate's attainable values, turning the state pair into a deterministic map on a finite set.
how_used: Since a_n is bounded it takes finitely many values; let M = lcm of all a_n and r_n = b_n mod M. As a_n | M, gcd(a_n,b_n)=gcd(a_n,r_n) and b_{n+1}=(a_n/gcd)b_n-1 reduces mod M to a formula in (a_n,r_n), so (a_n,r_n) determines (a_{n+1},r_{n+1}). Finitely many pairs forces the pair-sequence, hence (a_n), to be eventually periodic.

technique: To force two integers equal, exhibit their difference as divisible by EVERY n (by subtracting two copies of a 'divisible-for-all-n' hypothesis), then specialize to one n larger than the difference's absolute value.
how_used: Assuming f(m)=f(k), property (i) makes (k-m)/n = [f^n(m)-m]/n - [f^n(k)-k]/n an integer for all n; choosing n=|k-m|+1 forces k=m, proving f injective (which, with the finite non-image set, lets every positive integer be written uniquely as f^j(a_i), arranging Z>0 into the orbit Table).

technique: When finitely many rows are known arithmetic progressions, subtract their predictable per-window element counts from each fixed-length window to show the remaining rows jointly have constant positive density, pinning a linear growth bound on one of them.
how_used: Each window Delta_n=[n+1,n+T] with T=lcm(T_1,...,T_t) holds exactly T/T_i terms of AP-row i, so the count from the non-AP rows is constant for n>=A and must be >=1 (those rows are infinite); this yields f^d(a_x) <= A+(d+1)(k-t)T for some row x, making beta_d=(f^d(a_x)-a_x)/d a bounded positive integer, so by pigeonhole some step T_x recurs for infinitely many d (the 'dense row').

technique: Upgrade a relation known only along an infinite index subset to all indices: for a fixed target index, pick a subset index far enough that both candidate quantities are divisible by their gap while their difference is smaller than the gap, forcing the difference to vanish.
how_used: For each fixed j, choose y in Y (where f^y(a_x)=a_x+y*T_x) with y-j exceeding |f^j(a_x)-(a_x+jT_x)|; both f^y(a_x)-f^j(a_x)=f^{y-j}(f^j(a_x))-f^j(a_x) and f^y(a_x)-(a_x+jT_x)=(y-j)T_x are divisible by y-j, so their bounded difference is 0, giving f^j(a_x)=a_x+jT_x for all j and proving every Table row is an AP (whence f(n)-n is periodic with period lcm(T_i)).

technique: To rule out the gcd ever being as small as 1, pigeonhole the inputs by the parity of their function values until you can pick a pair whose two gcd-arguments are forced to share the factor 2.
how_used: If two distinct even inputs both have even f-values, or two distinct odd inputs both have odd f-values, then 2 | gcd(f(m)+n, f(n)+m); otherwise one can pick an even m with f(m) odd and an odd n with f(n) even, again making both f(m)+n and f(n)+m even — so 2 always divides the gcd and no 1-good function exists.

technique: Engineer the construction so that each f(n)+n is exactly one less than a power of the prime you want to control, so the symmetric sum of the two gcd-arguments has a pinned-down valuation at that prime.
how_used: Set f(n)=2^{g(n)+1}-n-1 so f(k)+k=2^{g(k)+1}-1; then A+B=(f(m)+m)+(f(n)+n)=2^{g(m)+1}+2^{g(n)+1}-2, which is ≡2 (mod 4) and hence has 2-adic valuation exactly 1, so 4 cannot divide gcd(A,B).

technique: Take the exponent of the fixed base to be a factorial large enough that p−1 divides it for every prime p that could divide an argument, so Fermat's little theorem makes that base-power ≡1 (mod p) no matter which such prime arises.
how_used: With g(n+1)=(2^{g(n)+1})! and the weak bound 2^{g(m-1)+1}≥B, any odd prime p|B satisfies p-1<B≤2^{g(m-1)+1}, so p-1 divides g(m)=(2^{g(m-1)+1})!; hence 2^{g(m)}≡1 (mod p), giving A+B≡2^{g(n)+1} (mod p), which with p|A+B forces p=2 — contradiction.

technique: To turn a 'count of large prime factors' inequality into an exact divisibility statement, assume a forbidden divisor and lcm it with an inductively-known divisor to overshoot the prime-factor count and reach a contradiction.
how_used: Step 1 proves k | a-b iff k | f(a)-f(b) for every large k (all prime factors >N), by induction on k. If k0 | f(x)-f(y) with 0<x-y<k0, set l=L(x-y); the induction hypothesis gives l | f(x)-f(y), so lcm(k0,l) | f(x)-f(y). Since lcm(k0,l) is large and >l, mho(f(x)-f(y)) >= mho(lcm(k0,l)) > mho(l) = mho(x-y), contradicting the hypothesis mho(f(x)-f(y)) <= mho(x-y). This forces f to be injective mod k0 on any window of k0 consecutive integers, hence periodic mod k0.

technique: Bound the smooth (small-prime) part of a value by selecting, among finitely many candidate differences, one divisible by no small prime power — valid because each fixed prime power divides at most one of the differences when the underlying values are distinct modulo it.
how_used: Claim 2 (Step 2): choose alpha with 2^alpha > f(N) so f(0),...,f(N) are distinct mod p^alpha for each small prime p. For t>N each small p has at most one index i<=N with p^alpha | f(t)-f(i); since there are fewer than N small primes, some j<=N has S(f(t)-f(j)) < P=d^alpha. Combined with L(f(t)-f(j))=L(t-j)<=t-j from Step 1, this gives f(t) = f(j) + L*S < f(N)+(t-j)P <= ct, a linear upper bound, after which f(t)/t (an integer for large primes t) takes finitely many values, yielding an infinite set on which f(t)=at.

technique: Upgrade a divisibility congruence f(x) = ax (mod m) into the exact equality f(x)=ax by arranging the modulus m to exceed an a-priori bound on the discrepancy |f(x)-ax|.
how_used: Step 3, Claim 3: for x in residue class R_{r+1}, pick n in R_r with f(n)=an and |n-x| > |f(x)-ax|. Because n-x is congruent to -1 (mod N!), the gap n-x has no small prime factors (is large), so Step 1 gives n-x | f(x)-f(n) = f(x)-an which equals f(x)-ax mod (n-x). A divisor larger than the bounded |f(x)-ax| forces f(x)=ax. Bootstrapping across the residue classes R_i mod N! extends f(x)=ax from the infinite linear set to all of Z.

technique: Query two index-sets that overlap in exactly one index so the value appearing in both anonymized responses is forced to be the shared index's number, anchoring the rest.
how_used: Sergey asks S1={17,18} and S2={18,19}; only a_18 appears in both responses, so it is identified, and then a_17, a_19 are pinned as the remaining values. The three indices are chosen pairwise-coprime with 17*18*19=5814>5000, so CRT determines N uniquely within the range.

technique: To make two candidate values indistinguishable under modular responses, choose their difference to be a common multiple of every gcd that can arise between the queried moduli.
how_used: Take the ambiguous pair N=1 and N'=M+1 with M=lcm(1,...,10)=2^3*3^2*5*7=2520; since gcd of any two distinct integers <=20 is at most 10, every such gcd divides M = N'-N, which is exactly the compatibility condition needed to relabel residues.

technique: Defeat a single anonymized query by building a cyclic system where each returned value simultaneously matches one candidate mod its own index and the other candidate mod the next index, solvable by CRT because consecutive gcds divide the candidates' difference.
how_used: For Sergey's set S={s_1,...,s_k}, choose distinct b_i with b_i = 1 (mod s_i) and b_i = M+1 (mod s_{i+1}) (indices cyclic); compatibility holds since gcd(s_i,s_{i+1}) | M, so Xenia's single response {b_i} is consistent with both N=1 (labeling i->s_i) and N=M+1 (labeling i->s_{i+1}), the cyclic shift being a bijection on S.

technique: To force a completely multiplicative {+1,-1}-valued function to take a prescribed sign at an integer, write that integer as a fixed constant times a perfect square, exploiting that the function is +1 on every square.
how_used: Since lambda is completely multiplicative with lambda(p)=-1, lambda(k^2)=+1, so lambda(c*k^2)=lambda(c). To get +1 one writes the integer as 6*y^2 (lambda(6)=lambda(2.3)=+1) or as x^2; to get -1 one uses 2*y^2 (lambda(2)=-1) or 3*x^2 (lambda(3)=-1). The two sign demands on n and n+1 reduce to choosing such constant factors.

technique: Encode the requirement that two consecutive integers each be a fixed constant times a square as a generalized Pell equation A*x^2 - B*y^2 = 1, and use that Pell theory guarantees infinitely many solutions.
how_used: For part i) n=6y^2, n+1=x^2 becomes x^2-6y^2=1 (solutions from (5+2sqrt6)^n), giving infinitely many n with lambda(n)=lambda(n+1)=+1. For part ii) n=2y^2, n+1=3x^2 becomes 3x^2-2y^2=1 (solutions from (sqrt3+sqrt2)^{2n+1}), giving infinitely many with both values -1.

technique: When a target consecutive-pair sign pattern at n may fail, guarantee it instead by a pigeonhole over the three overlapping pairs (n,n+1), (2n,2n+1), (2n+1,2n+2), using that doubling negates the function value (lambda(2m)=lambda(2)lambda(m)).
how_used: Bogdanov's argument: pick 2n+1 a product of the right number of odd primes so lambda(2n+1)=epsilon. If lambda(2n)=epsilon or lambda(2n+2)=epsilon one of the doubled pairs works; otherwise lambda(n)=-lambda(2n)=epsilon and lambda(n+1)=-lambda(2n+2)=epsilon, so (n,n+1) itself works. Hence infinitely many (epsilon,epsilon) pairs for both epsilon=+1 and -1.

technique: Interpret perfect-square values of a cubic as integer points on the curve y^2 = f(x), take the chord through two known integer points, and locate the third intersection via Vieta: its x-coordinate is an integer because the three x-coordinates sum to a coefficient of the cubic.
how_used: After translating so the special indices become 0 and 1, the squares a_0=p^2 and a_1=q^2 give points (0,p),(1,q) on y^2=x^3+bx^2+cx+d. The line y=(q-p)x+p meets the curve in a cubic in t whose root sum is (q-p)^2-b, so the third root is X=(q-p)^2-b-1, an integer, and Y=(q-p)X+p is integer too, making a_X a square. Since the only allowed square terms are at indices 0 and 1, X must be 0 or 1, forcing (q-p)^2 = b+1 or b+2.

technique: Exploit the y -> -y symmetry of y^2=f(x): reflect one of the known integer points across the x-axis and rerun the chord construction to obtain a second, independent constraint of the identical form.
how_used: The point (0,-p) also lies on the curve, so the line through (0,-p) and (1,q) yields (q+p)^2 in {b+1,b+2} just as (q-p)^2 did. Closing: (q-p)^2 and (q+p)^2 differ by 4pq so have the same parity, yet {b+1,b+2} is a two-element set of consecutive integers of opposite parity; both squares must therefore equal the same element, giving (q-p)^2=(q+p)^2, i.e. 4pq=0, so pq=0 and a_0 a_1 = 0.

technique: Show a quadratic value cannot be a perfect square by trapping it strictly between two consecutive squares (n-1)^2 and n^2.
how_used: For the explicit witness a_n = n(n^2-n+1) (chosen with d=0 so a_0=0, a_1=1), n and the positive coprime factor n^2-n+1 must each be squares; for n>1 the bound (n-1)^2 < n^2-n+1 < n^2 shows n^2-n+1 is never a square, leaving only n=0 and n=1 as square terms.

technique: Encode every contiguous-block subset sum as a difference of two prefix sums, so a forbidden zero block-sum becomes distinctness of the prefix sums mod m.
how_used: Set s_i = n_1+...+n_i. If s_i ≡ s_j (i<j) then n_{i+1}+...+n_j ≡ 0 (mod 2008), a forbidden nonempty subset sum; so the 2007 prefix sums are pairwise distinct and all nonzero mod 2008, hence realize exactly the residues {1,2,...,2007}.

technique: Match a single sequence term to a residue already realized by some prefix sum, then subtract that term out to expose a shorter nonempty zero-sum that omits it.
how_used: Since n_2 ≢ 0, n_2 ≡ s_i for some i. For i>1, s_i − n_2 = n_1+n_3+...+n_i is a nonempty subset sum ≡ 0 (mod 2008), contradicting the hypothesis; for i=1, n_2 ≡ n_1 with both in {1,...,2007} forces n_1 = n_2, contradicting n_1 ≠ n_2.

technique: View a symmetric two-variable Diophantine equation as a monic quadratic in one variable, and use Vieta to read off a second integer root from a known solution.
how_used: Clearing denominators gives x^2-(4y-1)x+(y^2+y)=0; treating y as a parameter, from a solution (x0,y0) the second root is x1=4y0-1-x0, which is an integer by the sum x0+x1=4y0-1 and positive by the product x0*x1=y0^2+y0>0.

technique: Compose the Vieta root-flip with the equation's variable-swap symmetry to chain solutions into a single linear recurrence.
how_used: The flip sends (x,y) to (4y-1-x,y), and symmetry of the equation in x,y makes (y,4y-1-x) a solution too; seeding with (1,1) this yields the sequence z_{n+2}=4z_{n+1}-1-z_n with each consecutive pair (z_n,z_{n+1}) a solution.

technique: Establish that a recurrence-generated family of solutions is all distinct by inducting a strict growth bound on the sequence.
how_used: Prove by induction that z_{n+1} >= 2 z_n (and z_{n+1} >= 1) for n>=2, using z_{n+2}=4z_{n+1}-1-z_n >= 4z_{n+1}-z_{n+1}-z_{n+1}=2z_{n+1}; strict increase makes the pairs (z_n,z_{n+1}) pairwise distinct, giving infinitely many.

technique: Force an adjacent pair of divisors (a difference of exactly 1 high in the list) by exhibiting two consecutive integers that each factor into terms already available inside the factorial.
how_used: Take the consecutive integers floor(n/2)^2 - 1 = (floor(n/2)-1)(floor(n/2)+1) and floor(n/2)^2 = floor(n/2) * (2 floor(n/2)); both products use distinct factors at most n, so both divide n!, making them adjacent divisors with gap 1 at value approximately n^2/4.

technique: Read the monotone (non-decreasing) gap hypothesis backward: a gap of 1 occurring at some divisor forces every earlier gap to also be 1, i.e. every integer up to that value must itself be a divisor.
how_used: Since the differences d_i - d_{i-1} are non-decreasing and a gap of 1 appears between floor(n/2)^2 - 1 and floor(n/2)^2, every integer below floor(n/2)^2 must divide n!; this is the closure that is then contradicted.

technique: Invoke Bertrand's postulate to place a prime strictly inside a chosen interval, producing a value too large to divide the factorial yet small enough to lie below the forced consecutive pair.
how_used: After checking floor(n/2)^2 - 1 >= 2n for n >= 13, Bertrand gives a prime p with n < p < floor(n/2)^2 - 1; since p > n it cannot divide n!, contradicting that all integers below floor(n/2)^2 divide n!.

technique: Transport a given relation among consecutive terms of the ordered divisor list to the complementary terms via the involution d -> n/d (d_i·d_{k+1-i}=n), then clear denominators to get a relation among the original terms.
how_used: The hypothesis d_i | d_{i+1}+d_{i+2} is applied at the reflected position: since d_{k-i-1}=n/d_{i+2}, d_{k-i}=n/d_{i+1}, d_{k-i+1}=n/d_i, the condition at the large end reads (n/d_{i+2}) | (n/d_{i+1})+(n/d_i). Multiplying through by d_i d_{i+1} d_{i+2} and cancelling n gives d_i d_{i+1} | d_i d_{i+2}+d_{i+1}d_{i+2}, hence d_i | d_{i+1}d_{i+2}.

technique: Subtract two divisibilities sharing the same modulus to cancel a common cross-term and isolate divisibility by a pure square.
how_used: From the reflected fact d_i | d_{i+1}d_{i+2} and the original condition scaled by d_{i+1}, namely d_i | d_{i+1}(d_{i+1}+d_{i+2}) = d_{i+1}^2 + d_{i+1}d_{i+2}, cancel the shared d_{i+1}d_{i+2} to get d_i | d_{i+1}^2 for all i. Taking d_2=p the smallest prime, p | d_i forces p | d_{i+1} by induction up the list, so every divisor >1 is a multiple of p and n has a single prime.

technique: Under a two-smallest-primes assumption, pin the leading run of the ordered divisor list as consecutive powers of the smallest prime capped by the second-smallest prime, then reflect to locate specific large divisors and apply the condition.
how_used: Assuming p<q are the two smallest primes, the bottom divisors are 1,p,...,p^j,q, so by reflection d_{k-j-1}=n/q, d_{k-j}=n/p^j, d_{k-j+1}=n/p^{j-1}. The condition gives (n/q) | (n/p^j)+(n/p^{j-1}) = (n/p^j)(p+1), i.e. p^j | q(p+1); since gcd(p,p+1)=1 and p≠q this is impossible (equivalently v_p(n)=v_p(n/q) ≤ v_p(n)-j).

technique: When a prime power equals a difference of two squares, factor it and conclude each factor is itself a power of the same prime.
how_used: Writing p^a = b^2 - a^4 = (b+a^2)(b-a^2) forces both b+a^2 = p^{a-x} and b-a^2 = p^x to be powers of p, so 2a^2 = p^x(p^{a-2x}-1).

technique: Pin an unknown exponent by taking the p-adic valuation of both sides, using that a factor of the form p^k - 1 is coprime to p.
how_used: In 2a^2 = p^x(p^{a-2x}-1) with gcd(p, p^{a-2x}-1)=1, equating valuations gives x = 2v_p(a) for odd p; setting m = v_p(a), a = p^m n reduces the relation to 2n^2 = p^{p^m n - 4m} - 1. (The p=2 branch is settled separately by a short mod-4 argument and is not the critical move.)

technique: Solve an exponential-equals-polynomial equation by lower-bounding the exponent linearly in the variable so the exponential outgrows the polynomial, leaving finitely many small cases to check.
how_used: For 2n^2+1 = p^{p^m n - 4m}, bounding the exponent below (a monotonicity/growth estimate giving p^{p^m(n-1)} > 2n^2+1 for n>=3 and large m) forces n=1 or 2 and small m, after which checking 3^m - 4m = 1, 2n^2+1=3^{3n-4}, 2n^2+1=3^n yields a=1,2,6,9 with p=3.

technique: Rewrite the exponent to which a composite base divides n! as the minimum, over the base's prime factors weighted by multiplicity, of the floor-divided p-adic valuations of n!.
how_used: E_10(n)=min(v_5(n!),v_2(n!))=v_5(n!) since always v_5(n!)<=v_2(n!), and E_9(n)=floor(v_3(n!)/2) since 9=3^2; this turns the comparison E_10 vs E_9 into comparing v_5(n!) against floor(v_3(n!)/2), both via Legendre's formula.

technique: Take the factorial argument to be a pure prime power p^t so Legendre's sum becomes a finite geometric series with exact closed form (p^t-1)/(p-1).
how_used: With n=5^{2l-1}, v_5(n!)=5^{2l-2}+...+1=(n-1)/4 exactly, pinning E_10(n)=(n-1)/4; symmetrically m=3^{4l-2} gives v_3(m!)=(m-1)/2 so E_9(m)=(m-1)/4 exactly. This fixes one of the two competing quantities to an exact value near n/4.

technique: Choose the factorial argument's residue modulo the rival prime so each Legendre floor rounds strictly down, pushing that prime's valuation a positive constant below its geometric ceiling.
how_used: 5^{2l-1}=2 (mod 3) forces floor(n/3)=(n-2)/3 etc. giving v_3(n!)<n/2-2/3, hence E_9(n)<n/4=E_10(n); dually 3^{4l-2}=4 (mod 5) gives v_5(m!)<m/4=E_9(m). The strict floor losses break the tie since both exponents otherwise sit at ~n/4. (Sol.2 strengthens the gap arbitrarily using n=-1 mod 3^b via orders/Euler totient.)

technique: Subtract consecutive instances of a running-sum divisibility relation to cancel the partial sum and leave a clean recurrence in the integer quotients.
how_used: Set b_k = 2(a_1+...+a_{k-1})/a_k (a positive integer by hypothesis). Subtracting b_k a_k = 2(a_1+...+a_{k-1}) from b_{k+1} a_{k+1} = 2(a_1+...+a_k) eliminates the sum and yields b_{k+1} a_{k+1} = b_k a_k + 2a_k = (b_k+2) a_k, the relation that drives the whole proof.

technique: For an integer sequence that can rise by at most 1 each step yet is unbounded, take the smallest index where it first reaches a target value N to pin that term to exactly N and its predecessor to exactly N-1.
how_used: From b_{k+1} a_{k+1} = (b_k+2) a_k and a_k < a_{k+1} one gets b_{k+1} < b_k+2, i.e. b_{k+1} <= b_k+1 (jumps at most 1). Choosing n > b_2 and the smallest k with b_{k+1} >= n forces b_k = n-1 and b_{k+1} = n exactly, giving a_{k+1} = a_k(n+1)/n, whence gcd(n,n+1)=1 yields n | a_k.

technique: Show a divisibility relation with a bounded multiplier would confine all prime factors of the sequence to a finite set, contradicting the assumption of infinitely many prime divisors, to force the multiplier sequence unbounded.
how_used: Rewrite a_{k+1} = a_k(b_k+2)/b_{k+1} so a_{k+1} | a_k(b_k+2). If (b_k) were bounded by B, every prime factor of any a_k would be <= B+2 or divide a_1 or a_2 - a finite prime set - contradicting that infinitely many primes divide some a_k; hence (b_k) is unbounded.

technique: Reduce a branching recurrence modulo a small prime to show each new term inherits the residue of one of its predecessors, bounding the set of attainable residues.
how_used: Each branch a_{n+1}=3a_n-2a_{n-1} or 4a_n-3a_{n-1} gives a_{n+1}=3(a_n-a_{n-1})+a_{n-1} or +a_n, so a_{n+1} is congruent to a_n or a_{n-1} mod 3; starting from a_0=0,a_1=1 every term stays in {0,1} mod 3. Two consecutive kawaii integers m,m+1 both lying in {0,1} mod 3 forces m congruent 0 mod 3.

technique: Show the very first branch choice fixes a prime that divides every later first-difference, so each sequence is purely one divisibility type.
how_used: Setting b_n=a_n-a_{n-1} gives b_1=1 and b_{n+1}/b_n in {2,3}; the first ratio b_2 is 2 or 3. If b_2=2 every b_n is even so all terms are odd; if b_2=3 every b_n is divisible by 3 so all terms are 1 mod 3. Hence no kawaii integer is divisible by both 2 and 3. Since 3|m, m is odd, so m+1 is even, forcing its sequence to have b_2=3 and 3|b_n for all n>=2.

technique: Divide every first-difference of the sequence by their common factor and reindex by one to manufacture a smaller instance of the same recurrence whose partial sum lands on the target.
how_used: In the kawaii sequence containing m+1=a_t, all differences b_n (n>=2) are divisible by 3; define b'_n=b_{n+1}/3 so b'_1=1 and ratios b'_{n+1}/b'_n=b_{n+2}/b_{n+1} stay in {2,3}, giving a new kawaii sequence a'_n=sum b'_k with a'_{t-1}=(a_t-1)/3=m/3, proving m/3 is kawaii.

technique: Realize every target of a prescribed multiplicative shape by choosing the unknowns as a geometric progression, so a symmetric additive invariant is forced to carry a perfect-square factor.
how_used: To attain any non-square-free n, write n = ℓ(k+1)² and set (a,b,c,d) = (ℓ, kℓ, kℓ, k²ℓ), a geometric progression of ratio k. Both harmonic sums collapse to kℓ and (a+b)(c+d)/(a+b+c+d)=kℓ, so the equation holds, while a+b+c+d = ℓ(1+k)² carries the square (k+1)²; ranging over k,ℓ≥1 covers exactly the non-square-free integers.

technique: When the modulus is itself the sum of two quantities, a prime dividing the modulus and dividing one of the two summands must divide the other as well.
how_used: After clearing denominators to (ab(c+d)+cd(a+b))(a+b+c+d) = (a+b)²(c+d)², any prime p | S = a+b+c+d divides the RHS, hence divides a+b or c+d; since S = (a+b)+(c+d), p then divides BOTH a+b and c+d, so every prime factor of S divides a+b.

technique: For a square-free integer, upgrade 'every prime factor divides m' to 'the whole integer divides m'.
how_used: S = a+b+c+d is assumed square-free and every prime factor of S divides a+b; distinct pairwise-coprime prime factors then multiply to give S | a+b. But c+d≥2 forces a+b < S, contradicting S | a+b, so S cannot be square-free.

technique: Rescale a matched pair by its gcd to strictly reduce the quantity being minimized, justifying a WLOG-coprime assumption.
how_used: Replacing a_i and b_i by a_i/g_i and b_i/g_i where g_i=gcd(a_i,b_i)>1 divides every product P_k (each contains a factor of a_i or b_i, both divisible by g_i) by g_i, keeping the n+1 products a strictly increasing AP but scaling the common difference to D/g_i<D; since we minimize D, we may assume gcd(a_i,b_i)=1 for all i.

technique: Equate two fractions each known to be in lowest terms and conclude their numerators and their denominators are individually equal.
how_used: Setting consecutive product-differences equal and cancelling the shared factor gives (b_i-a_i)a_{i+1}=b_i(b_{i+1}-a_{i+1}), i.e. a_{i+1}/(b_{i+1}-a_{i+1}) = b_i/(b_i-a_i); coprimality makes both fractions reduced, forcing a_{i+1}=b_i and b_i-a_i=b_{i+1}-a_{i+1}, so a_1, b_1=a_2, ..., b_{n-1}=a_n, b_n collapse into a single arithmetic progression.

technique: Bound a strictly increasing sequence of positive integers below by its index, a_i >= i.
how_used: The collapsed AP a_1<a_2<...<a_n of positive integers with a_1>=1 gives a_i>=i, so D=(b_1-a_1)a_2 a_3 ... a_n >= 1*2*...*n = n!, with equality realized by a_i=i, b_i=i+1 (common difference exactly n!).

technique: Strengthen the induction claim from the one target window to 'every block of N consecutive terms is distinct mod N', so the hypothesis can be re-applied at a shifted starting index.
how_used: Induct on k with the claim that ANY 3^k consecutive values f(n),...,f(n+3^k-1) are distinct mod 3^k (not just f(1..3^k)); this generality is what lets the step lift to 3^{k+1} apply the hypothesis to arbitrary sub-windows starting at any n.

technique: Make a windowed sum of a periodic term independent of the window's starting index by showing its exponents run over a complete residue system modulo the period.
how_used: Telescoping the recurrence gives f(n+3^k)-f(n)=sum_{j} 2^{f(j)} over the block; since 2^x mod 3^{k+1} has period 2*3^k, and the IH (distinct mod 3^k) together with f always odd makes f(n),...,f(n+3^k-1) a complete set of odd residues mod 2*3^k, the sum collapses to 2^1+2^3+...+2^{2*3^k-1}=C independent of n.

technique: Pin the exact p-adic valuation of a geometric-sum constant via lifting-the-exponent to control how a fixed shift behaves modulo a prime power.
how_used: With C=2*(4^{3^k}-1)/3, LTE gives v_3(4^{3^k}-1)=v_3(4-1)+v_3(3^k)=1+k so v_3(C)=k exactly; thus C and 2C are nonzero mod 3^{k+1}, making f(n),f(n+3^k),f(n+2*3^k) pairwise distinct mod 3^{k+1} and (with within-block IH distinctness) lifting to all 3^{k+1} values.

technique: Reduce integrality of a quotient of factorials to a floor inequality at each prime power q, using that v_p(n!)=Σ_i floor(n/p^i) splits the p-adic valuation into independent terms indexed by q=p^i.
how_used: Integrality of (k^2)!·∏_{j=0}^{k-1} j!/(j+k)! is reduced to v_p ≥ 0 for all p; by Legendre it suffices, for every prime power q=p^i, that floor(k^2/q)+Σ_{j=0}^{k-1} floor(j/q) ≥ Σ_{j=0}^{k-1} floor((j+k)/q).

technique: Convert an integer-valued floor inequality into a fractional-part inequality by writing floor(x)=x−{x} so the value parts cancel, using integrality to trade the strict >−1 for the needed ≥0 via one isolated bounded fractional term.
how_used: Since both floor-sums are integers, floor(k^2/q)+Σfloor(j/q) ≥ Σfloor((j+k)/q) is equivalent to the strict >−1 form; substituting floor(x)=x−{x}, the integer parts (k^2/q and Σ(j+k)/q vs Σj/q) cancel exactly, giving {k^2/q}+Σ{j/q} < 1+Σ{(j+k)/q}; as {k^2/q}<1 this reduces to Σ_{j=0}^{k-1}{j/q} ≤ Σ_{j=0}^{k-1}{(j+k)/q}.

technique: Compare the residue-sums mod q of two equal-length windows of consecutive integers by decomposing each window into full periods (start-independent) plus a leftover tail, so the window anchored at a multiple of q minimizes the residue sum.
how_used: Show Σ_{j=0}^{k-1}(j mod q) ≤ Σ_{j=0}^{k-1}((j+k) mod q): writing k=mq+r, any length-k window contributes m·C(q,2) from full periods plus a tail; the block 0,…,k-1 starts at multiple 0 so its tail 0,…,r-1 attains the minimal C(q_2)-tail C(r,2), hence its residue sum is ≤ that of the shifted block k,…,2k-1.

technique: Diagonalize a symmetric binary quadratic form by substituting the sum and difference of the two variables.
how_used: Set a=x+y, b=x-y (same parity forced since x=(a+b)/2, y=(a-b)/2 must be integers), turning x^2+xy+y^2 into (3a^2+b^2)/4 so the equation becomes 3a^2+b^2=4(a/3+1)^3; integrality of the left side forces (a/3+1) to be an integer, hence 3|a, letting one write a=3c and reduce to b^2=4(c+1)^3-27c^2.

technique: Recognize that a one-variable cubic obtained after substitution factors as a perfect square times a linear factor.
how_used: After a=3c the relation becomes b^2=4(c+1)^3-27c^2=4c^3-15c^2+12c+4, which factors exactly as (c-2)^2(4c+1), peeling off the perfect-square factor (c-2)^2.

technique: When a perfect square equals a perfect square times a remaining cofactor, conclude the cofactor is itself a perfect square and set it equal to a square to parametrize.
how_used: From b^2=(c-2)^2(4c+1) deduce 4c+1 is a perfect square (for c!=2; c=2 gives 9), write 4c+1=m^2 with m=2n+1 odd, so c=n^2+n; back-substituting a=3c and b=±(c-2)m yields the full family x=n^3+3n^2-1, y=-n^3+3n+1 and permutations.

technique: To prove a linear form is composite, assume it equals a prime p, reduce its defining relation mod p, and raise that congruence to the exponent appearing in the problem's power constraints so the constraint data can be substituted in.
how_used: Suppose p = ac+bd is prime; then ac ≡ −bd (mod p). Raising to the 4th power gives a^4 c^4 ≡ b^4 d^4 (mod p), the exponent that matches a^4+b^4=c^4+d^4=e^5. Substituting c^4 = e^5−d^4 and b^4 = e^5−a^4 makes the e^{10} cross-terms cancel, leaving a^4 e^5 ≡ d^4 e^5, i.e. p ∣ e^5(a^4−d^4) = e^5(a−d)(a+d)(a^2+d^2); symmetrically p ∣ e^5(c^4−b^4).

technique: A prime dividing a product of bounded factors must divide one of them, so after discarding the factors it is coprime to, the prime is at most the largest remaining factor.
how_used: From p ∣ e^5(a−d)(a+d)(a^2+d^2) and the claim p > e (so p ∤ e), the prime p divides one of a−d, a+d, a^2+d^2, each ≤ a^2+d^2, hence p ≤ a^2+d^2. By the symmetric divisibility p ≤ b^2+c^2, so ac+bd = p ≤ min(a^2+d^2, b^2+c^2). (The official solution asserts p > e without proof; the reconstruction correctly follows that same asserted path.)

technique: From two paired sets with equal symmetric power sums, deduce that the corresponding paired component differences are forced to share a common sign.
how_used: The bound p ≤ a^2+d^2 rewrites as a(a−c)+d(d−b) ≥ 0 and p ≤ b^2+c^2 as b(b−d)+c(c−a) ≥ 0. Because a^4+b^4 = c^4+d^4, if a>c then b<d, so a−c and d−b share a strictly nonzero common sign; whichever sign it is, one of the two inequalities has both its terms strictly negative — a contradiction, so p = ac+bd cannot be prime (and it exceeds 1), i.e. composite.

technique: Turn a divisibility into an inequality by assuming the integer quotient is at least 2, so twice the divisor is at most the dividend.
how_used: With (m+n)^3 dividing the positive 2n(3m^2+n^2)+8, the quotient is >=1; assuming it is >=2 gives (m+n)^3 <= n(3m^2+n^2)+4, which after expanding (m+n)^3 = m^3+3m^2n+3mn^2+n^3 and cancelling 3m^2n+n^3 collapses to m^3+3mn^2 <= 4, forcing m=1 then n=1.

technique: Recognize an expanded alternating cubic a^3-3a^2b+3ab^2-b^3 as the perfect cube (a-b)^3 to reduce an equation to a linear difference.
how_used: In the quotient=1 branch, (m+n)^3 = 2n(3m^2+n^2)+8 rearranges to m^3-3m^2n+3mn^2-n^3 = 8, identified as (m-n)^3 = 8, hence m-n=2, giving the family (m,n)=(n+2,n).

technique: Factor a^{p-1}-1=(a^{(p-1)/2}-1)(a^{(p-1)/2}+1) and use primality of p to force the half-power to be congruent to +1 or -1, splitting the argument into two cases by that sign.
how_used: With base a=10 and p not in {2,5}, Fermat gives 10^{p-1}≡1, so p divides (10^{(p-1)/2}-1)(10^{(p-1)/2}+1); primality forces 10^{(p-1)/2}≡-1 (case a) or 10^{(p-1)/2}≡+1 (case b). This sign is exactly what makes shifting an index by (p-1)/2 multiply its power of 10 by ±1, and selects which digit-response rule Eduardo uses.

technique: Reply to the opponent's move on element i by immediately playing the image of i under a fixed involution of the index set, which stays legal because the involution's two-element orbits are always either both empty or both filled before each opponent move.
how_used: Eduardo first spends his opening move fixing the unpaired index p-1 with digit 0 (and for p=2,5 just sets index 0 to 0 to win); the remaining indices 0..p-2 are paired by i↔i+(p-1)/2. After every Fernando move at index i Eduardo answers at its partner, which is available precisely because each pair {r, r+(p-1)/2} is all-or-nothing chosen, letting him control each pair's total contribution mod p.

technique: Choose the response value on the paired index so the pair's combined contribution becomes independent of the opponent's chosen value, summed over all pairs to a quantity that vanishes mod p.
how_used: Since 10^{i+(p-1)/2}≡±10^i: in case a (≡-1) Eduardo copies the digit a_j=a_i so a_j10^j≡-a_i10^i and each pair sums to 0; in case b (≡+1) he complements a_j=9-a_i so each pair contributes 9·10^i, and Σ_{i=0}^{(p-3)/2}9·10^i=10^{(p-1)/2}-1≡0 mod p.

technique: Defeat a 'some cyclic window avoids a multiple of n' property for composite n=ab by filling the cycle with the constant equal to one factor so that a window of length the other factor sums exactly to n.
how_used: For n=ab with a,b>=2, set a_k=a for 1<=k<=n-1 and a_n=0. The total a(n-1) is not divisible by n, yet from any start i a window of length b (or b+1 across the single 0) sums to a*b = n = 0 mod n, so no start index avoids a multiple of n.

technique: Greedily chain the forced zero-sum blocks into a running endpoint sequence and pigeonhole those endpoint positions modulo n to extract a closed cyclic interval with zero sum.
how_used: Each start index has a forced zero-sum block of length <= n-1 (length n is excluded since the full sum is nonzero mod n). Chaining n such blocks from 0 gives n+1 endpoints i_0<...<i_n; two are congruent mod n, and the interval between them is a sum of zero-sum blocks, hence 0 mod n.

technique: Evaluate a cyclic sub-interval that returns to a position congruent modulo the period as an integer number k of full periods, making its sum equal to k times the full-period sum, then apply primality of the period to force non-divisibility.
how_used: Since i_s = i_r mod n, i_s - i_r = k*n; each block having length <= n-1 over <= n steps bounds i_s - i_r <= (n-1)n so 1<=k<=n-1. The loop sum equals k*(a_1+...+a_n); with n prime dividing neither k nor the total, n cannot divide the product, contradicting that the loop sum is 0 mod n.

technique: Reframe a 'least exponent t with fixed modulus dividing 10^t-1' condition as a multiplicative order, so the target set becomes the image of a function over a finite index set.
how_used: After reducing to gcd(m,10)=1, the m-tastic numbers are exactly ord_{cm}(10) as c ranges over C={1≤c≤2017 : gcd(c,10)=1}; thus S(m)={ord_{cm}(10):c∈C} is the image of c↦ord_{cm}(10) on C, so |S(m)|≤|C|, and counting c≡1,3,7,9 (mod 10) gives |C|=4·201+3=807 as the upper bound.

technique: Engineer the modulus as 10^α-1 with α chosen so every relevant prime already divides it, then apply Lifting the Exponent to 10^{kα}-1 to make each prime valuation grow linearly in k.
how_used: Take P={primes p≤2017, p≠2,5}, α=φ(∏P) so every p∈P divides m=10^α-1; then m|10^t-1 forces α|t (t=kα), and LtE gives ν_p(10^{kα}-1)=ν_p(m)+ν_p(k), so cm|10^{kα}-1 ⇔ ν_p(c)≤ν_p(k) ∀p ⇔ c|k; least k=c, hence ord_{cm}(10)=cα, giving 807 distinct values and attaining the bound.

technique: To exploit that a fraction is an integer, work modulo its denominator M: replace the numerator by the denominator (they share most of their structure) so the heavy common factor cancels and only a clean 'base raised to a power is congruent to 1' relation survives.
how_used: Setting M=(p+q)^{p-q}(p-q)^{p+q}-1, the integrality means M divides the numerator, so numerator ≡ denominator (mod M); the matching factors (p+q)^{p-q}(p-q)^{p-q} cancel, leaving (p+q)^{2q} ≡ (p-q)^{2q} (mod M), i.e. ((p+q)(p-q)^{-1})^{2q} ≡ 1 (mod M) after multiplying by the inverse of (p-q) which is coprime to M.

technique: Constrain the prime divisors of an expression a^n ≡ 1 by intersecting the two facts that the multiplicative order divides n and (by Fermat) divides r−1: each prime r is therefore ≡ 1 modulo a prime factor of n, or else the order is so small that r divides a small explicit base-difference.
how_used: For q≥5 the order of (p+q)(p-q)^{-1} mod a prime r|M divides 2q, hence is 1,2,q, or 2q; orders q,2q force r≡1 (mod q), while orders 1,2 force r | (p+q)^2-(p-q)^2 = 4pq so r=p or q. The case r=p is killed by reducing M mod p with Fermat: M ≡ q^{p-q}(-q)^{p+q}-1 = (q^2)^p-1 ≡ q^2-1=(q-1)(q+1) (mod p), and both factors are < p. So every divisor of M is ≡ 0 or 1 (mod q).

technique: Defeat a 'all divisors lie in residue set S mod q' restriction by factoring the number via difference of squares into two factors that are too close together to both lie in S.
how_used: M = ((p+q)^{(p-q)/2}(p-q)^{(p+q)/2}-1)((p+q)^{(p-q)/2}(p-q)^{(p+q)/2}+1) is a product of two consecutive odd numbers (differing by 2); each must be ≡ 0 or 1 (mod q), but two numbers differing by 2 cannot both lie in {0,1} mod q once q≥5, contradiction, eliminating all q≥5.

technique: Exploit a scale-invariant constraint by normalizing the tuple to make one of the two quantities equal to 1, collapsing the pair of integrality conditions into a single integrality condition on the other quantity over integer representatives.
how_used: Since multiplying (a_1,...,a_n) by a positive rational rescales the sum and the reciprocal-sum inversely, the product f=(Σa)(Σ1/a) is scale-invariant; the solver normalizes to x+y+z=1, writes (x,y,z)=(a/(a+b+c), b/(a+b+c), c/(a+b+c)) with integer a,b,c, so the only remaining requirement is that 1/x+1/y+1/z = (b+c)/a+(a+c)/b+(a+b)/c be an integer, reducing the rational problem to a divisibility problem in integers.

technique: Given one integer solution of a symmetric quadratic Diophantine equation, view it as a quadratic in one variable and replace a root by the conjugate (Vieta jumping) to manufacture an infinite ascending sequence of distinct solutions.
how_used: After fixing a=1, the condition becomes b^2+c^2-3bc+b+c=0; treating it as b^2-(3c-1)b+(c^2+c)=0 with c fixed, the second root b_0=(c^2+c)/b is an integer satisfying b_0=(c^2+c)/b>c when c>=b, so from (2,3) one jumps to (3,6),(6,14),(14,35),... giving infinitely many distinct triples.

technique: Translate 'sum is an integer and reciprocal-sum is an integer' for a coprime-fraction pair into two divisibility relations on numerators and denominators, then use coprimality to force the denominators (and numerators) equal, killing the degrees of freedom.
how_used: For (x,y)=(a/b, c/d) with gcd(a,b)=gcd(c,d)=1, the conditions become bd | ad+bc and ac | ad+bc; from the first, d|bc with gcd(c,d)=1 gives d|b and b|ad with gcd(a,b)=1 gives b|d, so b=d, and analogously a=c, forcing x=y and leaving only finitely many solutions — establishing n=1,2 fail so the answer is n=3.

technique: Pin down a function known only through Euclidean-step functional equations by encoding its value as the sign/position of the canonical Bezout coefficient chosen in a balanced residue window.
how_used: For coprime (a,b) take the unique Bezout pair g(a,b)=(u,v) with ua+vb=1 and u in (-b/2,b/2]; conjecture f(a,b)=1 iff u>0, equivalently iff the inverse of a mod b (in {1,...,b-1}) is < b/2. The conjecture is verified by induction on a+b: the subtractive axiom f(a+b,b)=f(a,b) sends g(a,b) to g(a-b,b)=(u,v+u) so u is unchanged, and the swap axiom f(a,b)+f(b,a)=1 in the a<b case is handled by bounding v from vb=1-ua (with the integrality step 2v<a+1 => 2v<=a) to show g(b,a)=(v,u), reducing back to the already-settled subtractive case.

technique: Replace a sum of an inverse-indicator over squares by an indicator over the squares themselves, using that the set of squares equals the set of inverse-squares as a multiset mod p.
how_used: Since f(n^2,p)=1 iff (n^2)^{-1} mod p < p/2, and {n^2 mod p : 1<=n<=p-1} = {n^{-2} mod p : 1<=n<=p-1} as multisets (each quadratic residue hit twice in each), via the bijection n->n^{-1}, the sum equals twice the count of quadratic residues k^2 (1<=k<=(p-1)/2) whose own least residue is < p/2.

technique: Lower-bound a count of residues lying in an initial interval by restricting to elements small enough that no modular reduction occurs, so they equal their own residue.
how_used: To bound #{k<=(p-1)/2 : k^2 mod p < p/2} from below, keep only k with k^2 < p/2 itself: these k^2 are already their own residues and automatically lie below p/2. Their number is floor(sqrt(p/2)) > sqrt(p/2)-1, giving the sum > 2(sqrt(p/2)-1) = sqrt(2p)-2.

technique: Instantiate the universal divisibility hypothesis at a modulus that is a high power of the target number, so the congruence pins the exact p-adic valuation rather than just a lower bound.
how_used: Apply the hypothesis with k=b^2; since b^2 (hence p_i^{2alpha_i}) divides b - a_k^n, we get a_k^n ≡ b mod p_i^{alpha_i+1}, forcing v_{p_i}(a_k^n) to equal exactly alpha_i = v_{p_i}(b) for every prime p_i dividing b.

technique: Conclude that an exponent is divisible by n by noting that the prime valuation of a perfect n-th power is a multiple of n.
how_used: Because a_k^n is a complete n-th power, v_{p_i}(a_k^n) is divisible by n; having pinned this valuation to alpha_i, deduce n | alpha_i for each i, so b = (prod p_i^{alpha_i/n})^n = A^n.

technique: Certify a fixed linear combination never hits a multiple of the modulus by trapping its value in an interval and pinning its parity, so no admissible multiple of the modulus lies inside.
how_used: Take the 10-residue template J={-9,-7,-5,-3,-1,1,3,5,7,9}. For any a,b,c,d,e in J the alternating sum a-b+c-d+e is odd and lies in [-45,45]; the only multiple of 47 in that interval is 0, which is even, so the sum is never divisible by 47 and J is 'good'.

technique: Pull back a known good configuration under multiplication by a unit, using that a homogeneous linear congruence is invariant under scaling every variable by the same unit.
how_used: For each k=1..46 set A_k={x in X : kx mod 47 lands in J}. If some A_k were bad (47 | a-b+c-d+e for elements of A_k), then 47 | k(a-b+c-d+e) with each residue ka,...,ke in J, contradicting that J is good; hence every A_k is a good subset of X.

technique: Count membership of each element across all dilation classes to get a fixed per-element multiplicity, sum to a total, then average over the classes to force one large class.
how_used: As k runs over the 46 units, kr runs over all nonzero residues, so each x in X lands in J for exactly 10 values of k; hence x lies in exactly 10 of the A_k, giving sum_k |A_k| = 10|X| = 100000, and averaging over the 46 values of k yields some |A_k| >= 100000/46 > 2173 > 2007, from which any 2007-subset of that A_k works.

technique: When a quantity equals a polynomial evaluated at x and the polynomial is odd (P(-x)=-P(x)), drop the even-degree terms and write P(x)=x^3 Q(x)+c x so the high-power valuation is controlled solely by the linear coefficient.
how_used: After rewriting the difference as (front factor)·P(2^k) with P(x)=(x+1)(x+3)...(x+2^k-1)-(x-1)(x-3)...(x-2^k+1), one checks P(-x)=-P(x) (using k>=2), so P has only odd-power coefficients. Then P(2^k)=2^{3k}Q(2^k)+2^k c, the cubic-and-higher part is automatically divisible by 2^{3k}, and the exact 2-adic valuation reduces to evaluating v_2 of the linear coefficient c.

technique: To extract a power of 2 from a symmetric sum of reciprocals, pair the term at index a with the term at the complementary index N-a so that 1/a+1/(N-a)=N/(a(N-a)) pulls the modulus N out front as a factor.
how_used: The linear coefficient is c=2·(2^k-1)!!·sum_{i} 1/(2i-1); pairing 1/(2i-1) with 1/(2^k-2i+1) gives sum 2^k/((2i-1)(2^k-2i+1)), so c=2^k·S, immediately contributing a clean factor of 2^k toward the final valuation 3k-1.

technique: To evaluate a sum of inverse-squares over the units modulo 2^k, use that inversion x -> x^{-1} permutes the odd residues, so the sum of inverse-squares equals the sum of squares of the residues themselves.
how_used: S = sum (2^k-1)!!/((2i-1)(2^k-2i+1)) ≡ -sum (2^k-1)!! a_{2i-1}^2 mod 2^k (since 2^k-2i+1≡-(2i-1) and a is the inverse); as 2i-1 ranges over odd residues so does its inverse, converting the inverse-square sum to -(2^k-1)!!·sum(2i-1)^2. The routine closed form sum_{i}(2i-1)^2 = 2^{k-1}(2^{2k}-1)/3 then gives v_2(S)=k-1, so v_2(c)=2k-1 and v_2(P(2^k))=3k-1.

technique: Take the minimal element d of an addition-closed subset of N and show the subset is exactly the multiples of d, ruling out non-multiples by minimal-counterexample descent.
how_used: For a fixed prime p, set d = min{x : p | f(x)} (nonempty by surjectivity). The iff-condition (p|f(a) and p|f(b) ⟹ p|f(a+b)) makes {x : p|f(x)} closed under addition, so all multiples kd lie in it by induction. If some y with d∤y had p|f(y), the minimal such y exceeds d and applying the condition to (d, y−d) forces p|f(y−d) with d∤(y−d), a smaller counterexample — contradiction. Hence p|f(x) ⟺ d|x.

technique: Pair an argument with a complementary argument chosen so their sum lands in a controlled set, pinning one value to the negative of the other mod p to transfer a residue equivalence.
how_used: To prove x ≡ y (mod d) ⟺ f(x) ≡ f(y) (mod p), pair x with c = 2xd−x: since d | x+c = 2xd, crux 0 gives p|f(2xd), and the condition yields p|f(x)+f(c), so f(x) ≡ −f(c). When d|(y−x) the same complement c satisfies d|(y+c), so f(y) ≡ −f(c) = f(x); conversely f(x)≡f(y) makes p|f(y)+f(c), so p|f(y+c) and crux 0 forces d|(y+c)=d|(y−x). Counting distinct residues both ways with surjectivity then forces d=p, so p|f(x) ⟺ p|x and x≡y (mod p) ⟺ f(x)≡f(y) (mod p).

technique: To rule out a candidate value differing from the target, take a prime divisor of (their difference + 1) so the two become congruent to consecutive integers mod that prime, contradicting a congruence-detection lemma.
how_used: In the induction f(n)=n with k=f(n): if k>n, a prime p | k−n+1 gives k ≡ n−1 (mod p); with f(n−1)=n−1 the lemma f(x)≡f(y)⟺x≡y forces n−1 ≡ n (mod p), impossible. If k<n, a prime p | n−k+1 gives n ≡ k−1 (mod p) and, with f(k−1)=k−1, forces k ≡ k−1 (mod p), impossible. Only k=n survives, completing f(n)=n.

technique: Introduce an auxiliary second variable to recast a one-parameter divisibility condition as a symmetric two-variable relation that supports descent.
how_used: Set x=k and y=2n so that the target divisor 8kn-1 becomes 4xy-1 and (4k^2-1)^2 becomes (4x^2-1)^2; the problem is then equivalent to the lemma '4xy-1 | (4x^2-1)^2 iff x=y', and the forced equality k=2n is solvable exactly when k is even (giving n=k/2).

technique: Show the complementary cofactor of a divisor lies in the same parametric family by computing its residue modulo the base, then bound its size to get a strictly smaller instance.
how_used: For a bad pair with x<y, let r=(4x^2-1)^2/(4xy-1); since 4xy-1 ≡ -1 (mod 4x) one gets r ≡ -(4x^2-1)^2 ≡ -1 (mod 4x), so r=4xz-1, and r<4x^2-1 forces z<x, producing a smaller bad pair (x,z).

technique: Use the unit relation a*b ≡ 1 (mod ab-1) to substitute inside the dividend and transfer a divisibility to the swapped variable.
how_used: Since 4xy ≡ 1 (mod 4xy-1), replace the 1 to get (4y^2-1)^2 ≡ (4y^2-(4xy)^2)^2 = 16y^4(4x^2-1)^2 ≡ 0, proving 4xy-1 also divides (4y^2-1)^2 so the swapped pair (y,x) is bad; combined with the cofactor step this makes the asymmetric weight 2x+y a strictly decreasing monovariant on bad pairs.

technique: Split a factorial's p-adic valuation across a power-of-p cut: when an argument is (a multiple of p^k) plus a remainder smaller than p^k, the valuation of the factorial splits additively into the valuations of the two pieces.
how_used: Lemma: for p^k > r, ν_p(qp^k + r) = ν_p(qp^k) + ν_p(r), where ν_p(n)=ord_p(n!)=sum of ord_p(i). Proven via the claim ord_p(qp^k + i) = ord_p(i) for 0<i<p^k (since ord_p(i)=e<k makes p^e divide both qp^k and i but p^{e+1} divide only qp^k). This makes the residue-vector invariant f_i(n)=ν_{p_i}(n) mod d additive across operands separated by a high prime power.

technique: Define a tower sequence (each term = a fixed base raised to the previous term) so that every earlier term is dwarfed by a prime-power factor common to all later terms, forcing a valuation-based invariant to be additive over any subset-sum of the terms.
how_used: Set n_1=1, n_{ℓ+1}=(p_1 p_2 ... p_k)^{n_ℓ}. Then p_i^{n_{ℓ_1}} divides n_{ℓ_2}+...+n_{ℓ_m} and exceeds n_{ℓ_1}, so the cut-additivity lemma applies with k=r=n_{ℓ_1}, giving by induction f(n_{ℓ_1}+...+n_{ℓ_m}) = f(n_{ℓ_1})+...+f(n_{ℓ_m}) for any increasing indices.

technique: Annihilate an additive residue-vector invariant mod d by summing exactly d objects that share the same value of it (pigeonhole over the finite residue codomain supplies infinitely many equal-valued objects).
how_used: f takes finitely many values in (Z/d)^k, so an infinite subsequence n_{ℓ_1},n_{ℓ_2},... has constant f. By additivity, f(n_{ℓ_{m+1}}+...+n_{ℓ_{m+d}}) = d·f(n_{ℓ_1}) = (0,...,0), so each block of d gives an n with d | ν_{p_i}(n) for all i, yielding infinitely many.

technique: Use the multiplicative order of the ratio of the two bases to reindex the p-divisible terms of a two-term sequence along an arithmetic progression, normalizing the expression into a single-exponent form x^n - y with x congruent to 1 mod p.
how_used: p divides c a^N - d b^N exactly when (a/b)^N is congruent to d/c mod p, so the p-divisible indices form an AP kappa, kappa+lambda, kappa+2lambda,... with lambda the order of a/b mod p. Writing N = kappa + n*lambda and factoring out the unit c b^N, nu_p(c a^{kappa+n lambda} - d b^{kappa+n lambda}) = nu_p((a^lambda/b^lambda)^n - d b^kappa/(c a^kappa)) = nu_p(x^n - y), where x = (a/b)^lambda is congruent to 1 mod p (definition of order) and y = d b^kappa/(c a^kappa) is congruent to 1 mod p (since index kappa is p-divisible, (a/b)^kappa is congruent to d/c). This reduces the whole problem to: for units x, y congruent to 1 mod p with all nu_p(x^n - y) bounded, show they are constant.

technique: To manufacture an element with a prescribed p-adic valuation e, raise a unit congruent to 1 to the power p^{e-d}: by LTE each p-th power adds exactly one to the valuation of (unit - 1).
how_used: Given two terms with d = nu_p(x^n - y) < nu_p(x^m - y) = e, subtract them: x^m - x^n = (x^m - y) - (x^n - y) has valuation min = d (strict-min), and since x is a unit this gives nu_p(x^{m-n} - 1) = d. Then by LTE for the odd prime p, nu_p((x^{m-n})^{p^{e-d}} - 1) = d + (e-d) = e, so k = p^{e-d}|m-n| yields a unit x^k = 1 + p^e u with p not dividing u, i.e. nu_p(x^k - 1) = e exactly.

technique: Perturb the target element by a controlled unit raised to a free exponent r, expand to first order in the leading p-power, and solve a linear congruence in r mod p to cancel the leading p-adic digit and gain one more power of p.
how_used: Write x^k = 1 + p^e u and x^m = y + p^e v with p not dividing u, v. Then x^{kr+m} - y = (1 + p^e u)^r (y + p^e v) - y = p^e (v + y u r) + p^{2e}(...). Choosing r with 1 <= r <= p-1 and r congruent to -v/u mod p (using y congruent to 1) makes v + y u r divisible by p, so nu_p(x^{kr+m} - y) >= e+1: a new index whose valuation exceeds the current top e. Re-triggering the LTE-unit step and this perturbation drives valuations to infinity, contradicting the bound M; hence nu_p(x^n - y) is constant, and undoing the reparametrization gives a single T.

technique: Recognize that an additive operator intertwines multiplication-by-x with the Frobenius p-th-power map, proved via freshman's dream (a+b)^p = a^p+b^p in characteristic p.
how_used: For Psi(sum a_i x^i) = sum a_i x^{p^i}, one shows Psi(xP) = Psi(P)^p: shifting P's exponents by 1 sends x^{p^i} to (x^{p^i})^p, and the p-th power passes through the sum because cross terms vanish mod p. Extending linearly gives Psi(R*P) = sum r_i Psi(P)^{p^i}, every summand divisible by Psi(P), hence P | Q implies Psi(P) | Psi(Q), which yields Psi(gcd(F,G)) | gcd(Psi(F),Psi(G)).

technique: Apply an additive operator to a Bezout linear combination to transfer a gcd-divisibility in the harder converse direction.
how_used: From Bezout, AF + BG = gcd(F,G); applying the additive Psi gives Psi(AF) + Psi(BG) = Psi(gcd(F,G)). Since Psi(F) | Psi(AF) and Psi(G) | Psi(BG), the common divisor gcd(Psi(F),Psi(G)) divides the right side, so gcd(Psi(F),Psi(G)) | Psi(gcd(F,G)); both sides monic gives equality.

technique: Translate a run of consecutive zero binary digits into a one-sided fractional bound: scale the number by the power of two that moves the run to just after the radix point, putting the scaled value just above an integer with gap below 2^{-(run length)}.
how_used: Assuming b_n,...,b_{2n} are all 0, multiply sqrt(3) by 2^{n-1} so its fractional part starts at digit b_n; the n+1 zero digits force k < 2^{n-1} sqrt(3) < k + 1/2^{n+1} for the integer k = floor(2^{n-1} sqrt(3)), strict because sqrt(3) is irrational.

technique: Square a tight one-sided dyadic approximation of an irrational so the irrational part becomes an exact integer, then trap that integer strictly between two consecutive integers for a contradiction.
how_used: Squaring k < 2^{n-1} sqrt(3) < k + 1/2^{n+1} makes the middle term the integer 3*2^{2n-2}, and showing k^2 < 3*2^{2n-2} < k^2+1 contradicts integrality.

technique: Collapse an n-dependent linear cross-term arising from squaring back into an O(1) constant by re-substituting the original approximation bound into the term's variable.
how_used: The cross-term k/2^n from squaring is bounded by replacing k with 2^{n-1} sqrt(3) (using k < 2^{n-1} sqrt(3)), giving k/2^n < sqrt(3)/2, so the total error sqrt(3)/2 + 1/2^{2n+2} stays below 1 for all n.

technique: Define a completely additive arithmetic function by its prime values through the recursion p ↦ p−1 that mirrors the iterated map, so the function's value at n lower-bounds the number of iterations.
how_used: Set w(ab)=w(a)+w(b), w(2)=1, w(p)=w(p−1) for odd primes p. Then w(n) counts the total powers of 2 produced as φ is applied repeatedly, and since the 2-exponent drops by at most 1 per φ-step (w(φ(n))=w(n)−1 when n even, =w(n) when n odd), the number of iterations satisfies k ≥ w(n). Reducing n≤3^k to log_3 n ≤ k then needs only w(n) ≥ log_3 n.

technique: Prove a logarithmic lower bound for a recursively-defined prime function by strong induction, peeling off the guaranteed factor of 2 in p−1.
how_used: To get w(n) ≥ log_3 n it suffices (by additivity over the factorization) to show w(p) ≥ log_3 p for every prime; w(2)=1≥log_3 2 is direct. For odd p, write w(p)=w(2)+w((p−1)/2) ≥ 1 + log_3(p−1) − log_3 2 by the inductive hypothesis on (p−1)/2 < p, and 1+log_3((p−1)/2) ≥ log_3 p reduces to 3(p−1)≥2p, i.e. p≥3.

technique: Use the subgroup's multiplicative closure to normalize a three-term additive relation a+b=c by dividing through by one element, recasting the forbidden relation as a common root of two fixed integer polynomials.
how_used: For S the cyclic multiplicative subgroup of order n generated by a primitive nth root of unity α, a sum-free violation a+b≡c divides by a to give 1+α^k≡α^m, so X=α^k is a common root mod p of (X+1)^n−1 and X^n−1 (both vanishing on nth roots of unity / their shifts).

technique: Bound the bad primes for a coprime polynomial pair: write the Bezout identity a(X)f+b(X)g=c (constant) in Z[X], so any shared root mod p forces p∣c, leaving only finitely many primes.
how_used: Applied to f=(X+1)^n−1, g=X^n−1: their Z[X]-Bezout combination equals a nonzero integer constant c, so a common root mod p forces p∣c, hence the construction fails (loses sum-freeness) only for finitely many small primes p. A prime p≡1 (mod n) outside this finite bad set exists by Dirichlet on primes in arithmetic progression, giving |S|=n≥N.

technique: Prove two integer polynomials share no complex root by intersecting the modulus constraints they impose on a root, pinning any common root to specific roots of unity.
how_used: A common root ω of (X+1)^n−1 and X^n−1 must satisfy |ω|=1 and |1+ω|=1; these two unit-circle conditions force ω=−1/2±(√3/2)i, which are the primitive cube roots of unity (order 3), an nth root of unity only when 3∣n — so for n≢0 mod 3 the two polynomials have no common complex root and hence gcd in Z[X] is 1.

technique: Replace a variable by its residue modulo the denominator to collapse the numerator into a recognizable closed form
how_used: Since b ≡ -a^2-3 (mod a^2+b+3), substitute this into the numerator ab+3b+8 to get a(-a^2-3)+3(-a^2-3)+8 ≡ -(a+1)^3, so the denominator dividing the numerator means it divides (a+1)^3

technique: Use a cube-free hypothesis to demote divisibility of a cube to divisibility of its square
how_used: Because a^2+b+3 has no prime to the third power, the fact that it divides (a+1)^3 forces it to divide (a+1)^2 (each prime power in the divisor caps at exponent 2, matching what (a+1)^2 already supplies)

technique: Trap a divisor strictly between zero and twice the number being divided to force them equal
how_used: From 0 < (a+1)^2 < 2(a^2+b+3) and a^2+b+3 | (a+1)^2, the only multiple available is exactly (a+1)^2 = a^2+b+3, giving b=2(a-1) and n=2

technique: Reduce a 'two-in-same-class share property P' problem to constructing a single triple all of whose pairwise relations satisfy P, so a 3-into-2 pigeonhole forces the conclusion.
how_used: To guarantee two cards in one of the two piles sum to a square, it suffices to exhibit three cards a,b,c among {n,...,2n} whose three pairwise sums a+b, b+c, a+c are ALL perfect squares; any split of these three into two piles puts two of them together, and that pair sums to a square.

technique: Build three integers with all three pairwise sums prescribed by treating the targets as a 3x3 linear system (each number = half-sum-of-targets minus the opposite target), and pick the targets as consecutive squares to make the solution explicit.
how_used: Setting a+b,b+c,a+c equal to the three consecutive squares (2k-1)^2,(2k)^2,(2k+1)^2 solves to (a,b,c)=(2k^2-4k, 2k^2+1, 2k^2+4k); consecutiveness keeps the three values tightly clustered so they can all land in [n,2n].

technique: Show a parametrized construction works for a tail by proving the validity interval for index k overlaps that of index k+1, so the union of intervals covers everything past a threshold.
how_used: k works for all n in I_k=[k^2+2k, 2k^2-4k+1] (the constraints n<=2k^2-4k and 2k^2+4k<=2n); checking (k+1)^2+2(k+1) <= 2k^2-4k+1 for k>=9 makes I_k and I_{k+1} overlap, so I_9 ∪ I_10 ∪ ... = [99,∞), settling all n>=99.

technique: From a condition that each partial sum is a perfect square, write the i-th term as a difference of consecutive square-roots and factor it to lower-bound the term by the sum of those roots.
how_used: Setting d_1+...+d_i = s_i^2 with s_0=0 and s strictly increasing integers, d_i = s_i^2 - s_{i-1}^2 = (s_i+s_{i-1})(s_i-s_{i-1}) >= s_i + s_{i-1} >= 2i-1; this forces the divisor 1 to occupy position 1 (d_1=1).

technique: Turn a per-position value lower bound into a cap on where a given divisor can sit: if a divisor equals a known value, match it against d_j >= (monotone position bound) to force its index small.
how_used: The factor s_{i+1}+i of d_{i+1} is itself some divisor d_j; since s_{i+1}+i = s_{i+1}+s_i and d_j >= s_j+s_{j-1} with s increasing, value-matching forces j<=i+1, while size forces j>i, so j=i+1, yielding s_{i+1}^2 - s_{i+1} = i(i+1) hence s_{i+1}=i+1, d_{i+1}=2i+1 (inductively divisors are 1,3,5,...,n).

technique: Use that the second-largest divisor of n divides n, so it divides the small gap to n itself.
how_used: Having shown the divisors are exactly 1,3,5,...,n with largest = n and second-largest = n-2, n-2 | n=(n-2)+2 gives n-2 | 2, so n in {1,3}.

technique: To turn a 'product of any k-subset divides the product of its complement' condition into a per-prime lower bound on how many elements a prime divides, apply the condition to the k elements of largest valuation at that fixed prime.
how_used: With k=100: for a prime q, picking the 100 elements richest in q would otherwise out-weigh the complement, so at least 200 elements of S must be divisible by q (a prime p in S divides itself with one factor, forcing 199 further multiples); and when exactly 200 are divisible the q-valuations of all of them must be equal.

technique: Construct a set meeting the divisibility condition by padding each prime's support with extra multiples so no k-subset can monopolize that prime's total exponent.
how_used: Take 1819 primes p_i, their product P, and P*p_1,...,P*p_199; then each p_i divides exactly 201 elements (itself plus all multiples of P) with at most one carrying p_i^2, so any 100 chosen elements contain at most 101 factors p_i while at least 101 remain in the complement.

technique: Eliminate the boundary count by showing the extremal configuration forces several objects to be identical, contradicting distinctness.
how_used: If S had exactly 199 non-primes, each non-prime would have to contain every prime of S exactly once and no prime outside S, so all 199 equal the product of S's primes and cannot be distinct; hence at least 200 non-primes and at most 1819 primes.

technique: Run a Euclidean-algorithm step on a pair of linear forms in n, replacing the larger leading coefficient by its remainder while keeping the gcd value set unchanged, to set up a descent on that coefficient
how_used: With 0<=a<=c, write c=aq+r (0<=r<a) and use gcd(an+b,cn+d)=gcd(an+b,q(an+b)+rn+d-qb)=gcd(an+b,rn+(d-qb)); define the new tuple (a',b',c',d')=(r,d-qb,a,b), checked to preserve a'd'-b'c'=-(ad-bc)!=0 and gcd(a',b',c',d')=1, so induction on a reduces to the base case a=0 where the value set is exactly the divisors of b stripped of primes shared with c

technique: Eliminate n from two linear forms by a fixed integer combination to show their gcd divides the constant determinant, then substitute that constant for one of the forms inside the gcd
how_used: The identity a(cn+d)-c(an+b)=ad-bc gives gcd(an+b,cn+d) | ad-bc; localizing at a prime p with p not dividing a yields gcd_p(an+b,cn+d)=gcd_p(an+b,ad-bc), pinning the achievable p-parts to p-power divisors of ad-bc

technique: Realize an independently prescribed p-adic component at each prime simultaneously by choosing n congruent to the per-prime witness modulo a high power of that prime via CRT
