how_used: Given desired values gcd_p(a n_p+b,c n_p+d) for each p|ad-bc, pick one n with n=n_p mod a large power of p for all such p, so the single gcd(an+b,cn+d) equals the product of those independently chosen p-parts; combined with each p-part ranging over all p-power divisors of ad-bc, the full value set is all divisors of the largest factor of ad-bc coprime to gcd(a,c)

technique: Clear the denominator in a nested-iteration functional equation to express n^2 as a product of two specific iterate-values of the unknown function
how_used: The relation f^{f(n)}(n) = n^2 / f(f(n)) is rewritten as f^{f(n)}(n) · f^2(n) = n^2, turning the equation into a factorization of n^2 into two terms that are themselves values of iterates of f at n, setting up an extremal/factoring argument.

technique: Take the minimal element of a function's forward orbit so that any factors of a product equal to its square, being orbit terms no smaller than it, are each forced equal to it
how_used: For the smallest term m of the sequence n, f(n), f(f(n)), ..., both factors f^2(m) and f^{f(m)}(m) of m^2 = f^2(m)·f^{f(m)}(m) are iterates of m hence ≥ m, so each equals m; this gives f(f(m))=m, and (with injectivity to fold back any pre-periodic tail) every orbit is 2-periodic, so f∘f = identity everywhere.

technique: Reduce an iteration count to its value mod 2 once the function is known to be an involution, collapsing f^k(n) to n or f(n) by parity of k
how_used: With f∘f = id established, f^{f(n)}(n)=n must equal n, but f^{f(n)}(n) equals n when f(n) is even and f(n) when f(n) is odd; forcing the odd case to give f(n)=n shows f fixes all odd integers while acting as an involution on evens, so f(1000) can be any even integer.

technique: Find a residue class modulo a well-chosen prime that is closed under every allowed operation, making it a conserved invariant the target value escapes.
how_used: Pick an odd prime p dividing m+n; then q = m/n satisfies q ≡ 1/q ≡ -1 (mod p) since m ≡ -n. Because both numbers are units ≡ -1 mod p, the denominator x+y ≡ -2 is invertible, so the arithmetic mean (x+y)/2 ≡ -1 and the harmonic mean 2xy/(x+y) ≡ -1 both stay ≡ -1. Thus every board number is ≡ -1 (mod p) forever, but 1 ≢ -1, so an odd prime factor of m+n makes 1 unreachable — forcing m+n to be a power of 2.

technique: Realize a target value as a dyadic convex combination of two seed values via iterated arithmetic-mean bisection.
how_used: With m+n = 2^k, the identity (n/(m+n))·q + (m/(m+n))·(1/q) = 1 writes 1 as a convex combination of the seeds q and 1/q with power-of-two-denominator weights n/2^k and m/2^k. Since the arithmetic mean of two dyadic combinations of the seeds is again a dyadic combination with averaged weights, binary bisection reaches the weight n/2^k in k steps, producing 1 using the arithmetic mean alone (harmonic mean unneeded).

technique: Realize a system of multiplicative congruences with an unknown modulus by first solving the corresponding exact equations over the rationals, then reducing a chosen sufficiently large prime.
how_used: Instead of directly finding residues a_i mod p with a_i a_{i+1} a_{i+2} a_{i+3} ≡ i (mod p), first produce distinct positive rationals r_i = u_i/v_i (irreducible) with the exact identity r_i r_{i+1} r_{i+2} r_{i+3} = i for 1≤i≤k, then set a_i ≡ u_i v_i^{-1} (mod p); a prime p must avoid every v_i (so a_i is defined), every u_i (so a_i ≢ 0, i.e. a_i ∈ {1..p-1}), and every cross-difference v_j u_i − v_i u_j (so the a_i stay distinct), reducing the existence claim to a finite avoidance of bad primes.

technique: Divide two consecutive sliding-window product constraints to collapse the whole family into a single fixed-shift recurrence, with the window length as free seed parameters.
how_used: From r_i r_{i+1} r_{i+2} r_{i+3} = i and r_{i+1} r_{i+2} r_{i+3} r_{i+4} = i+1, taking the ratio gives r_{i+4} = ((i+1)/i) r_i; the first window is seeded by three distinct primes r_1=x, r_2=y, r_3=z greater than k and r_4 = 1/(r_1 r_2 r_3), and the step-4 recurrence then determines all remaining r_i as exact positive rationals satisfying every window constraint by telescoping.

technique: Prove all terms of a constructed sequence are distinct by attaching a distinct prime tag to each residue class through the prime that divides each term's numerator.
how_used: In the step-4 recurrence the numerator of r_i (as an irreducible fraction) is divisible by x when i≡1, by y when i≡2, by z when i≡3, and by none of x,y,z when i≡0 (mod 4) since the seed primes exceed all small integer factors; this separates the four classes, and within each class the relation r_i < r_{i+4} makes the subsequence strictly increasing, so no two r_i coincide and the cross-differences r_i − r_j are all nonzero.

technique: Orient each edge along a deterministic successor map so every vertex has out-degree at most 1, bounding the total edge count by the number of vertices that emit an arrow.
how_used: A bridge joins islands m,n iff n≡m²+1 or m≡n²+1 (mod p). Orient the bridge from m to n exactly when n≡m²+1, i.e. along the map f(m)=m²+1 mod p. Then at most one arrow leaves each island, so the number of bridges is at most the number of arrows ≤ p.

technique: Count the out-degree-zero vertices as roots of the orienting map's fixed-point equation, pairing them via a root involution to guarantee at least two, dropping the edge total below |V|−1 (whence disconnection follows routinely).
how_used: An island m emits no arrow precisely when f(m)=m, i.e. m²+1≡m, so m is a root of x²−x+1≡0 (mod p). When p divides some x²−x+1 there are two distinct such roots x and p+1−x (via the involution x↦1−x, since (1−x)²−(1−x)+1=x²−x+1, and they coincide only at x=(p+1)/2 where 4(x²−x+1)=p²+3 is not divisible by p). Hence bridges ≤ p−2 < p−1, and a p-vertex graph with fewer than p−1 edges is disconnected.

technique: Euclid-style construction for primes dividing polynomial values: evaluate the polynomial at the product of the known qualifying primes to get a value coprime to all of them, forcing a new prime divisor of the same form.
how_used: Given primes p₁,…,p_k each dividing some x²−x+1, the number (p₁···p_k)²−(p₁···p_k)+1 exceeds 1 and is ≡1 modulo every p_i, so it is divisible by none of them and contributes a fresh prime dividing a value of x²−x+1, giving infinitely many such primes.

technique: Recognize the difference of two equal-length prefix sums of an additive function as the value of that function on a binomial coefficient.
how_used: Since f is additive, sum_{j=1}^k f(n-j) = f((n-1)(n-2)...(n-k)) and sum_{j=1}^k f(j) = f(k!), so their difference is f of the quotient (n-1)!/[k!(n-1-k)!] = f(C(n-1,k)). The symmetry condition f(k)=f(n-k) makes the two sums equal, forcing f(C(n-1,k))=0 for all k; because f is additive and non-negative, this means the prime p with f(p)>0 divides no entry C(n-1,k) of row n-1 of Pascal's triangle, for every good n.

technique: Apply Lucas' theorem to convert 'an entire row of Pascal's triangle is coprime to p' into a base-p digit constraint on the row index.
how_used: From p not dividing any C(n-1,k) (k=1,...,n-2), Lucas' theorem (equivalently Kummer's carry criterion) forces n-1 to have all base-p digits maximal below its top digit, i.e. n = a*p^s with a0 would likewise force n = b*q^t with b

technique: To inflate one multiplicative function while holding another fixed, multiply n by extra primes p whose p-1 is smooth over the prime set already present in n: each such p multiplies d(n) but introduces no new prime into phi(n).
how_used: Adjoin to n all primes p in (N,2N], each to the first power. Their p-1 factors only over primes <= N (since p-1 = 2t with t < N), so phi(n) stays supported on those small primes (the prime support of d(phi(n)) is unaffected), while each new p contributes an extra factor 2 to d(n), producing the 2^s boost that becomes 2^{s-1} in phi(d(n)).

technique: Give every base prime of n the common exponent q-1 with q a huge prime, so the same large prime power q^k appears as the leading factor of both d(n) and (through it) d(phi(n)), forcing the q-dependence to cancel in the target ratio and leaving a fixed residual.
how_used: With all primes <= N raised to q-1, d(n)=q^k*2^s gives phi(d(n))=q^{k-1}(q-1)2^{s-1}, while phi(n)=prod p_i^{q-2+c_i} gives d(phi(n))=prod(q-1+c_i); the ratio = 2^{s-1}*(q-1)/q*prod q/(q-1+c_i) -> 2^{s-1} as q->infinity.

technique: Establish that the count of primes in (N,2N] is unbounded by arguing that a uniform cap of C primes per dyadic block [2^l,2^{l+1}) would make sum 1/p converge, contradicting its divergence.
how_used: Need s = #primes in (N,2N) arbitrarily large; if every block held <= C primes, sum_p 1/p <= sum_l C/2^l < infinity, contradicting the known divergence of the reciprocal-prime sum.

technique: Strengthen the inductive invariant to a symmetric sum-AND-difference divisibility ban, so the step's failure produces exactly the forbidden term.
how_used: Instead of only forbidding a_i | a_{i-1}+a_{i+1}, prove the stronger 'nice' property that a_i divides neither a_{i-1}+a_{i+1} nor a_{i-1}-a_{i+1}; the extra difference-clause is precisely what the final contradiction (b_k | b_{k-1} - ε1ε2 b_{k+1}) needs to contradict niceness of T.

technique: Pigeonhole the insertion slots against the elements that can block them: more gaps than possible blockers forces one element to block two adjacent gaps.
how_used: Inserting a=max S into the (n-1)-term nice ordering of T gives n candidate positions N_j; each failing N_j is blamed on a neighbor b_{j-1} or b_j (never on a, by Observation A). With n slots but only n-1 elements of T, some b_k is assigned to two indices, which must be k and k+1.

technique: Eliminate the freshly inserted element between two divisibility relations sharing the same modulus to recover a relation among the old elements.
how_used: From b_k | b_{k-1}+ε1 a and b_k | a+ε2 b_{k+1}, reduce mod b_k to get b_{k-1} ≡ -ε1 a ≡ ε1ε2 b_{k+1}, so b_k | b_{k-1} - ε1ε2 b_{k+1} — a sum-or-difference divisibility entirely within T, contradicting its niceness.

technique: When an operation can only multiply by 2, treat the power-of-two part of a denominator as free and reduce 'become an integer' to killing the odd primes of the denominator one at a time.
how_used: Since x_n = 2x_{n-1} (+ optional 1/n), extra doublings clear any power of two in the denominator; so it suffices to show that at any time t one can strip a single factor of an odd prime p from the odd part q=p^e of the denominator of x_t while introducing only new powers of two, then iterate over all odd prime factors and finally double to an integer.

technique: Schedule the optional additive operations only at indices that are powers of two times the current denominator, so each surviving contribution becomes a power-of-two numerator over that fixed denominator (hence a unit modulo the target odd prime).
how_used: With q=p^e the odd part of the denominator fixed, take the '+1/n' option only at indices n=2^{r+i}q (r large so all exceed t); running forward to N the add at 2^{r+i}q becomes c_i/q with c_i=2^{N-2^{r+i}q}/2^{r+1} a power of 2, a unit mod p, giving x_N = 2^{N-t}x_t + sum_i c_i ε_i/q with independent ε_i in {0,1} and no new odd prime introduced.

technique: Show that a subset-sum set built from nonzero residues covers all of Z/p by lower-bounding the iterated sumset with Cauchy-Davenport (or a pigeonhole count).
how_used: The free binary ε_i make the achievable numerator adjustment the sumset {0,c_1}+...+{0,c_m} mod p with each c_i a unit; Cauchy-Davenport gives |sumset| >= min(p, m+1), so m=p-1 forces it to equal Z/p (pigeonhole with m=O(p^2) also works), letting one choose ε_i with 2^{N-t}A + sum ε_i c_i ≡ 0 mod p, dropping v_p of the denominator by one.

technique: Parametrize a half-integer sequence term by the 2-adic decomposition of its integer part, writing it as (power-of-two)·(odd) plus the fixed fractional remainder, so that the appearance of an integer is reduced to the power-of-two exponent reaching zero
how_used: For odd m=2^p·n_0+1, write each term as a_k = 2^p·n_k + 3/2 with n_k odd; since a_{k+1}=a_k⌊a_k⌋ and a_k is a half-integer, the product is an integer exactly when ⌊a_k⌋ is even, i.e. when the exponent p has dropped to 0

technique: Use the exponent in the 2-adic decomposition as a strict monovariant, computing the recurrence's product directly to show the exponent drops by exactly one each step while the cofactor stays odd, forcing termination
how_used: Expanding a_{k+1}=(2^p n_k+3/2)(2^p n_k+1)=2^{p-1}(2^{p+1}n_k^2+5n_k)+3/2 with the bracket odd shows p decreases by 1 per iteration; after p steps the exponent is 0, the floor is even, and the next term is an integer

technique: Split the middle term of a three-term partial-fraction triple by adding and subtracting a copy of it, so the surviving terms close up into a complete consecutive-integer harmonic block minus a dilated every-third-term harmonic block.
how_used: Partial fractions give 2/((3k-1)(3k)(3k+1)) = 1/(3k-1) - 2/(3k) + 1/(3k+1); rewriting -2/(3k) = +1/(3k) - 3/(3k), the +1/(3k) fills the multiples-of-3 gaps so the running terms become the full block 1/2+1/3+...+1/(q+2), while -3*sum 1/(3k) = -(1+1/2+...+1/((q+1)/3)), turning 2S_q into a difference of two harmonic sums.

technique: Translate a harmonic block whose denominators exceed p down to start at 1 using the inverse-periodicity 1/(p+i) congruent to 1/i (mod p).
how_used: The tail block 1/(p+1)+1/(p+2)+...+1/(q+2) is reduced mod p to 1+1/2+...+1/(q-p+2), re-indexing it to begin at 1 so it can be matched against the subtracted dilated block.

technique: Exploit a problem-specific index coincidence to make two harmonic blocks identical so they cancel, leaving only the canonical H_{p-1}.
how_used: From q=(3p-5)/2 one derives q-p+2 = (q+1)/3, so the translated tail block 1+...+1/(q-p+2) equals the subtracted dilated block 1+...+1/((q+1)/3) and they cancel; the remainder is H_{p-1}=1+1/2+...+1/(p-1), which is congruent to 0 mod p by pairing i with p-i, yielding 2S_q - 1/p + 1 congruent to 0, i.e. 1/p - 2S_q congruent to 1, so p divides m-n.

technique: Translate "prime p divides 2^n - 1" into a divisibility condition on n by computing the multiplicative order of 2 modulo p
how_used: Computing ord_3(2)=2, ord_5(2)=4, ord_7(2)=3 turns the constraint into: 3 | 2^n-1 iff 2|n, 5 | 2^n-1 iff 4|n, 7 | 2^n-1 iff 3|n. This lets the solver restrict to which of 3,5,7 can occur for a given parity/divisibility of n, e.g. odd n forces 2^n-1 to avoid factors 3 and 5, partitioning the problem into a finite menu of pure/mixed prime-power equations indexed by n mod 12.

technique: Rule out a 2^n - 1 = (prime power) equation by reducing modulo a power of 2 where 2^n-1 sits in a forced residue but the prime power can only land in a restricted residue set
how_used: For odd n divisible by 3 the only chance is 2^n-1 = 7^c; for n>=4 this needs 7^c == -1 mod 16, but 7^2 = 49 == 1 mod 16 so 7^c is only ever 1 or 7 mod 16, a contradiction, leaving n=3.

technique: When a single prime power equals a product of two near-equal factors with small gcd, factor and force one factor to be at most the gcd to kill all large solutions
how_used: In each even case the equation collapses to a prime power equal to (x-1)(x+1) (e.g. 4^k = 5^{4d'}-1 = (25^{d'}-1)(25^{d'}+1), or 8^k = (3^{a'}-1)(3^{a'}+1)) where gcd of the two factors is 2; since the whole product is a power of a single prime, one factor must be <= 2, forcing the exponent to its minimal value and pinning n=2,4,6.

technique: To prove a digit subsequence sampled at moving indices is eventually periodic, track the sampling index modulo the period of the source's eventually-periodic digit string.
how_used: x rational has eventually period-d digits; the nth digit of y is the (2^n)th digit of x, so y's nth digit depends only on 2^n mod d. Showing the residue sequence 2^n mod d is eventually periodic with some period w forces y's digits to repeat with period w, hence y rational.

technique: To control 2^n modulo a composite m, factor out the 2-adic part m = 2^u·v (v odd): the power-of-two part has 2^n ≡ 0 (mod 2^u) once n ≥ u, and the odd part is handled by the multiplicative order of 2 mod v.
how_used: With d = 2^u·v, choose w (e.g. w = φ(v)) so 2^w ≡ 1 (mod v), giving 2^{n+w} ≡ 2^n (mod v); meanwhile 2^{n+w} ≡ 2^n ≡ 0 (mod 2^u) for n ≥ u. Combining the two coprime moduli yields 2^{n+w} ≡ 2^n (mod d) for all n ≥ u, the eventual periodicity needed.

technique: When integer iterates close into a cycle, use that each consecutive difference divides the next all the way around the loop, so a cyclic chain of divisibilities forces every difference to share one absolute value.
how_used: From u-v | P(u)-P(v), the orbit differences x_0-x_1, x_1-x_2, ..., x_{k-1}-x_k each divide the next, and since x_k-x_{k+1}=x_0-x_1 the chain closes, so all |x_i-x_{i+1}| are equal; taking the minimum x_m of the orbit forces x_{m-1}=x_{m+1}, making the orbit alternate between two values, i.e. every integer fixed point of Q is a fixed point of P(P(x)).

technique: Apply the difference-divisibility u-v | P(u)-P(v) to cross-pairs drawn from two DISTINCT 2-orbits, getting mutual divisibilities that collapse (after ruling out the all-plus case by subtraction) into a single linear relation between the orbits.
how_used: With one 2-orbit {a,b=P(a)} fixed and any other fixed point alpha of P∘P with beta=P(alpha), the four pairs (alpha,a),(beta,b),(alpha,b),(beta,a) give alpha-b=±(beta-a) and alpha-a=±(beta-b); both-plus yields a-b=b-a (contradiction since a≠b), so a minus sign forces alpha+beta=a+b.

technique: Bound the number of integer solutions by exhibiting that they all satisfy one fixed polynomial relation, then invoke that a degree-n polynomial has at most n roots.
how_used: Setting C=a+b, every integer fixed point alpha satisfies C-alpha-P(alpha)=0, so all of them are roots of F(x)=C-x-P(x); since deg P=n>1 forces deg F=n, there are at most n such fixed points.

technique: When a prime p satisfies both x^a ≡ 1 and x^{p-1} ≡ 1 with gcd(a, p-1)=1, use a Bézout combination of the two exponents to force x ≡ 1 (mod p)
how_used: For a prime p | (x^7-1)/(x-1), p divides x^7-1, and Fermat gives x^{p-1}≡1. Assuming 7∤(p-1) makes gcd(7,p-1)=1, so 7k+(p-1)m=1 yields x ≡ (x^7)^k(x^{p-1})^m ≡ 1 (mod p); then the cyclotomic value 1+x+...+x^6 ≡ 7 (mod p), forcing p=7. Hence every prime divisor is ≡1 mod 7 or equals 7, so every divisor d of N is ≡0 or 1 (mod 7).

technique: Push a derived divisor-residue restriction onto a polynomial factor of the right-hand side and check its forced residue for a contradiction
how_used: Since N = y^5-1 and both y-1 and 1+y+y^2+y^3+y^4 are positive divisors of N, each must be ≡0 or 1 (mod 7), forcing y≡1 or y≡2 (mod 7). Evaluating 1+y+y^2+y^3+y^4 gives 5 (mod 7) when y≡1 and 3 (mod 7) when y≡2 — neither is 0 or 1, contradicting that this factor is also a divisor of N. So no solutions exist.

technique: Parametrize every solution of a linear Diophantine equation by adding the kernel vector, turning a minimum-norm quantity into a minimization over a single integer shift.
how_used: All representations of c are written c = a(x-kb) + b(y+ka), so w(c) = min over integer k of |x-kb| + |y+ka|; this reframing of the weight as a one-parameter integer minimization is what makes the local-champion inequalities w(c) >= w(c +/- a), w(c +/- b) comparable across shifts.

technique: Pin the minimizer of a convex, integer-valued lattice function to the adjacent step: interpolate (Jensen) from a known distant value that is <= 0 down to the neighbor to get a strict <1 bound, which integrality rounds to <= 0.
how_used: With f(t)=|x+1-bt|+|y-at|-(x+y), known f(0)=1 and f(k)<=0 for the true optimal shift k>0, convexity gives f(1) <= (1-1/k)f(0)+(1/k)f(k) < 1; since f(1) is an integer, f(1)<=0, forcing the competing representation to use k=1 and yielding |x|<b, |y|<a and x+y = floor((a+b)/2).

technique: Decide whether two sign-symmetric arithmetic progressions coincide or are disjoint by comparing their residues modulo the common difference, splitting on the parity of the parameters.
how_used: Champions form C+ and C- = +/-(ax-by), each an AP of length b-1 with difference a+b; for a,b both odd the symmetry x<->b-x, y<->a-y makes C+=C- giving b-1 champions, while for opposite parities computing 2c1 = -2c2 = -a (mod a+b) and a+b odd & coprime to a shows C+, C- lie in distinct residue classes, hence disjoint, giving 2(b-1).

technique: Strengthen a bare existence/surjectivity claim mod n into a uniform induction hypothesis that hits EVERY residue mod n and can place its witnesses beyond any prescribed bound, so the hypothesis produces exactly the raw material the inductive step consumes.
how_used: Instead of proving only that some m has n | 2^m + m, prove by induction on d that for every bound N there exist b_0,...,b_{d-1} > N with 2^{b_i}+b_i ≡ i (mod d) for all i; taking m=b_0 (the i=0 witness) recovers the original claim, and the 'every residue' plus '>N' freedom is exactly what the inductive step needs to feed a smaller modulus.

technique: Bound the eventual period of a base's powers mod a strictly below a by noting its residue-cycle cannot contain all a residues (0 is either absent or isolated in the cycle), yielding a strictly smaller modulus via gcd for the descent.
how_used: Let k be the period of 2^i mod a (repeating from exponent M on); since the cycle of powers of 2 omits 0 or contains it alone, k<a, hence d=gcd(a,k) satisfies 0<d<a, so the induction hypothesis applies to the smaller modulus d to supply the base witnesses b_i with 2^{b_i}+b_i ≡ i (mod d).

technique: Increment the exponent by the period so the power term stays fixed mod a while the linear term sweeps an arithmetic progression, decoupling the two summands so that combining several such progressions realizes a complete residue system.
how_used: For each i form 2^{b_i+mk}+(b_i+mk), m=0..a'-1 (a'=a/d); since 2^{b_i+mk}≡2^{b_i} (mod a), these equal 2^{b_i}+(b_i+mk), so the additive part advances by k each step; the d progressions give a'd=a values, proven pairwise distinct mod a by reducing mod d (forcing i=j since d|k) then cancelling k via k'=k/d coprime to a' (forcing m=n), so they realize all residues mod a.

technique: Substitute n = 10^k - 1 so the input digit-sum S(n) = 9k scales linearly in the parameter k while the polynomial output P(n) keeps only O(k) decimal digits.
how_used: For deg P = d >= 2, plugging n = 10^k - 1 into S(P(n)) = P(S(n)) gives S(P(n)) = P(9k); since P(n) ~ n^d has O(dk) digits its digit sum S(P(n)) is O(k), but P(9k) grows like k^d, impossible for large k when d >= 2, eliminating all degrees >= 2.

technique: Apply digit-sum subadditivity S(m+n) <= S(m) + S(n) to the increment between two chosen evaluation points to bound a leading coefficient.
how_used: From S(an+b) = aS(n)+b, evaluating at n=2025 and n=2020 gives S(2025a+b) - S(2020a+b) = 5a; writing 2025a+b = (2020a+b)+5a and applying subadditivity yields 5a <= S(5a), which with a >= 1 forces a = 1.

technique: Choose the variable so that adding the constant offset produces an all-9's number, triggering a maximal carry cascade that makes the digit-sum drop by a large multiple of the digit count.
how_used: After reducing to S(n+b) = S(n)+b, pick n with n+1+b = 10^k so n+b is all 9's; then S(n+1+b)-S(n+b) = 1-9k, while the equality forces it to equal S(n+1)-S(n) >= 2-9k (as S(n) < 9k), a contradiction killing b > 0 (and symmetrically b < 0), leaving P(x) = x.

technique: Classify prime factors by their residue mod p, then convert a target residue condition on a divisor into a parity condition on how many of the (-1)-residue primes it uses.
how_used: A divisor of m is ≡1 mod 3 exactly when it contains no factor of 3, uses primes ≡1 mod 3 freely (they contribute 1), and uses an EVEN total number (with multiplicity) of primes ≡2 mod 3 (each contributing -1≡2). This isolates the q_j-primes (≡2 mod 3) as the only constrained part, reducing τ₁ to counting exponent tuples on the q's with even total exponent.

technique: Count exponent tuples whose total is even by splitting each factor's exponent range into even-count ⌈(b+1)/2⌉ vs odd-count ⌊(b+1)/2⌋ and convolving: if any single range has even size the split is exactly half, otherwise track even/odd subtotals inductively.
how_used: Number of q-exponent choices with even total exponent equals ⌈½∏(b_j+1)⌉, so τ₁(m)=∏(a_i+1)·⌈½∏(b_j+1)⌉; for 10n this becomes ∏(a_i+1)·⌈½(y+2)(z+2)∏(b_j+1)⌉, the ceiling being what distinguishes the c-even and c-odd cases.

technique: When a quantity equals 2(x+1)c/(c+1), use gcd(c,c+1)=1 to shift the integrality requirement onto the complementary factor c+1, forcing (c+1)∣2(x+1) and rewriting the value as k·c.
how_used: With c=(y+2)(z+2)∏(b_j+1) odd, the ratio is 2(x+1)c/(c+1); coprimality of c and c+1 forces c+1∣2(x+1), so writing 2(x+1)=k(c+1) gives ratio=kc=k(y+2)(z+2)∏(b_j+1). Since y,z odd ⇒ y+2,z+2≥3, every such value is composite; combined with ratio=2(x+1) (any even integer) when c is even, and the construction n=3^{(ab-1)/2}2^{a-2}5^{b-2} realizing each odd composite ab, the value set is exactly 2 plus all composites.

technique: Add the divisor itself to the dividend to absorb the lone +1/-1 constant and expose a multiple that factors out a power of the base
how_used: From n^k+mn^l+1 | n^{k+l}-1, add the divisor to the dividend so n^k+mn^l+1 | (n^{k+l}-1)+(n^k+mn^l+1)=n^{k+l}+n^k+mn^l, a positive multiple every term of which carries a positive power of n

technique: Cancel a coprime power of the base out of a divisibility because gcd(divisor, base)=1
how_used: Since (n^k+mn^l+1, n)=1, the divisor must divide n^{k+l}+n^k+mn^l with its common factor n^{min} stripped: dividing by n^k (case l>=k) gives n^k+mn^l+1 | n^l+mn^{l-k}+1, and dividing by n^l (case l<k) gives n^k+mn^l+1 | n^k+n^{k-l}+m

technique: Pin a divisibility to an equality by sandwiching the (positive) dividend strictly between the divisor and twice it
how_used: The reduced target n^l+mn^{l-k}+1 (resp. n^k+n^{k-l}+m) is positive and less than 2(n^k+mn^l+1), so it must equal the divisor; equating then forces m=1, l=2k in the first case and m=(n^{k-l}-1)/(n^l-1) with l|k in the second (l|k via the standard n^a-1 | n^b-1 iff a|b)

technique: With the parameter fixed, view the defining relation as a monic integer quadratic in one symmetric coordinate and use Vieta to read off the conjugate root as a second integer solution
how_used: Substituting u=x+y, v=x-y turns k=(x^2-a)/(x^2-y^2) into u^2-(4k-2)uv+(v^2-4a)=0; treating this as a quadratic in u with v,k fixed, one root known, Vieta gives the other root as an integer, yielding a new solution (x_1,y_1) of (1) -- equivalently x_1=-x+2k(x-y) is manifestly integral, with x_1+y_1=x-y preserved

technique: Bound the size of the Vieta-conjugate solution to show a single jump lands on the opposite side of a fixed threshold, then run the jump as a monovariant down to the minimal representative
how_used: For B subset A the conjugate satisfies x_1 >= 2a/(x+y) > sqrt(a) (since x,y<sqrt(a) gives x+y<2sqrt(a)), so a solution with x<sqrt(a) jumps to one with x>sqrt(a); for A subset B, choosing the solution of smallest x+y, the estimates y_1>4a/(x+y)>0 and 4a/(x+y)<x+y force x_1+y_1<x+y, so by minimality x_1 cannot exceed sqrt(a), and x_1=sqrt(a) is impossible as a is a nonsquare, giving 0<=x_1<sqrt(a)

technique: From a divisibility d | A, subtract a chosen integer multiple of d from the dividend A to replace it by a much smaller, controllable expression that d still divides.
how_used: Using f(m)+f(n)-mn | mf(m)+nf(n): with n=1 (and f(1)=1 from m=n=1) subtract p times the divisor f(p)-p+1 from pf(p)+1 to get f(p)-p+1 | p^2-p+1; with m=n=p subtract p times 2f(p)-p^2 from 2pf(p) to get 2f(p)-p^2 | p^3. These reduce the messy dividend to a fixed/prime-power target.

technique: Combine an explicit size bound on a divisor of a prime power with the fact that all its divisors are powers of the prime, then show the divisor is trapped in an open interval that contains no such power to force a contradiction.
how_used: The n=1 relation forces either f(p)=p^2 or (since p^2-p+1 is odd) p^2-p+1 >= 3(f(p)-p+1), giving f(p) <= (p^2+2p-2)/3. Plugging this into 2f(p)-p^2 | p^3 puts 2f(p)-p^2 strictly in (-p^2,-p) for p>=7, an interval containing no divisor (1,p,p^2,p^3) of p^3, so the alternative is impossible and f(p)=p^2.

technique: Specialize one variable to an arbitrarily large prime so a target divisor grows without bound while the remaining congruence difference is a fixed quantity; a fixed integer divisible by an unbounded modulus must be zero.
how_used: Setting m=p (large prime) with f(p)=p^2 and stripping the coprime factor p (p does not divide f(n) for large p) yields p^2-pn+f(n) | p^2-pn+n^2, hence p^2-pn+f(n) | n^2-f(n). Since n^2-f(n) is fixed but the modulus can be made arbitrarily large, n^2-f(n)=0, so f(n)=n^2 for all n.

technique: Reduce a cyclic-polygon divisibility problem to smaller cyclic polygons by cutting along a diagonal that still satisfies the hypothesis, isolating the hard case where no diagonal qualifies
how_used: After the k=3 Heron base case, for k>=4: if some diagonal has squared length divisible by n it splits P into two smaller cyclic polygons whose sides and that diagonal all have squares divisible by n, so induction on k applies; this lets the solver assume no diagonal's squared length is divisible by n=p^t, i.e. nu_p(diagonal^2) < t while nu_p(side^2) >= t

technique: Square Ptolemy's chord relation for a cyclic quadrilateral to clear the radical cross-term and obtain a polynomial identity among only the squared chord lengths
how_used: On cyclic quadrilateral A1 A_{m-1} A_m A_{m+1}, Ptolemy gives A1A_{m+1}*A_{m-1}A_m + A1A_{m-1}*A_mA_{m+1} = A1A_m*A_{m-1}A_{m+1}; isolating and squaring yields A1A_{m+1}^2*A_{m-1}A_m^2 = A1A_{m-1}^2*A_mA_{m+1}^2 + A1A_m^2*A_{m-1}A_{m+1}^2 - 2*A1A_{m-1}*A_mA_{m+1}*A1A_m*A_{m-1}A_{m+1}, an integer relation among squared distances from A1 to consecutive vertices

technique: In an additive identity among products, exploit that two terms of unequal p-adic valuation cannot cancel so a third term inherits the minimum valuation, propagating a strict valuation inequality to the next index until a bounded strictly-decreasing valuation chain gives a contradiction
how_used: In the squared-Ptolemy identity nu_p(A1A_{m-1}^2*A_mA_{m+1}^2) > nu_p(A1A_m^2*A_{m-1}A_{m+1}^2) and the 2-sqrt cross term has even larger valuation, forcing nu_p(A1A_{m+1}^2) < nu_p(A1A_m^2); this builds p^t > nu_p(A1A_3^2) > ... > nu_p(A1A_k^2), yet the side A1A_k has nu_p >= p^t, contradiction

technique: Pick a prime in an arithmetic progression so that gcd(p-1, d) = 1, making the d-th-power map a bijection mod p and upgrading a^d ≡ b^d to a ≡ b.
how_used: By Dirichlet's theorem (d odd) choose a large prime p ≡ 2 (mod d), so (p-1, d) = 1. After pigeonholing d+1 of the y_i into one residue class mod p, in the case p∤cQ(y_1) the congruence cQ(y_1) ≡ cQ(y_i) gives l^d ≡ m^d (mod p); injectivity of x↦x^d then forces l ≡ m (mod p), hence p | (m·y_i − l·y_1).

technique: To force two near-equal d-th powers to be literally equal, factor a^d − b^d = (a−b)·(cofactor) and show that a single gained prime factor (or a smallness bound on a side) makes the leading cofactor term dominate the bounded lower-degree remainder, so the linear factor a−b must vanish.
how_used: Writing Q(y_i)/Q(y_1) = l^d/m^d and expanding gives b_d(m^d y_i^d − l^d y_1^d) = −Σ_{j≤d-2} b_j(m^d y_i^j − l^d y_1^j). Factor the LHS as b_d(m·y_i − l·y_1)(Σ m^{d-1-k}y_i^{d-1-k} l^k y_1^k). In Case 1 (p | cQ(y_1)) cancellation forces m ≤ p^{-1/d}(2cB)^{1/d}y_1, shrinking the RHS bound; in Case 2 the factor p | (m·y_i − l·y_1) multiplies the LHS by p. Either way, against the bound (RHS ≤ 21(d−1)B(2cB)^{1/d}m^{d-1}y_i^{d-1}), large p makes m·y_i − l·y_1 ≠ 0 impossible, giving Q(y_i)/Q(y_1) = y_i^d/y_1^d.

technique: Convert pointwise value-ratios into a polynomial identity by exhibiting a polynomial of degree ≤ d that vanishes at d+1 distinct points, hence is identically zero.
how_used: From the Claim Q(y_i)/Q(y_1) = y_i^d/y_1^d, the polynomial Q(y_1)·y^d − y_1^d·Q(y) (degree ≤ d) vanishes at the d+1 distinct values y_1,...,y_{d+1}, so it is the zero polynomial, forcing Q(y) = b_d y^d. Reversing the Tschirnhaus substitution y = d·a_d·x + a_{d-1} and clearing denominators yields P(x) = a(rx+s)^d.

technique: Subtract two simultaneous perfect-square equations so the unknown squares combine into a single difference of squares equal to a known factored quantity.
how_used: From a^2=p-q and b^2=pq-q, subtracting gives b^2-a^2=p(q-1), i.e. (b-a)(b+a)=p(q-1), turning the prime p into a factor of a difference of squares.

technique: Bound each factor of a difference of squares strictly below the prime's range so the prime is forced to equal the sum-factor of the factorization.

technique: Exploit that a sum and a difference of the same two integers always share parity to pin down a variable.
how_used: b+a=p and b-a=q-1 have equal parity, so p and q-1 share parity; with p an odd prime this forces q-1 odd, i.e. q=2 (then mod 3 gives p=3).
